section_text: This player never uses rewards, even when others contribute more than they do and when prosocial reward behavior is clearly present in the group. They do not invest to encourage high contributors, nor do they attempt to strategically reinforce allies or cultivate reciprocity. Their stance appears efficiency- and cost-focused at the individual level: they avoid spending coins on mechanisms that transfer payoff away from themselves, even when such transfers could plausibly improve future cooperation. They also show no sign of imitating others’ rewarding behavior. The absence of rewards, combined with steady moderate contributions, fits a “self-contained cooperator” profile: willing to contribute reliably to the public good but not willing to pay extra to manage others’ behavior through positive incentives.

The player is clearly willing to reward and does so in a pattern consistent with “pro‑social reinforcement.” They direct rewards to those who contribute at or near full levels and who appear to sustain group welfare. They do not reward moderate or low contributors, even when those individuals are not being punished, indicating that their standard for positive reinforcement is reserved for the very cooperative. Rewarding is intermittent: they do not reward every time someone contributes highly, nor do they reward all high contributors in a given round. This suggests they treat rewards as a targeted, somewhat scarce signal rather than an automatic rule. They may be using reward both to express appreciation and to encourage sustained high contributions from particular players who have been especially consistent. There is no evidence they use rewards strategically to manipulate more selfish players into contributing more; instead, they focus on already‑good cooperators. That is more consistent with helping “heroes” than recruiting “defectors.” Given the positive payoff cost of rewarding, they accept a reduction in their own earnings to support and recognize high cooperators, but they do this sporadically rather than continuously, perhaps balancing altruistic signaling with payoff concerns.

They never spend on rewards, even though they routinely observe others investing in positive incentives. This includes frequent cases where other players reward high contributors. Their abstention is consistent across both cooperative and more defect‑prone environments, suggesting that they treat rewarding, like punishment, as outside their strategy set. This likely reflects either a strong preference to avoid spending on social tools altogether, a belief that cooperation can be sustained without explicit reinforcement, or a narrow focus on maximizing personal payoff subject to maintaining their own cooperative standard. They do not engage in alliance‑building via rewards nor do they attempt to shape others’ behavior with positive incentives.

They are willing but selective in using the reward mechanism. They rarely spend on rewards overall, and when they do, they spend modest amounts rather than systematically reinforcing all cooperators. Their use of rewards coincides with periods where others are contributing relatively more, suggesting that their reward behavior is conditional on observed cooperation level. They do not use rewards to compensate themselves (since rewarding others is payoff-neutral by design, and they still choose to do it at times), implying that when they pay to reward, they are expressing approval or trying to support cooperative behavior rather than pursuing direct material gain. They show no clear pattern of rewarding in proportion to others’ exact contributions, and they do not reward every time cooperation improves, so their reinforcement is intermittent and somewhat cautious. They refrain from rewarding during some full-cooperation episodes, which may reflect uncertainty about the impact of rewards, a desire to conserve payoff, or a belief that verbal and collective norms are sufficient once cooperation is established. They never use rewards punitively or manipulatively (the mechanism is symmetric cost/benefit), and their pattern suggests a primarily prosocial, norm-affirming intent: they are willing to incur small costs to encourage or acknowledge cooperative behavior but do not treat rewarding as a central or heavily strategic tool.

The player uses rewards sparingly and selectively. They are willing to spend on rewards but do so infrequently, suggesting that they reserve rewarding for specific situations rather than routine reinforcement. Their first use of rewards occurs in a context where cooperation is already very high, indicating that they may use rewards more as a signal of appreciation or encouragement than as a tool to change poor behavior. They do not systematically reward every instance of strong cooperation, nor do they appear to run a consistent “reward-the-highest-contributor” strategy. They receive rewards in several cooperative contexts, which may reinforce their sense that contributing relatively high amounts is socially approved, but their own rewarding does not clearly escalate in response. Overall, they show a moderate willingness to reward, with no evidence of targeted strategic reinforcement or explicit gratitude signaling beyond the bare act of rewarding itself. Their reward behavior suggests they tentatively support prosocial norms but prioritize conserving their own payoff over actively incentivizing others.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, even when others’ contributions are high or align with the norm they promote. They do receive rewards from others but do not reciprocate. This indicates that, in this environment, they treat the reward mechanism as personally costly without sufficient perceived benefit, and they prioritize keeping their own payoff over reinforcing others’ cooperation through formal rewards. Their reinforcement of norms thus occurs exclusively via chat and contributions, not via the explicit reward channel. They use verbal encouragement and social feedback rather than material incentives. This suggests they either see rewards as strategically unnecessary, do not find them intuitive or salient, or simply decide that the one-to-one cost-benefit of the reward option does not justify use. There is no evidence that they use reward to signal gratitude or to cement alliances; any appreciation for others’ high contributions is expressed only in words (e.g., affirming when others “get it right”) and in occasionally matching full contributions. Their willingness to invest in cooperation is selective and occurs through their own contribution choices, not through paying extra to boost others. Their reward behavior is therefore best characterized as non-rewarding and chat-based norm-enforcing, with no observed strategic use of the formal reward mechanism.

They never spend any resources to reward others, despite repeatedly encountering high contributors and even receiving rewards themselves in some rounds. Their baseline choice is to refrain from using the reward mechanism entirely. This is not because they are being ignored by others; they do get rewarded in several instances, but they do not reciprocate or adopt a rewarding strategy afterward. Given this, their cooperative stance is not complemented by active incentive-setting or gratitude signaling. They seem to separate contribution from reward behavior: they contribute heavily but do not invest in reinforcing others’ contributions, even when rewarding would plausibly support high-cooperation episodes or reciprocate kindness. The absence of any reward use suggests either that they see reward as an unnecessary or unprofitable expenditure, or that they are focused on their own contribution rule and pay relatively little attention to the additional strategic lever that rewards provide. Their profile here is “non-rewarding unconditional cooperator”: generous in the public good, but passive with respect to positive sanctioning.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite rarely, if ever, being in a position where others’ behavior would not qualify as rewardable under a cooperative norm. They receive rewards in some rounds, which signals that at least one other group member is willing to pay to encourage or recognize contributions, but they do not reciprocate this behavior toward anyone. Their revealed stance is that paying 1 coin to give 1 coin provides no strategic or emotional benefit worth the cost. They do not use rewards either to reinforce high contributors or to try to shape future group behavior. This is consistent with a strictly payoff-maximizing evaluation of the reward mechanism: since giving 1 to transfer 1 is neutral in material terms and unlinked to identifiable partners, they see no reason to engage. There is no indication of gratitude signaling: they do not publicly acknowledge having been rewarded, nor do they adjust their contributions in response in any stable way. Their non-use of reward persists even in rounds where the group achieves near-full or full contributions. Overall, they treat reward as an inefficient and ineffective tool and effectively ignore it in their strategy.

They essentially do not use rewards as an instrument. Across many opportunities with identifiable high contributors and norm-promoting players, they almost never spend to reward others. The very few instances in which they receive rewards from others do not trigger reciprocal rewarding from them. This indicates that they do not view positive reinforcement as a worthwhile or intuitive tool, either for strategic norm creation or for social bonding. The asymmetry between their high willingness to punish and near-zero willingness to reward is notable and consistent: they incur costs to harm but almost never to help. This fits a profile of someone more motivated by expressing disapproval and frustration than by supporting or encouraging cooperative partners.

They never spend to reward others, even though several players repeatedly contribute fully and some engage in intense pro-social efforts. They do not reward high contributors or those who try to coordinate cooperation, nor do they reward their punishers or anyone who might be seen as an ally. This indicates an absence of strategic rewarding as a tool to shape others’ behavior and a general reluctance to incur personal costs for positive social signaling. Given that they frequently benefit from others’ cooperation, their non-rewarding further reinforces their free-rider profile: they accept the benefit of others’ generosity, coordination, and sanctioning without contributing to that enforcement system. There is no evidence that they even consider reward as a lever.

They use rewards moderately and selectively, with an apparent emphasis on recognizing cooperative or supportive behavior rather than building strict, payoff‑maximizing incentives. For most of the game they refrain from rewarding despite contribution differences, which suggests caution about spending. When they do reward, it usually targets consistent cooperators or players making positive contributions to the group effort (either through contributions or perhaps through ongoing support), and occasionally appears aimed at reinforcing others late in the game when cooperation is fragile. They do not appear to use rewards in a systematic “if you contribute this round, I’ll reward you” contract; instead, rewards function as appreciation and moral support. Their late‑game willingness to spend a large fraction of a round’s payoff on multiple rewards, even after a long game, shows sustained prosocial motivation and a willingness to incur personal cost to encourage or thank others.

They are quite willing to spend on reward, including making sizable multi-person reward allocations that significantly reduce their own payoff. They reward high contributors and especially vocal cooperators, apparently to reinforce pro-social behavior and maintain morale. Their rewards target players who repeatedly contribute or advocate cooperation, and sometimes those who continue to contribute even while discouraged. They occasionally reward several players simultaneously, indicating a desire to broadly recognize cooperative behavior rather than form a narrow alliance. The timing and pattern of rewards suggest they use them as positive reinforcement for pro-group actions and as a way to encourage continued teamwork, despite their own minimal contributions. They also at times appear to reward in a way that stabilizes the group narrative (thanking or supporting the players who keep “holding out hope”). Their reward decisions are not obviously contingent on personal benefit or future reciprocation; they often give reward in situations where it is unlikely to be paid back materially. This reflects a willingness to use reward as expressive support for norms and group members, even at a cost, while still keeping their contribution level low.

They are an extremely active rewarder and use rewards broadly and generously throughout the game, often at substantial personal cost. Rewarding is not narrowly targeted; they reward both consistent high contributors and, at times, players whose contributions are low or ambiguous. Their pattern suggests several motives: - They consistently reward clear cooperators, reinforcing high contributions. - They sometimes reward players who are being punished or who appear disadvantaged, which looks like support or compensation rather than strict reciprocity. - They also occasionally reward players who are not obviously cooperative at that moment, implying a generalized prosocial or goodwill motive rather than purely strategic reinforcement. They frequently accept negative or very low round payoffs to give rewards, indicating they place significant weight on others’ outcomes or on signaling kindness, even when it is individually costly. They never combine reward with punishment, further emphasizing a one-sided, positive-sum approach to social control.

They almost never spend on reward, but they do make a single, modest use of it, targeting two consistently cooperative players with a small positive transfer. This occurs immediately after they verbally question punishment, suggesting a weak preference for positive reinforcement (reward) over negative enforcement (punishment) when they do engage. However, this is a one-off action; they do not persist with reward as an ongoing strategy to stabilize cooperation or to build alliances. The pattern suggests they conceptually distinguish between “pointless” punishment and slightly more acceptable reward, but that even reward is not worth repeated personal cost. They are not using reward as a calculated long-term investment; it appears more like a sporadic gesture acknowledging others’ efforts.

They never spend on rewards, despite often being surrounded by high or full cooperators and seeing others actively reward peers. They thus do not engage in positive reciprocity through this mechanism, even though rewarding is less efficient than contributing but can still incentivize cooperative norms. This consistent non‑use suggests one of: (i) they view direct cooperation via contributions as sufficient and more important than incentivizing others with side‑payments, (ii) they are conservative about spending on any “extra” social tools beyond contributions, or (iii) they are uncertain about how to use rewards effectively and prefer inaction. Given that they do accept the cost of punishment once, their avoidance of rewards appears deliberate rather than a blanket refusal to spend post‑stage resources. Overall, they rely on their own cooperative example and extremely rare punishment rather than on active positive reinforcement.

The player never uses reward, even though they are surrounded by many high contributors and repeatedly see others investing heavily in rewards. They do receive rewards multiple times but do not reciprocate by rewarding anyone in return. This indicates that gratitude, if present, does not translate into costly positive incentives. Their strategy appears to separate their own cooperative contribution choice from any additional, costly reinforcement of others’ behavior. Given that rewarding is less efficient than contributing directly to the public good and that they are already contributing near-maximally, they may implicitly view the reward mechanism as unnecessary or wasteful relative to direct contribution. However, this is speculative; their motives for not rewarding remain unknown. What is clear is that their cooperative type is “direct contributor only”: they help the group via contributions but abstain from meta-level interventions such as rewards or punishments.

The player is notably generous with rewards and uses them in a differentiated, contingent way. Early on, they are willing to spend substantial resources to reward nearly everyone who cooperates close to the maximum, signaling a strong preference for recognizing and reinforcing group-wide cooperation. Later, they target rewards more selectively toward individuals whose behavior is especially aligned with high contribution norms or who maintain strong cooperation while some others slacken. They do not mechanically reward in every round; instead, they cluster their rewarding in situations where cooperation is high or needs encouragement, and abstain in stable, high-cooperation periods where extra reinforcement seems less necessary. They never reward known free-riders when those free-riders have made very low contributions, and they do not “buy off” low contributors with positive incentives. This pattern points to a prosocial, norm-affirming strategy: they are willing to sacrifice their own payoff to acknowledge cooperative partners and to support a culture of high contribution, especially when it may influence behavior or express gratitude. Their use of reward is more frequent and heavier than their use of punishment, indicating a preference for positive reinforcement over negative sanctions.

They never use the reward option despite frequent high contributions from many others. This suggests that they either undervalue positive reinforcement compared to punishment or view it as unnecessary given that cooperation is already relatively high. It is also possible they see rewarding as less efficient or less justified than punishing clear violations, or they may simply prioritize conserving their own payoff over paying for rewards. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling through costly rewards; their norm-enforcement style is one-sided (punish defectors) rather than symmetric (reward cooperators and punish defectors).

They also never use the reward option, even when many others contribute fully and some players clearly bear high costs to enforce cooperation. This suggests they do not invest in positive sanctions or explicit reciprocity via the reward mechanism. The pattern indicates a narrow focus on their own direct contribution as their chosen cooperative act, with no additional expenditure to shape others’ behavior through incentives. Whether this stems from a payoff‑preserving motive (avoiding the extra cost), from indifference to formal reward tools, or from an assumption that rewards are unnecessary because cooperation is already high is uncertain. They do not appear to use rewards strategically (e.g., to encourage wavering contributors) or express gratitude monetarily.

They never reward anyone, despite recurrent high contributions by many players and occasional prosocial or coordinating chat messages by others. The reward technology is symmetric to punishment in cost-effectiveness, yet they consistently ignore it. This suggests that their norm-enforcement style is one-sided: they see negative sanctions against defectors as appropriate but do not see positive sanctions for cooperators as necessary or worthwhile. They do not use reward as a tool for strategic reinforcement or to signal gratitude. This pattern is consistent with a player who views full contribution as the baseline expected behavior that does not merit extra payment, while free‑riding is a violation that sometimes merits punishment. Whether they conceptually value reward but see it as inefficient, or simply don’t think in terms of positive incentives, is unknown.

They also never use the reward option, even though they are surrounded by many consistent high contributors and occasionally receive reward themselves. They do not reciprocate that positive treatment, nor do they strategically reward to reinforce cooperation or coordinate a high-contribution equilibrium. The choice not to reward remains constant across early and late rounds. Given that they benefit from others’ high contributions, their refusal to reward is consistent with a general preference to avoid any costly sanctioning action, whether positive or negative. They appear to treat reward similarly to punishment: as a non-essential, payoff-reducing tool they decline to use. There is no evidence that they differentiate between rewarding particularly cooperative players and ignoring them; instead, they simply never incur these costs. This makes them a “non-rewarding cooperator,” relying purely on their own contributions rather than explicit incentives to shape others’ behavior.

This player never uses the reward mechanism despite repeated high contributions by many cooperators. They do not provide positive reinforcement to aligned partners, even when the cost-effectiveness of reward is symmetric to punishment. This consistent absence implies that they either undervalue reward as a coordination tool, find it cognitively or emotionally less salient than punishment, or prioritize conserving coins except when expressing disapproval. The asymmetry—frequent punishment, zero reward—points to a “stick not carrot” enforcement style. Their social value orientation seems oriented toward punishing norm violators rather than supporting norm followers through direct transfers. Cooperation itself may be seen as its own reward; extra bonuses are not in their repertoire.

They never reward anyone, even when others contribute fully and sustain high levels of cooperation. This indicates no inclination to positively reinforce pro-social behavior, no visible gratitude toward high contributors, and no strategic use of rewards to shape future behavior. Given that they also do not punish, they entirely abstain from second-order pro-social tools. Their stance is consistent with a narrowly self-regarding payoff motive: they appear unwilling to pay any cost to influence others, whether through positive or negative incentives.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even when many others contribute fully and when some other players appear to be using reward occasionally. This indicates that they are not inclined to spend private resources to reinforce good behavior, even when such reward might encourage continued high contributions that would benefit them. Their simultaneous avoidance of both punishment and reward fits a purely self-regarding cost-benefit calculus: they are unwilling to incur any individual cost for the sake of changing others’ behavior, whether through positive or negative incentives. The absence of reward behavior when others cooperate strongly also implies limited gratitude signaling or reciprocity concerns; they appear content to benefit from group cooperation without investing back into it. Overall, they are an “enforcement free-rider”: they rely on others, if anyone, to supply both punishment and reward while they focus on cheap riding of whatever group norm emerges.

They never use rewards, even though several others reward and there are clear opportunities to positively reinforce high contributors (including themselves and other persistent cooperators). This suggests that they do not invest in explicit incentive shaping, even in a positive form. They appear focused on their own contribution behavior and final payoffs, not on engineering the group’s behavior via rewards. Their non-use of reward remains stable across early, middle, and late phases of the game, indicating that they do not switch into gratitude signaling or morale-boosting as the game unfolds. Their type is thus a “pure contributor”: they support the public good by contributing, but not by paying additional costs to socially manage others through rewards.

They never reward any player despite having repeated opportunities. This holds even when many others are consistently contributing at high levels and some players spend heavily to punish free-riders. Their lack of rewarding indicates a clear asymmetry: they are willing to invest resources to punish defectors but not to positively reinforce cooperation. This may reflect either a skepticism about the efficiency of rewarding, a preference for negative over positive enforcement, or a belief that full contributions are simply expected and do not merit extra compensation. There is no sign of gratitude signaling or strategic attempts to encourage cooperators with rewards.

They also never spend on rewards, even when some others are clear, consistent cooperators and when rewarding could act as positive reinforcement. This indicates a general reluctance to use the secondary mechanism at all—both punishment and reward. Their cooperative behavior is “self‑contained”; they do not try to shape others’ incentives through side payments. They neither signal gratitude nor selectively bolster allies. This may reflect a cost‑minimization stance regarding secondary mechanisms, a belief that contributions themselves are sufficient expression of cooperation, or simple inattention to the potential strategic benefits of reward. Their attitude toward gratitude or reciprocity in this channel remains unknown but behaviorally inactive.

They very rarely reward and do so only once, in a way that runs against the immediate punitive norm: they grant a small reward to a player who has been heavily punished and is socially targeted. This indicates a limited but meaningful willingness to use positive incentives, potentially as a corrective or supportive signal rather than as a systematic reinforcement of high contributors. The low frequency of rewards suggests they are cautious with costly prosocial actions beyond contribution itself; they may view direct contributions as the main cooperative tool and regard reward as exceptional rather than routine. Their one reward choice hints at some sympathy for disadvantaged or ostracized others, or at least a tendency to counterbalance extreme punishment rather than pile on.

They never reward anyone in the entire game despite many players consistently contributing fully. They even forgo cheap opportunities to reinforce cooperators, despite clearly attending to others’ contributions and outcomes. This indicates a strong asymmetry: they are willing to pay heavily to punish but unwilling to pay even a little to reward. Their cooperative norm is enforced negatively only; they appear to treat full contribution as expected baseline behavior that does not merit extra compensation. Strategic use of positive incentives is absent; they rely exclusively on sanctions to shape behavior.

They never reward anyone, despite multiple opportunities and the presence of clear high contributors. This strongly suggests that they do not use positive incentives to reinforce good behavior and instead rely purely on negative sanctions. The asymmetry between zero reward and massive punishment indicates a fundamentally punishment-centric enforcement style rather than a balanced reciprocity style. It is unclear whether they are indifferent to rewarding, see reward as inefficient, or simply prioritize punishing defectors over encouraging cooperators; only their revealed preference to allocate all enforcement budget to punishment can be inferred.

They never spend on reward, at any point, despite reward being net-beneficial to the recipient and commonly used by others. They also receive substantial reward across many rounds from others, yet this does not induce them to reciprocate or join the reward norm. Their behavior indicates a consistent “non-rewarder” stance, not an early-round hesitation. Possible motives (all speculative and thus low confidence) include: a preference to conserve personal payoff, a view that contributing is sufficient and side-payments are unnecessary, discomfort with the complexity of rewarding, or indifference to social reinforcement mechanisms. What is clear behaviorally is that they separate cooperation from reward: they fully cooperate but invest nothing in actively promoting or reinforcing others’ cooperation.

This player is an extremely heavy and frequent user of rewards, often spending substantial amounts on rewarding others, sometimes to the point of severely reducing their own payoff. They use rewards even in rounds where they contribute nothing themselves, indicating that their rewarding is not limited to reciprocating for having been personally cooperative; they are willing to “buy” cooperation or to encourage others even while personally free-riding. Their threshold for initiating rewards is low: as long as others appear cooperative, they are willing to spend. They particularly reinforce high contributors in generally cooperative environments, apparently trying to sustain or stabilize group cooperation by compensating others. At times, they spend far more on rewards than they receive, suggesting that immediate material payoff maximization is not their sole motive. They appear comfortable playing the role of “social investor,” bearing private costs to create or maintain a cooperative atmosphere. They also sometimes reward in rounds where they themselves are high contributors, suggesting that they may be attempting to build or sustain reciprocal reward networks and not only to exploit others’ generosity. However, their reward behavior is not tightly targeted in a clearly rule-based way (for example, only top contributors); instead, it looks broad and sometimes excessive. This points to a type who values pro-social reinforcement and sees reward as the main lever to influence others’ behavior, even at personal expense. At the same time, their willingness to free-ride while still rewarding others shows that their reward giving is not purely altruistic; they may believe that supporting a generally cooperative climate allows them to reap large benefits from occasional or repeated defection.

This player almost never spends on rewards and only uses the reward mechanism once, and even then at a modest level. Given that they themselves repeatedly receive rewards from others, their continued non-use of rewards suggests they do not treat rewarding as a necessary tool for sustaining cooperation. This may reflect a preference to conserve private payoffs, a belief that contribution behavior should stand on its own without extra incentives, or uncertainty about how to reward optimally. They do not systematically reciprocate the rewards they receive; there is no consistent move from having been rewarded to then investing in rewarding others. They also do not appear to use rewards to shape others’ contributions strategically (for example, to target borderline or improving contributors), most likely because they almost never reward. Overall, they act as a “cooperative non-rewarder”: they contribute a lot but largely leave incentive provision to others.

This player is an active and persistent rewarder. They spend on rewards in almost every round, including when it is personally costly and not obviously profitable in terms of immediate net payoff. They appear to use rewards primarily to support and encourage cooperators rather than to punish low contributors indirectly. Their reward giving is not tightly tied to their own contribution level; they continue to reward even in rounds where they contribute nothing, indicating that reward behavior is driven more by perceived norms of cooperation than by strict reciprocity for their own actions. They show willingness to invest substantial coins into rewards in some rounds, suggesting they treat rewarding as an important tool for sustaining or expressing approval of cooperative behavior. The pattern suggests a “pro-social incentivizer” type: they likely believe that rewarding cooperators stabilizes high contributions and may also express gratitude or group-mindedness, even if direct personal returns are unclear in the short run. Their reward choices do not appear purely self-serving; they sometimes sacrifice significant payoff to sustain this reward strategy.

The player never spends on rewards despite repeated opportunities and despite receiving rewards themselves in multiple rounds. This is stable and unconditional: they do not reward high contributors, do not reciprocate to those who seem supportive, and do not attempt to strategically encourage cooperation through positive incentives. Their zero-reward strategy holds in periods when others are extensively rewarding peers and when the environment is cooperative. This suggests that either they view the reward mechanism as not worth the cost, they are strongly payoff-protective with respect to direct expenditures beyond contributions, or they simply adopt a “no-cost sanctioning/reward” stance as a heuristic. Because they continue to cooperate without rewarding, they appear to rely on others’ propensity to use rewards, effectively free-riding on the reward system while supporting the public good via contributions. Their behavior does not display gratitude signaling through rewards, even when they personally benefit from others’ rewards.

They are a frequent and relatively heavy user of reward, often spending a substantial portion of their payoff to benefit others. They use reward in a broadly pro-social way that targets cooperators rather than defectors, indicating an intention to reinforce high contributions. They do not use reward as self-serving manipulation (e.g., they do not systematically reward low contributors to buy favor); rather, reward behavior tracks others’ apparent cooperativeness, suggesting a desire to sustain group norms. Their willingness to spend heavily persists over much of the game, even when others’ reward decisions are noisy or when they themselves are not always rewarded, which implies that they value norm reinforcement more than immediate personal payoff maximization. However, they are not blindly generous: at some points they suspend reward or cut back sharply, especially when the environment becomes less cooperative or when the marginal impact of reward appears lower. This indicates strategic prosociality—reward is used as a tool to shape behavior, not as a fixed moral rule. They do not appear to systematically reward in response to being rewarded themselves; any gratitude or reciprocity is present but not clearly dominant over an efficiency- and contribution-based targeting rule.

This player is willing to incur a small cost to reward a very low contributor who ends up with a high payoff. The reward is minimal and singular, suggesting that they are exploratory or selective rather than strongly committed to a broad rewarding strategy. Because the recipient is one of the lowest contributors, the reward is unlikely to be a straightforward “reward high cooperators” signal. Possible interpretations (all low confidence) include: attempting to encourage a low contributor to increase future cooperation; responding positively to the recipient’s overall outcome (e.g., high payoff) rather than to their contribution; or testing the institution of reward to see how it works in practice. They do not repeat reward behavior later, even when others engage in rewarding, which suggests they do not adopt reward as a central strategic tool for norm enforcement or alliance-building. In summary, they are capable of positive reinforcement but apply it sparingly and in a somewhat unconventional direction, with the underlying rationale uncertain.

They consistently avoid using the reward mechanism, even though it is available, identifiable, and used by others. They do not reward high contributors, co-contributors who also give the maximum, or players who reward them. This indicates that they do not engage in strategic reinforcement of cooperative behavior via material rewards. Their stance appears to be non-interventionist: they contribute a lot themselves but refrain from actively shaping others’ incentives through rewards. Whether this reflects indifference to others’ prosocial actions, a desire to conserve their own payoff, or a belief that rewards are unnecessary or inefficient is unknown. There is no sign of targeted or group-level rewarding; the reward channel remains unused.

They never use rewards despite having several clear opportunities to positively reinforce high contributors. This indicates a reluctance to incur costs even for prosocial reinforcement, and/or a belief that direct contribution is the primary channel through which they wish to express cooperation. They do not appear to engage in alliance-building or gratitude-signaling via rewards, even in the presence of clearly cooperative others. Strategically, they seem to separate their own contribution choice from secondary mechanisms; they cooperate through giving, not through meta-level incentives. Whether they conceptually approve of rewards but find them too costly, or are indifferent to shaping others’ behavior via incentives, is unknown; the observed pattern is simply non-use.

They consistently abstain from rewarding, even when they clearly observe several others making high or maximal contributions and see that some group members are willing to expend resources on rewards. This points to a pattern of not using positive incentives either, despite personally matching or exceeding the efforts of highly cooperative peers later on. Possible reasons include a desire to conserve resources, a belief that rewarding is unnecessary given already high cooperation, or a general preference to avoid intervention in others’ payoffs (specific motive unknown). Their stance is internally consistent: they cooperate themselves but do not pay to shape or acknowledge others’ behavior through formal rewards.

The player refrains from rewarding entirely, despite the presence of several high contributors. This suggests a clear asymmetry: they are willing to bear costs to punish norm violators but not to reward norm followers. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to encourage high contribution, build alliances, or express gratitude. Their cooperative enforcement is thus “stick-only” rather than “carrot-and-stick.” It is unclear whether they perceive rewards as inefficient, unnecessary, or simply overlook them; what is evident is that they prioritize negative sanctioning over positive reinforcement when trying to shape others’ behavior.

They are clearly willing to spend own resources to reward others, and they do so in a discriminating, contribution-contingent way. They selectively reward some of the highest contributors while ignoring medium or low contributors, consistent with a strategic reinforcement motive rather than indiscriminate generosity. The pattern suggests they use rewards to signal approval to strong cooperators and to encourage maintenance of high contributions. They do not appear to use rewards to “pull up” moderate or low contributors; instead, they focus on reinforcing a cooperative elite, perhaps seeing these players as key anchors for group norms or as especially deserving. They accept the cost of rewarding even in a very short game and without guaranteed future interaction awareness, which points to an intrinsic preference for positive reciprocity and norm-affirming behavior rather than a purely forward-looking investment.

They never use the reward option, despite the presence of many high contributors and visible instances of others rewarding. This indicates a strong tendency to avoid prosocial costly signaling when it provides no guaranteed payoff advantage. They do not selectively reward those who contribute more than they do, nor do they strategically reward potential partners or very high contributors. Their non-use of rewards suggests that they either (a) place little value on reinforcing others’ cooperation at their own expense, or (b) see the reward technology as strategically dominated by simply keeping coins. They do not appear to use rewards as a tool for alliance building, reciprocity, or norm expression; their stance is to keep interactions purely through contributions and payoffs rather than secondary transfers.

This player never uses the reward option despite multiple high contributors among peers and visible use of rewards by others. They do not selectively reinforce other high contributors, nor do they reward those who reward others. This could indicate a payoff-focused reluctance to spend on social incentives, a view that their own high contribution is already sufficient pro-social behavior, or uncertainty about the strategic value of rewarding. There is no sign of alliance-building via rewards or attempts to steer group norms through positive reinforcement.

The player never uses the reward option, despite multiple peers contributing highly and some players spending on rewards. They therefore do not engage in positive reinforcement, alliance building, or gratitude signaling through the reward mechanism. This suggests a strong payoff-conserving preference when it comes to voluntary transfers: they refrain from both positive and negative costly social actions. Whether they conceptually approve of rewarding cooperators is unknown; empirically, they choose not to pay to do so in this setting.

This player never uses rewards, even though many others contribute generously and some peers do use rewards. They do not spend to positively reinforce high contributors or to build explicit alliances. This suggests they prioritize direct monetary payoff over investing in others’ goodwill or future cooperation through rewards, at least over the short horizon they perceive. It may also reflect a belief that contributions are already high enough without additional incentives, or that their own cooperative level is sufficient without further signaling. Their nonuse of rewards is consistent and not contingent on others’ generosity levels, pointing to a baseline “non-rewarder” or strictly budget-conserving stance in both positive and negative sanctioning.

This player does not reward others at any point, even when many others contribute at high levels and when some peers engage in rewarding or punitive activity. They are therefore an unrewarding cooperator: they contribute generously themselves but do not spend additional resources to transfer payoff to others. There is no sign of targeted positive reinforcement toward high contributors or toward those who appear prosocial in other ways. The most parsimonious interpretation is that they value cooperation but treat reward as an unnecessary or inefficient expense, preferring to let their own contribution be their main prosocial act. Whether they refrain from rewarding due to cost–benefit calculation, indifference to expressive gratitude, or lack of belief in reward as an effective signal is unknown.

They never spend on rewards at any point, even though rewarding is sometimes used extensively by others. This indicates low willingness to sacrifice for indirect, reputational, or future-oriented gains in this environment, at least given the observed group dynamics. Whether this stems from skepticism about the efficacy of rewards, indifference to social signaling, or simple payoff-maximization is unknown, but their consistent non-use of rewards shows that any such motives are dominated by the desire to avoid these costs.

This player is an active but selective user of the reward mechanism. They are willing to incur non-trivial costs to reward others across many rounds, but do not reward in every opportunity. Their rewarding is not blanket generosity; instead, it targets specific players whose behavior they likely interpret as especially cooperative, constructive, or supportive of their own interests. They do not use reward as a purely efficiency-based tool (since the mechanism here is cost-neutral in aggregate). This pattern suggests they may be trying to stabilize cooperative clusters and encourage allies rather than maximize short-run payoffs. They also appear to react to their own payoff history: after periods of heavy reward expenditure and low personal returns, their willingness to spend drops sharply for a while, then returns in more modest doses. This implies they monitor the cost-benefit balance of rewarding and adjust intensity accordingly, rather than sticking dogmatically to a moral rule. Overall, they use reward strategically for norm reinforcement and relationship management, not as indiscriminate altruism.

This player never spends on rewards in any round, despite the presence of clearly identifiable, often generous contributors and repeated opportunities to reinforce them. This player never spends any coins on rewarding others, even in settings where others are contributing a lot or where some players are clearly using rewards to encourage cooperation. They almost never use the reward mechanism, despite having multiple opportunities and clear examples of other players spending heavily to reward. The player never uses the reward mechanism across all observed opportunities. This holds even when they themselves receive positive rewards and can see others using rewards intensively. This player is extremely active and erratic in the reward stage, at times spending very large amounts on rewards and at other times spending nothing. They do not use rewards in a tightly payoff-maximizing way: they sometimes spend so much that their own payoff is well below what they could have kept simply by not rewarding at all. This indicates a willingness to trade off personal earnings for other objectives, but those objectives are not transparently cooperative; they do not persistently reinforce high contributors or systematically discourage free riding. Overall, their reward behavior is best characterized as high-cost, highly selective, and unstable in direction, driven by their own shifting priorities rather than by a consistent social norm.

This player never uses the reward mechanism despite having multiple opportunities and clear examples of others rewarding cooperators. They never use the reward system, despite its availability and the fact that others cooperate fully. They never spend any of their own coins on rewards, despite being in a highly cooperative group and receiving rewards from others. They never actively spend on reward, but they accept and acknowledge rewards given by others. They almost never use the reward mechanism despite being in a highly cooperative group where most others consistently go all-in. They never spend coins on reward, despite being in an environment where nearly all others are consistently cooperating and where some peers explicitly mention rewarding in the final round. The player uses the reward mechanism actively but selectively. They sometimes spend nontrivial amounts on reward and sometimes spend nothing. They are an active and sometimes heavy user of the reward mechanism. They spend on rewards in many rounds, occasionally at substantial personal cost. They never spend any coins to reward others, despite having multiple opportunities and seeing others do so. They never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite having multiple opportunities and despite many group members using rewards to support cooperators. They never spend any coins on rewards, even though they consistently cooperate and often find themselves surrounded by other cooperators. The player never rewards anyone, despite many opportunities and despite facing others who contribute consistently and sometimes reward or punish in ways that could be interpreted as attempts at coordination. This player is willing to reward, but in a restrained, targeted, and sometimes strategically ambiguous way. The player never uses the reward option, even when others make very high or improving contributions and even when some peers spend substantial amounts rewarding. There is no evidence that they deploy reward strategically to stabilize high cooperation; their cooperation is offered regardless, and they appear to rely solely on punishment plus their own example rather than on formal rewarding.

They use reward selectively but in a highly nonstandard way. They never spend on rewards either, despite repeated instances where others make high or even perfect contributions and where some players lavish large positive transfers on cooperators. They never spend on rewards, even though they repeatedly receive rewards from others.
