However, the consistency across many opportunities makes confusion less likely and instead points to a simple rule: contribute fully but do not pay to reward. They implicitly separate their cooperative norm (contributing) from costly social sanctioning or thanking (rewarding), and keep the latter at zero.

They are an extremely active rewarder, repeatedly spending substantial portions of their payoff to give small, dispersed rewards to many or all other active players. They tend to give rewards even in rounds where they themselves contribute nothing, which shows that their rewarding is not contingent on their own cooperative behavior. Instead, they appear to use rewards as a tool to stabilize or encourage others’ cooperation: rewards are typically directed widely across the group, often favoring participants who contribute, and rarely if ever self-serving in a narrow sense. This pattern fits a “strategic rewarder–free-rider” hybrid: they invest in others’ satisfaction and cooperative inclination while continuing to maintain a personal contribution schedule that yields them high net payoffs. They do not use reward in a selective, reputation-building manner (e.g., favoring allies or punishing defectors indirectly); instead, their pattern looks like broad positive reinforcement of the group’s participation, even when they personally deviate. This suggests they accept significant personal cost to sustain a cooperative climate from which they can sometimes free-ride. Their willingness to spend repeatedly on rewards, despite never receiving corresponding rewards of similar magnitude, indicates they are not simply reciprocating; they appear comfortable acting as a unilateral reward provider to shape group behavior.

The player never spends any coins on rewards. Across all opportunities, their reward cost is always zero. This is notable because they consistently contribute fully, and other cooperators frequently reward contributors in some periods, yet this player does not reciprocate via the reward mechanism. They accept rewards from others but do not pay to reinforce desired behavior, suggesting they either (a) place very high weight on preserving their own payoff relative to the benefit of rewarding, (b) do not view rewarding as instrumental or necessary given already-high cooperation levels, or (c) do not pay much attention to, or do not value, the reward mechanism at all. There is no evidence that they use rewards strategically (e.g., to encourage borderline cooperators or to retaliate against free riders by withholding rewards). Instead, they behave as a “pure contributor but non-rewarder”: they are willing to provide the public good itself, but unwilling to invest further in costly social incentives.

They almost never spend on rewards. Across the game they only once pay a small, isolated amount to reward someone, and there is no clear pattern of them using rewards strategically or reciprocally. They instead function primarily as a passive recipient of rewards from highly prosocial others. Their chat suggests they conceptually like the idea of punishment and negative sanctions far more than positive reinforcement: early on they explicitly wish for a mechanism to take coins from others rather than to give them more, and later they question the logic of a player who heavily rewards while not contributing, treating that pattern as suspicious or “non‑human.” That reaction implies they view costly rewarding without contributing as atypical and perhaps as a sign of an artificial or experimental agent. Their own minimal rewarding behavior is consistent with a payoff‑concerned cooperator who is willing to invest in the public good but reluctant to incur extra individual costs for social signaling beyond contribution itself. They appear to treat full contribution as the main and sufficient cooperative signal, rather than using the reward stage as an additional lever.

They never spend on rewards despite having repeated opportunities and often being part of a highly cooperative majority. At the same time, they repeatedly receive small rewards from others, indicating that their cooperative behavior is recognized and valued, but they do not reciprocate in kind. The consistent absence of reward spending, even when everyone else cooperates and a pro-social reward norm emerges, suggests one of the following (true motive is unknown): (a) a strictly payoff-maximizing attitude toward the reward mechanism (reward is exactly cost-1 for benefit-1, so it is individually neutral and thus not worth the effort), (b) a belief that rewards are unnecessary when cooperation is already high, or (c) inattention or low salience of the reward step. Given their steady engagement with contributions and occasional chat, inattention to the mechanism seems less likely but cannot be ruled out. They do not use rewards strategically to shape others’ behavior, to express gratitude, or to coordinate against free-riders. They appear content to let others bear the cost of reinforcing cooperation and simply benefit from the public good and incoming rewards. This pattern is consistent with a “cooperative non-rewarder”: they invest heavily in the public account but treat the reward stage as irrelevant or inefficient, even when social norms around rewarding emerge.

They are willing to spend on rewards and do so selectively rather than uniformly. They never reward defectors; rewards are only directed to players who contribute to the public fund. They use rewards for two main purposes: to recognize consistent cooperators and to reinforce or encourage cooperative behavior in others. Their willingness to pay for rewards persists even when they themselves receive little or no reward in return, indicating prosocial rather than purely strategic reciprocity motives. At the same time, their reward pattern is not mechanically equal across all contributors: some cooperators receive rewards in certain periods and not others, suggesting they attend to recent behavior (e.g., a drop in contributions) or perceived reliability when deciding whom to reward. They are comfortable sacrificing some payoff to shape the group environment in a positive direction, but they do not escalate reward spending excessively; their total reward outlays are moderate relative to what would be possible, consistent with a boundedly “prosocial but still somewhat payoff-aware” type.

They never spend anything on rewards, even when others are clearly using rewards to encourage cooperation. They also abstain from rewarding those who reward them. This pattern suggests that they do not use the reward mechanism either as a strategic tool to shape others’ behavior or as an expression of gratitude or norm enforcement. Their guiding incentive appears to be strict payoff maximization within each round, with no willingness to invest current resources in potential longer-term group benefits or reputational gains. Whether they conceptually understand the potential strategic value of rewarding is unknown.

They never spend any coins on rewards, even when clear high contributors exist and even when they themselves are directly rewarded by others. This indicates a strong preference for keeping payoff rather than engaging in costly positive sanctioning, despite the neutral cost–benefit ratio of the reward mechanism in this setup. Their stance suggests that, for them, cooperation via contribution is sufficient, and additional reinforcement via reward is unnecessary or not worth the complexity or risk. There is no observable pattern of selective rewarding; absence of use across different contribution environments points to a generally non-rewarding, non-enforcing type rather than a nuanced, contingent reward strategy. They do not appear to use rewards to build alliances, express gratitude, or shape others’ behavior.

They show a limited but nonzero willingness to reward others. When they do reward, they target clear high contributors rather than non-contributors, which indicates a straightforward “reward cooperation” heuristic rather than strategic manipulation. Their reward use is sparse and not persistent; they reward in some cooperative phases but not in others, even when high contributors are present. This pattern suggests that rewards are not central to their strategy and may be used more as a one-off recognition or experiment than as a consistent tool to shape group behavior. There is no evidence that they weigh the cost-benefit of rewarding very carefully; the fact that the cost and benefit of reward are equal and they use it only occasionally suggests low salience of efficiency considerations rather than strong economic optimization.

They occasionally use rewards, but not consistently, and at notable personal cost. When they do reward, they expend a nontrivial portion of their payoff, indicating a willingness to sacrifice to reinforce behavior they view positively. However, they do not reliably reward all high contributors, nor do they maintain a stable pattern of rewarding over time. This suggests either exploratory or uncertain use of the mechanism rather than a clear long-term strategy. Their reward decisions appear loosely aligned with cooperation (they do not reward clear free-riders), so their trigger seems to be “contributed to the public good” rather than reciprocity for personal gain. They do not exploit the fact that reward is cost-neutral in aggregate (cost equals benefit), which implies they either misunderstand or ignore the strategic equivalence and use it more as a normative “pat on the back” tool than an efficiency-maximizing lever.

They never spend on rewards at any point, despite having multiple opportunities and despite being embedded in a group where several others actively use the reward option. This is not a borderline case: their reward expenditure is consistently zero. Given that contributing is privately costly and they already choose low-to-moderate contributions, it is consistent that they also avoid the added cost of rewarding. They do not seem to use rewards instrumentally to sustain high contributions by others, even though others around them are strongly rewarding high contributors. This suggests either they do not believe their individual reward choices matter for group behavior, or they place very high weight on preserving their own payoff relative to any social or strategic benefits of rewarding. Their lack of rewarding does not change when they personally receive reward from others; they remain non-rewarding even after being targeted. Thus they do not appear motivated by reciprocal gratitude in this mechanism, nor by an urge to “participate” in the emergent reward norm. They also do not selectively reward either consistent high contributors or exceptional changes in behavior. Overall, they behave as a pure non-rewarder, driven mainly by the immediate cost of rewarding rather than by long-term cooperative reinforcement.

This player uses the reward mechanism sparingly and in a highly conservative, self-focused way. Across the game, they either spend nothing or a very small amount on rewards relative to what is possible. They occasionally incur the minimum possible cost to give reward, and they do so only in situations where they themselves are also receiving some reward from others in that same period. There is no evidence they ever make large, costly reward investments. Given that buying one unit of reward is exactly cost-neutral (they pay the same amount as the recipient gains), their reluctance to spend suggests either (a) a preference to conserve their own payoff rather than actively shaping others’ behavior, or (b) a belief that reward will not meaningfully change others’ contributions. They do not adopt the “altruistic rewarding” pattern seen in some other players who spend heavily on others regardless of self-gain. Their small, occasional use of reward when they themselves are being rewarded is consistent with weak reciprocity or gratitude signaling: they may be mirroring the cooperative climate or reciprocating prosocial signals in a limited way. However, because they never escalate to strong, targeted rewarding of top contributors, they do not appear to use rewards strategically to reinforce specific high contributors or to build coalitions. Instead, their type is “light, reactive rewarder”: they mostly keep their coins, with only minimal, situational use of the reward tool.

This player is unusually willing to spend heavily on rewards and treats the reward stage as their primary lever for influencing the group. They repeatedly invest substantial amounts of their private payoff in rewarding others, even to the point of severely reducing their own earnings in some rounds. This is not consistent with pure payoff maximization and indicates a real preference for reinforcing behaviors they value or for shaping the social environment, even at significant personal cost. Their reward behavior appears selective and strategic rather than random. They do not reward in every round; instead, they concentrate reward spending in periods where cooperation is high and where others are also actively rewarding. This pattern suggests they are attempting to reinforce perceived cooperators and maintain a high-contribution equilibrium. When they later switch to free-riding, they simultaneously stop rewarding, indicating that in their mind, rewarding and contributing are part of the same “cooperative effort package”: once they disengage from one, they disengage from both. They sometimes receive rewards themselves, but their own reward behavior does not obviously track direct reciprocity; there is no clear evidence that they only or primarily reward those who rewarded them. This leans toward a more group-focused or norm-maintenance motive rather than dyadic tit-for-tat. However, because identities of rewarders are hidden and the mapping from their choices to realized receipts is opaque, their exact targeting rule is unknown. Confidence is higher that they reward high contributors and central cooperators than that they reward based on personal gain. The combination of high reward expenditure and later cessation suggests a player who initially uses reward as a way to “buy” or support a cooperative climate, then later views continued rewarding as wasted effort once they perceive the system as self-sustaining or as no longer worth the extra cost.

They almost never spend on rewards and, when they do, they use it sparingly. This reveals a strong reluctance to pay personal costs to influence or thank others. Reward is not a central tool in their strategy. Their rare use of reward coincides with them receiving substantial rewards from others. This suggests their own rewarding is more likely a reactive gesture—possibly signaling gratitude or reciprocity to someone perceived as generous—rather than a standing commitment to reinforcing high contributors in general. However, because identities of rewarders are hidden, any targeted reciprocity would be at best probabilistic; the low frequency and volume of their rewarding indicate that even probabilistic reciprocity is weak. They do not appear to use reward instrumentally to steer group behavior. High contributors and heavy rewarders exist in the group, yet this player does not systematically support them. They retain the option to free-ride not only on others’ contributions but also on others’ costly rewarding efforts. In summary, they treat the reward mechanism as something to mostly ignore, occasionally engaging with it in a limited way when they personally benefit, but without a consistent norm of rewarding cooperators or rewarders.

This player is very sparing with rewards and does not use them as a primary strategic tool. Most of the time they refrain from rewarding altogether, even when others clearly contribute at or near the maximum and even when others are actively rewarding high contributors. When they do spend on rewards, it is in small, isolated instances rather than as part of a sustained reinforcement pattern. The evidence does not allow clear identification of their exact targets, but the minimal and sporadic use of the mechanism suggests one of two main possibilities: either they place low value on reward as an instrument of cooperation management, or they are uncertain about how to use it effectively and thus default to non‑use. They do not appear to systematically “buy” better group behavior by supporting top cooperators, nor do they seem to use rewards to reciprocate being rewarded themselves. Their choices are not consistent with a strong “peer reward manager” or “strategic investor in reputation” type; instead, they resemble a player who relies on contribution norms alone and treats reward as optional, marginal, or secondary. There is no evidence of spite via the reward channel (e.g., deliberate non‑reward of low contributors as a meaningful “signal”), only a generally low engagement with the reward technology.

This player is extremely restrained in using rewards. They sometimes spend a small, non-escalating amount on rewards and often spend nothing, even when many other players reward extensively. Their reward use does not track obvious shifts in others’ contribution levels or payoff imbalances in a systematic way, and there is no clear evidence that they use rewards either as strong positive reinforcement or as a reciprocal “thank you” for receiving rewards themselves; they sometimes reward when they have not just been generously rewarded, and sometimes abstain even after receiving substantial rewards. This suggests that they do not treat the reward mechanism as a core strategic tool. Instead, they appear to view rewards as optional, low-frequency signals rather than as a continuous management of group behavior. They do not appear to be motivated by competitive status via paying out large visible rewards, nor by “keeping up” with more generous rewarders. Overall, they are a high contributor but a minimal, somewhat indifferent rewarder, with low inclination to pay private costs to differentially shape others’ incentives.

The player is an unusually heavy user of the reward mechanism and routinely spends substantial amounts to reward others. They clearly do not treat the reward channel as a rare or marginal tool: they often invest a large share of their payoff into rewarding. Given that reward is exactly cost-neutral and anonymous, this suggests they see it as a norm-enforcing or gratitude-signaling device rather than a way to change material payoffs. The main inferred trigger for rewarding is high or above-average contribution to the public good; there is no observable correlation between receiving rewards and later self-interested restraint in giving rewards, so reciprocity directed at personal benefactors is at best weakly supported and remains low confidence. They show a willingness to incur costs to reinforce cooperation even when they themselves are not directly rewarded in that period, indicating they prioritize supporting cooperative partners over preserving their own immediate payoff. They do not use rewards selectively to “buy” future help from specific targets in any clear, targeted way; instead, their pattern better fits diffuse, pro-social reinforcement of cooperators. They never reward low contributors or free-riders as far as can be inferred, indicating they try to keep the signal “clean”: rewards are reserved for those behaving cooperatively. Strategic sophistication is present but likely bounded: they understand that rewarding can encourage cooperation, but they do not seem to optimize the cost–benefit ratio of their reward spending and are willing to overinvest from a narrow payoff-maximizing perspective.

This player is willing to spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, but not consistently across all rounds. Their reward behavior is selective and appears tied to perceived contribution levels and perhaps to patterns of others’ rewarding, though precise targets cannot be inferred because identities of rewarders are hidden. They sometimes spend a meaningful portion of their payoff on rewards, and other times spend nothing even in similar contribution environments. This on–off pattern suggests they use rewards as an occasional tool rather than a constant strategy. The direction of spending aligns more with supporting cooperation than with self-enrichment: the reward technology is neutral in payoff terms, so any spending on rewards indicates a social or strategic motive rather than direct monetary gain. Their use of reward is compatible with several possible triggers: - reinforcing high or very high contributors; - signaling approval or gratitude to others who seem to reward or cooperate strongly; - experimenting to see whether increased rewarding stabilizes group cooperation. They do not appear to systematically “buy” personal favor; there is no pattern of always spending to secure their own incoming rewards, and at times they accept receiving rewards while giving none. This suggests they are not purely exchange-oriented or tit-for-tat in the reward stage. Instead, they seem to treat rewards as a discretionary, prosocial instrument, used when they judge it worthwhile to recognize or encourage certain behaviors, and withheld when they either feel it is unnecessary or too costly relative to the perceived benefit. Overall, they are a conditional rewarder: capable of generous reinforcement, yet willing to refrain from rewarding when signals from the group do not strongly justify the cost. Their focus appears more on supporting cooperative norms than on micromanaging every deviation.

This player never spends on rewarding others, despite having repeated opportunities and clear evidence that many others are using the reward mechanism extensively, often in ways that look like attempts to support high contributors or build alliances. Their own behavior shows a complete avoidance of that cost. This suggests that they treat the reward technology as individually unprofitable “noise” rather than as a tool to strategically shape behavior. They do not use reward to reciprocate high contributions, to express gratitude, or to signal themselves as a cooperative partner. They also do not use it to join existing informal coalitions of rewarders. Either they doubt that anonymous, one-shot rewards can meaningfully influence future behavior, or they simply prioritize retaining their own payoff over any long-term coordination benefits. Because they never reward in any context—neither when others are very generous nor when they themselves benefit from group cooperation—the most consistent interpretation is that they have a strictly individualistic cost–benefit threshold for prosocial moves: costly positive actions that do not yield clear, immediate, private gains are avoided. They may still value public-good contributions on their own, but they do not see additional value in layering rewards on top of that.

The player is notably reluctant to use the reward mechanism, despite being a consistent high contributor themselves and despite being in groups where many others also cooperate. They never pay to reward anyone, even when almost all others behave cooperatively and even after receiving rewards themselves. This indicates they do not treat positive reinforcement as worthwhile or necessary, perhaps because they view cooperation as a baseline obligation that does not merit extra payment. They also do not appear to use rewards strategically to build alliances or to stabilize cooperation. Compared with their willingness to punish, their use of rewards is effectively zero, suggesting an asymmetric norm‑enforcement style that relies on negative, not positive, incentives.

This player does not voluntarily reward others at any point, despite frequent high contributions by many peers and the availability of a one-to-one reward technology. They appear unwilling to incur certain personal cost merely to express gratitude or reinforce cooperation. This is consistent with a payoff-oriented mindset: if reward is neutral or negative in expected personal return, they avoid it. Their use of the reward mechanism is purely passive: they receive rewards from others but never initiate them. They therefore are not a “positive reciprocity” type and do not invest in building alliances or reputations through rewards, even when many around them behave cooperatively enough to justify such actions on normative grounds. In summary, they behave as a non-rewarder by default: they rely on contributions themselves as their main pro-social act and leave costly positive sanctioning to others.

This player does not use the reward mechanism at all, despite being a consistent high contributor and observing other high contributors. Their behavior suggests they do not see costly rewards as necessary for sustaining cooperation when a strong norm is already in place, or they view the cost–benefit ratio of reward as unattractive relative to punishment. They may implicitly rely on punishment and on their own example of full contribution as sufficient tools for norm enforcement. The absence of selective positive reinforcement, even toward especially cooperative or possibly allied players, indicates a preference for negative incentives when sanctioning is needed, and neutrality otherwise. Their stance on rewards as a strategic tool beyond this experiment is therefore uncertain, but in this environment they effectively ignore the reward option.

They are quite willing to spend on rewards and do so repeatedly, even though the reward technology is efficiency-neutral (they pay as much as they give). Their rewards consistently target players who contribute fully or who sustain or re-join high cooperation, including both consistently cooperative peers and at least one case of a prior low contributor who later cooperates. This pattern suggests that they use rewards both as gratitude for reliable cooperators and as positive reinforcement for (re-)alignment with the cooperation norm. They allocate multiple small, evenly distributed rewards rather than concentrating on a single ally, indicating a broad, group-oriented prosocial style rather than narrow coalition-building. They do not systematically reward every cooperator, implying some selectivity; they seem to highlight either especially salient cooperators or those whose behavior change or reliability stands out to them. Overall, they accept a personal payoff cost to support and stabilize group cooperation through positive incentives.

They show a clear willingness to use reward to reinforce cooperative partners, even though rewarding is financially neutral (no social multiplier). They direct rewards toward a consistently high-contributing peer, apparently treating this player as a “model cooperator” deserving positive reinforcement. This indicates a preference for affirming and supporting reliable contributors as a social norm, rather than using reward purely as a profit-seeking tool. They are selective: only one partner is rewarded repeatedly, which suggests they either (a) view that partner as especially trustworthy or exemplary, or (b) believe reinforcing one salient cooperator is enough to stabilize cooperation. They do not reward everyone, even when nearly all contribute, so their reward strategy is not egalitarian; it is targeted recognition. The timing of rewards, especially when cooperation levels dip in the group, suggests an attempt to buttress and encourage stable cooperation under pressure. Overall, they function as a focused positive-reinforcer of group norms rather than a broad “reward for all” type.

This player never uses the reward option, even when nearly all other players contribute fully and at least one peer rewards them. They do not engage in positive reinforcement of high contributors, nor do they reciprocate specific rewards received. Given that reward in this game is exactly cost-equal to its benefit, their non-use suggests they see it as at best neutral and at worst a distraction from maximizing their own payoff. It is unclear whether they conceptually value reciprocity; they simply do not spend coins on rewarding others, even in highly cooperative environments. Their revealed preference is to rely on their own stable cooperation rather than attempt to shape others’ behavior via costly prosocial incentives. Thus they appear “reward-averse or reward-indifferent,” favoring self-contained cooperative behavior over explicit positive sanctioning.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite being a consistent high contributor and receiving rewards themselves in some cooperative periods. This strongly indicates that they are not inclined to spend their own resources to positively reinforce others, even when they might appreciate being rewarded. Their non-use of rewards suggests a focus on own-payoff preservation once their contribution decision is made: they are willing to cooperate by contributing, but not to further reduce their payoff through additional prosocial transfers. It also implies they do not attempt to build explicit alliances or reciprocity loops via the reward tool. Their behavior fits a “pure contributor, not a meta-enforcer” profile: cooperation through their own contributions, but no extra-cost involvement in social engineering of others’ behavior, whether via carrots or sticks.

The player never uses the reward option, including in long stretches where everyone cooperates and where positive reinforcement of high contributors could be strategically or normatively natural. This suggests that they do not engage in costly prosocial signaling via rewards and do not attempt to engineer better group outcomes through positive incentives. Given that they receive a sizable reward transfer from someone else after they defect, their continued refusal to reward even then indicates that they are not motivated by gratitude‑based reciprocity or by symmetric “returning favors” logic within the reward mechanism. They accept windfalls but do not mirror the behavior. Taken together with their non‑punishment, their overall sanctioning profile is one of complete passivity: they neither punish nor reward. They rely on their own contribution decisions rather than shaping others’ payoffs through the incentive tools the game provides.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even in long stretches of perfect mutual cooperation. This indicates they are not inclined to spend on positive reinforcement, gratitude, or strategic rewarding to stabilize cooperation. Given that they actively value cooperation in their chat and contribution choices, their non-use of rewards is more plausibly about the perceived inefficiency or personal cost of rewarding, rather than indifference to others’ cooperative behavior. They appear to believe that verbal encouragement plus their own consistent full contributions are sufficient signals, and that paying extra to reward is not worth it. They thus rely on contribution behavior and minimal communication rather than monetary incentives to influence others, behaving as a “non-incentivizer” on both the punishment and reward sides.

The player never uses reward, even when others fully cooperate alongside them. They do not employ positive reinforcement to encourage high contributions, despite repeated cooperative group outcomes. This suggests that, for them, cooperation is expected and does not merit extra incentive payments, or that they regard the reward mechanism as economically inefficient and therefore avoid it. Their behavior is consistent with a neutral stance toward reward rather than a desire to build reciprocal alliances or signal gratitude through the mechanism. They appear to prefer that the payoff structure of the PGG itself do the work of incentivizing cooperation, instead of layering additional, costly rewards on top.

This player frequently uses the reward mechanism and consistently spends some of their payoff to reward others. They are willing to incur real cost to support and reinforce cooperation, even though the reward technology is exactly cost-equal (no multiplier), so the act cannot increase total surplus. This indicates a preference for social reinforcement and possibly gratitude or reciprocity rather than pure efficiency maximization. The pattern suggests they are not purely altruistic toward everyone at all times: they vary the amount they spend across rounds, which likely reflects selective rewarding of perceived cooperators, though exact targets and rules are unknown because reward recipients are not identified. They also receive rewards from others and do not verbally reference them, so there is no explicit gratitude signaling, but their continued cooperation and ongoing use of rewards are consistent with a generalized, indirect reciprocity strategy: support the cooperative norm, reward contributors, and maintain group cohesion. Their willingness to give up coins for equal-value rewards suggests they may value the psychological or normative function of reward—encouraging group-wide cooperation and acknowledging others—over strictly maximizing individual earnings.

They never spend on rewards, despite repeatedly receiving rewards from others and despite clear information on the reward mechanism. This strongly suggests a non-rewarding cooperative type: they contribute fully but do not engage in costly social reinforcement, even when it is normatively encouraged by others’ behavior. They do not appear to reciprocate received rewards, nor do they use rewards to shape others’ contributions, indicating either a strong concern for keeping all residual payoff, skepticism about the value of rewarding when it has no social multiplier, or simple disinterest in that mechanism. Their stance fits a “pure contributor” profile: they generate public benefits through contributions alone while abstaining from extra costly prosocial actions like rewarding.

They make substantial use of the reward mechanism, including in rounds where they themselves contribute nothing. At multiple points, they spend nontrivial amounts on rewards, even when the reward technology is break-even at best, indicating that they are not using reward to increase their own direct material payoff. Instead, they appear willing to sacrifice some earnings to influence or acknowledge others, though the exact targets are unobservable. Their pattern suggests a few possibilities, all with low to moderate confidence: they may be rewarding high contributors to sustain group cooperation that they can free-ride on; they may be selectively rewarding particular players (e.g., for social reasons or perceived deservingness); or they may use rewards in the end-game to redistribute for idiosyncratic reasons unrelated to future incentives. They never use rewards to punish, as punishment is unavailable, and they do not use the absence of punishment to adjust their exploitation of the group. Overall, they are a non-cooperative contributor but an active, sometimes heavy, spender on rewards, implying a type that free-rides on the public good while still valuing the ability to transfer or acknowledge payoffs at personal cost.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, even though they consistently cooperate and repeatedly receive rewards themselves. This indicates that they separate their own contribution norm from any notion of reciprocal reward or social reinforcement. They do not appear to use the reward mechanism instrumentally (to encourage group cooperation) or expressively (to signal gratitude or approval), despite being a beneficiary of others’ rewards. This could reflect a preference to conserve private payoff once the contribution decision is made, a belief that rewards are unnecessary or inefficient, or simple inattention or disinterest in the extra mechanism. Since they never reward anyone, their criteria for rewarding (e.g., reciprocity, conditionality on others’ cooperation, or targeting defectors vs. cooperators) remain unknown.

The player is an active but selective rewarder whose behavior changes across the game. Early on, they invest heavily in rewards, then sharply reduce this spending in the mid-game, and finally ramp it back up at the end. This non-monotonic pattern suggests exploratory, strategic, or mood-driven use of rewards rather than a rigid rule. Because specific targets are unobserved, the exact triggers are unknown, but the timing implies they may initially try to encourage group cooperation, then conserve resources once high contributions appear established, and later use rewards more freely as the game concludes. The player shows clear willingness to sacrifice private payoff to provide rewards, indicating some combination of altruistic preferences, norm reinforcement, or a desire to reciprocate perceived kindness. At one point, they give no rewards despite continued full cooperation, implying they do not feel obligated to constantly reward others and are capable of withholding relational investment while still contributing. Their reward decisions seem decoupled from short-term material self-interest, and instead aligned with loose, evolving intuitions about when positive reinforcement is “worth it,” rather than with precise, outcome-based calculations.

This player never spends any of their own coins to reward others, despite consistently receiving positive rewards from the group. They do not reciprocate reward behavior materially, even after being singled out as a recipient of broad, group-wide rewarding from at least one other cooperator. This pattern suggests that they treat reward as a passive bonus rather than a tool they actively use for norm reinforcement or gratitude signaling. They appear to rely on others to provide incentive structures (cooperation guidance, rewards) rather than initiating such structures themselves. Their non-use of reward is not clearly hostile or exploitative; it may reflect a preference for keeping their own payoff, a lack of attention to or understanding of the strategic purpose of rewards, or simply a low inclination to manage others’ behavior. Overall, their “reward type” is best described as a non-rewarding cooperator: they cooperate strongly once convinced, but they do not invest in pro-social incentives for others, even when they personally benefit from those incentives.

The player is willing to spend substantial resources on rewarding in some situations and completely withholds rewards in others, indicating that they view rewarding as discretionary rather than obligatory. When they do reward, they spend a sizable portion of their available payoff, consistent with a readiness to incur meaningful personal cost to reinforce behavior they see as desirable. However, the exact trigger for rewarding is partially opaque because the specific targets are not visible. The timing suggests that they are more inclined to reward when they may be trying to establish or reinforce cooperative norms early on, and less inclined once widespread cooperation is already established or when they do not see additional benefit from reinforcing it. They exhibit no visible pattern of retaliatory or corrective rewarding (e.g., selectively excluding defectors) from the transcript alone; whether they strategically differentiate between high and low contributors in their reward allocations is unknown. They do not verbally thank others for rewarding them nor signal gratitude through chat, so any reciprocity in their reward behavior is implicit rather than explicitly communicated. Overall, they are a high-cooperation player who treats rewards as a flexible, context-dependent tool rather than as a consistent part of their cooperative identity.

This player is an active and generous user of rewards, willing to spend substantial resources to incentivize and reinforce cooperation. They repeatedly incur nontrivial costs to give rewards to others, indicating that they are not using the reward tool sparingly or only in extreme cases. Their reward behavior is clearly strategic and norm-expressive: they direct rewards broadly to cooperators and explicitly withhold reward from at least one persistent non-contributor, calling attention to that exclusion in chat. This shows they treat rewards both as positive reinforcement and as a soft, non-punitive way to sanction free-riding by omission. They also reward while simultaneously encouraging lagging players to change, suggesting they see rewards as part of a package of persuasion and norm-building. Their willingness to keep rewarding late in the game, and to reward many players at once, indicates low aversion to the personal cost of rewarding and a strong orientation toward shaping a cooperative environment, even when individual material returns from those rewards are uncertain or limited.

This player is a heavy and highly active rewarder, frequently spending large amounts of their payoff to transfer coins to others. They use reward in a distinctly prosocial, not profit-maximizing, way: they sometimes give away so much that their own earnings drop substantially, revealing that reinforcing cooperation and relationships matters more to them than maximizing personal income. Their reward targeting is nuanced rather than purely proportional to contributions. They often give substantial rewards to those who have under-contributed in previous rounds but then increase contributions, consistent with a “rehabilitative” or encouragement approach: they appear to reward positive change or effort rather than only absolute high contribution levels. They also sometimes reward wide swathes of the group, including already high contributors, as if to maintain a broad cooperative climate and express appreciation. They do not use reward strictly as a narrow strategic investment (e.g., only to those most likely to reciprocate materially). Instead, they seem to treat it as a social signal and motivational tool: rewarding to say “I see your effort,” “you’re still part of the group,” or “thank you for cooperating,” even when there is little evidence that specific individuals will reliably pay them back. They are willing to spend extremely high amounts on rewards relative to any reward they personally receive. This identifies them as a strong positive reciprocator and moralist: they behave as though increasing the group’s cooperative atmosphere is intrinsically valuable, even when net costly.

They are highly willing to reward others and do so in a diffuse, inclusive manner. Early and mid-game, they spend substantial amounts of their own payoff to distribute rewards widely, rather than concentrating them on a few top cooperators. They sometimes even reward players who have contributed less than the apparent group norm, indicating that they do not use reward as a strict proportional-pay scheme. Instead, they tend to reward almost everyone who is participating at all, with only limited differentiation by contribution level. This looks more like a prosocial, morale-building or “friendliness-signaling” strategy than a tightly strategic reinforcement of only the highest contributors. They also occasionally suspend rewarding entirely, even when others are highly cooperative, indicating that they do not see reward as an obligatory tit-for-tat rule but as an optional tool. Over time, their use of rewards becomes less frequent, which may reflect learning about its limited impact on behavior or a desire to conserve payoff; this interpretation is tentative. Overall, they act as a generous, broadly affiliative rewarder who spends real resources to make others better off, without using reward in a fine-grained, performance-contingent way.

Rewarding is central to this player’s type. They reward in almost every round, often at very large personal cost, including extreme expenditures that significantly lower or even make their round payoff negative. They show no concern for optimizing their own earnings when choosing reward levels. Triggers for reward are broadly cooperative behavior: they repeatedly distribute rewards widely across many or all peers, typically including both very high contributors and moderate contributors, and at times even those who contribute little. This indicates that rewards are not tightly calibrated to exact contribution levels; instead, they appear to use rewards to maintain goodwill, encourage group cohesion, and perhaps signal appreciation for ongoing participation more generally. At a few points, they concentrate large rewards on a single peer who is consistently cooperative, suggesting willingness to form or signal especially strong support for highly cooperative partners. However, because they also distribute rewards even when some peers have recently under‑contributed, they are not using a strict “reward only the best” strategy; their threshold for “deserving” is low. This player is an active but selective rewarder. They spend substantial amounts on rewards in multiple rounds, accepting a one-for-one cost-to-benefit ratio that does not improve total group surplus, which suggests that the function of reward for them is social or strategic rather than purely efficiency-driven. Reward targets are not limited to the highest contributors; instead, they often include moderate or even relatively low contributors, especially those who are being heavily punished or are at risk of marginalization. This points to a pattern of using reward to support or protect vulnerable players and to counterbalance harsh punishment by others, consistent with a “pro-social, anti-harshness” orientation. They sometimes reward individuals whose contributions are low but appear to be improving, which fits a reinforcement logic: they may hope to encourage upward movement rather than punish past under-contribution. They also reward stable, high contributors in some later interactions, which indicates a desire to recognize and reinforce reliable cooperation. Their reward usage fluctuates; at times they pause rewarding, likely to conserve payoff or because the group’s cooperation level seems self-sustaining. They never combine reward with punishment, underscoring a preference for positive incentives exclusively.

This player also never uses the reward option, even though many others in the group do use rewards to support cooperators and maintain high contributions. They do not spend to reward those who contribute a lot, nor do they reciprocate when they themselves receive rewards. This suggests they give very low weight to positive reciprocity or to maintaining goodwill through prosocial spending. It is unclear whether this comes from self-interest (not wanting to spend any coins for others), skepticism about the effectiveness of rewards, or emotional distance from group outcomes; the data only show that they consistently decline to pay any cost for improving others’ payoffs. They also do not appear to use rewards strategically (e.g., to encourage others to keep contributing high amounts), even though their own payoff would benefit from sustained group cooperation. Overall, they behave as a strictly non-rewarding, non-signaling type.

They never allocate rewards despite frequently contributing at high levels and being surrounded by other high contributors. Their own payoff is sometimes augmented by others’ rewards, but this does not induce reciprocal rewarding. This pattern suggests a preference for negative over positive sanctioning: they rely on punishment to regulate behavior and do not invest in reinforcing prosocial actions. Whether this reflects a belief that rewards are strategically weak (given equal cost and benefit) or a general reluctance to spend on positive reciprocity is unknown. In new environments, they can be expected to enforce norms primarily through costly punishment rather than through reward-based encouragement.

This player is consistently non-rewarding: they never spend any coins to reward others, even when others cooperate fully and even when rewarding could reinforce behavior that benefits them materially. They also maintain this stance despite repeatedly receiving positive rewards from others. Their refusal to engage in rewarding holds both when they cooperate and when they defect, suggesting a stable preference or rule against spending on positive sanctions, not just opportunistic withholding when they are free-riding. This could reflect a strong reluctance to incur any form of second-order cost, a belief that rewards are unnecessary or ineffective, or a narrow payoff-maximizing stance regarding the reward mechanism specifically. However, their own cooperative choices are not purely self-interested, so their non-rewarding stance is not simply “pure selfishness.” Instead, they look like a “non-sanctioning cooperator”: willing to contribute to the public good at times, but unwilling to invest further resources into metacooperative acts such as rewarding others, regardless of how fair or generous the group is toward them. Their reward behavior shows no clear gratitude signaling, no tit-for-tat reciprocity via rewards, and no strategic attempt to guide others’ behavior with incentives.

This player is willing to incur nontrivial personal costs to provide rewards, at least early on. They use the reward mechanism actively when mutual high contribution is observed, indicating an inclination to reinforce cooperative behavior. Their rewards appear selective: they do not reward uniformly across all others in every cooperative configuration, and they disengage from rewarding at times even when cooperation is present. This suggests that they treat rewards as a strategic tool rather than a fixed moral obligation. They seem capable of both generous and restrained reward behavior. Initially they spend significantly on rewards, which aligns with a desire to acknowledge or incentivize cooperation. Later, even when cooperation returns, they sometimes refrain from rewarding, indicating either learning about the limited necessity or profitability of rewarding, or a shift toward conserving payoff. They do not use rewards to “correct” a free-rider; there is no evidence of rewarding a low contributor to encourage improvement, pointing to a preference for rewarding clear cooperators rather than using rewards as a rehabilitation device. As the game progresses and especially toward the end, they stop rewarding altogether, consistent with recognition that future benefits from reinforcing norms are no longer available.

This player occasionally uses the reward mechanism but sparingly and in small amounts. They are willing to pay a cost to reward others, which signals some concern for reinforcing cooperative behavior or expressing approval, but they do not do so systematically in every cooperative context. Their threshold for rewarding seems selective; they do not indiscriminately reward all cooperators every time. They continue to reward even in environments where others are not uniformly cooperative, indicating that their use of rewards is not strictly conditional on everyone behaving “perfectly.” They never escalate to heavy or repeated rewarding, suggesting they are cost-conscious about rewards or uncertain about their impact. There is no direct evidence of them using rewards in a clearly strategic, forward-looking way versus an expressive or gratitude-based way; their underlying motive is unknown, but they are clearly not a non-rewarder type. They also never use the final round to send a last, large reward signal; instead, their last-round behavior is reward-neutral.

The player is a **selective but at times heavy user of rewards**, especially in early and middle stages. They demonstrate willingness to incur substantial cost to provide others with higher payoffs when broad cooperation is present. This indicates that they value: - Reinforcing cooperative norms. - Encouraging or sustaining group-wide full contribution patterns. - Possibly reciprocating perceived prosocial behavior. When the group is fully cooperative, the player spends nontrivial resources on rewards. This is consistent with an **efficiency-enhancing rewarder**: they are willing to use the positive incentive mechanism to stabilize or signal support for mutual cooperation, despite the personal cost. Their reward behavior, however, is **sensitive to context and changes over time**: - When one peer free-rides but cooperation from others continues, the player does not resort to punishment (not available in this design) and does not consistently use rewards to “correct” that deviation; instead, their own reward spending varies and sometimes drops to zero even while continuing to contribute fully. - In later cooperative rounds, they sometimes withhold rewards entirely despite others maintaining full contributions. This suggests that they do not treat reward as a strict obligation; they may feel that once cooperation is stable, extra spending on incentives is no longer necessary or cost-effective. The player does not appear to use reward in a **strictly tit-for-tat** or one-to-one reciprocal manner. Their own receipt of rewards from others does not systematically dictate whether they reward back; at times they reward heavily even when they themselves are not especially favored, and at other times they abstain from rewarding while still being rewarded. This suggests that their reward use is **group-level and strategic** rather than purely dyadic. In the final round, they **do not reward at all**, consistent with horizon-aware payoff maximization; without future rounds, there is no strategic reason to invest in others’ outcomes. This further reinforces that their reward behavior is forward-looking, aimed at shaping **future cooperation**, not purely altruistic giving. Overall, the player is an **early- and mid-game prosocial incentivizer**: they are willing to pay for rewards to sustain cooperation when it matters for future rounds, but they are not rigidly committed to always rewarding cooperators, and they fully shut down reward use at the known end.

They also never use the reward mechanism, despite sustained and often high cooperation by their peers and despite occasionally receiving rewards themselves. This indicates absence of costly-positive reciprocity: even when others’ contributions and rewards benefit them directly, they do not respond with reinforcing rewards. Reward is not used strategically to encourage ongoing cooperation or to thank cooperators. The player appears purely payoff-oriented with respect to the endowment and treats both punishment and reward tools as dominated options that only reduce their own earnings. As with punishment, there is no evidence that they try to shape group behavior through positive incentives; they passively accept benefits provided by others without contributing to the incentive structure.

Their reward behavior is present but restrained and more exploratory than systematic. They sometimes reward higher contributors, and they never reward the persistent free‑rider. When they do reward, it is targeted at peers who are contributing at or above the emerging group norm, suggesting they understand rewards as a way to recognize and reinforce high cooperation. However, they do not reward consistently. Even when others contribute very generously, this player often withholds rewards, especially later. This asymmetry—relatively frequent, persistent punishment of defectors versus occasional, episodic rewarding of cooperators—implies that negative sanctions are their primary tool for norm enforcement, while rewards are secondary and somewhat discretionary. Their willingness to both punish and reward the same cooperator in different periods (rewarding in some cooperative contexts, not rewarding in others) suggests they are not operating with a strict tit‑for‑tat reinforcement rule. Instead, they appear to be weighing the cost of reward, their own payoff needs, and perhaps an early desire to signal appreciation or encouragement. Once cooperative behavior among some peers appears stable, they largely cease using rewards, likely because they no longer see them as necessary to maintain contributions or as worth the cost. In motivational terms, this is a “selective positive reinforcement” style: they appreciate and sometimes support cooperators but rely much more heavily on punishing defectors than on subsidizing contributors. Their gratitude is behaviorally expressed but not systematically maintained, and they do not use rewards as a primary driver of group dynamics.

The player is willing to use reward as a costly, positive reinforcement tool, but in a nuanced and adaptive way. They reward others only when they see full contributions from those players and never reward defectors in the same period. Early on, they withhold both reward and punishment when everyone cooperates, behaving as a “quiet cooperator” who does not automatically subsidize others. Once someone has previously defected and then returns to full contribution, the player uses small, targeted rewards toward that individual, consistent with signaling approval and reinforcing improved behavior. At times, they also reward everyone in the fully contributing group, which resembles a prosocial move to support and recognize a cooperative climate even though it is individually costly and only efficiency-neutral (reward tech has no net social gain). Later, they settle into selectively rewarding particular full contributors, especially those who have either previously defected and improved or those who remain consistently cooperative while someone else defects. This pattern suggests a strategic use of reward to communicate norms, show gratitude for cooperation, and possibly stabilize alliances with other apparent cooperators. They nevertheless often abstain from rewarding even when all cooperate, indicating they are sensitive to the cost of reward and do not treat it as obligatory.

The player has a strong, persistent tendency to reward others. They use reward in many rounds, often at nontrivial personal cost, even when rewards are not efficiency-enhancing in terms of payoff technology. This implies an intrinsic preference for encouraging or acknowledging perceived good behavior rather than pure payoff maximization. Their reward pattern is not mechanical: they selectively reward some consistent high contributors more than others. They often favor one particular partner, sometimes also including another, and occasionally extend rewards even to players who have just defected. This indicates that they may be using rewards both to reinforce cooperation and to build or maintain relational bonds with specific individuals. They sometimes reward a low contributor immediately after that person deviates or after a mixed signal (e.g., punishing them once and rewarding another), which is consistent with a “carrot-and-stick” mindset: they differentiate between individuals, sometimes punishing one and rewarding another in the same period to make their approval/disapproval structure clearer. Overall, they are a generous and persistent rewarder, more socially-oriented than payoff-oriented.

This player is quite willing to spend on reward, even though the reward technology is break-even and not payoff-enhancing. They use rewards more frequently than punishment, indicating a prosocial or relationship-building orientation. Rewards are typically directed toward high contributors and are withheld when a peer fails to contribute, so the player uses positive reinforcement to encourage cooperative behavior. They sometimes reward selected cooperating peers rather than everyone, suggesting they may be differentiating between especially salient or consistently cooperative partners, or experimenting with alliances. At other times they reward all cooperators symmetrically, which is consistent with a fairness-based or gratitude-based norm: “everyone who does their part deserves recognition.” They continue to reward some peers even after having been punished, which suggests they are not using reward primarily as a retaliatory or exclusion tool. Instead, they appear to use reward to maintain overall cooperation and possibly to express appreciation or signal goodwill. They do not obviously tie reward decisions to short-run personal gain, as they reward even when doing so reduces their own payoff and when the cooperative norm is already strong. Their reward strategy is thus best characterized as expressive and norm-reinforcing rather than narrowly instrumental.

They never use the reward option, despite being repeatedly rewarded themselves and seeing that others are willing to pay the cost to transfer coins to them. This indicates that even positive sanctioning is not part of their strategy. They do not engage in mutual reinforcement or gratitude signaling through reward, either toward consistent cooperators or toward those who match their contributions. Given that reward in this setting is exactly cost-neutral (each unit costs them as much as it gives to the recipient), their refusal to use it suggests either a high emphasis on conserving their own coins, a lack of interest in active norm enforcement, or uncertainty about the strategic value of reward. They rely entirely on their own contribution behavior to sustain cooperation and do not attempt to create alliances or encourage others via costly positive signals.

This player never spends on rewards, despite repeatedly being in groups where others fully cooperate and despite receiving rewards themselves. They do not use rewards either as direct gratitude, as a way to reinforce high contributors, or as a strategic investment in future cooperation. This is not because they are averse to all costly social actions (they do punish), but rather because they appear to judge punishment as more effective or more worth the cost than rewarding, or they may see rewarding as unnecessary when cooperation is already strong. Given that rewards here are payoff-neutral to the giver and receiver (cost and benefit are equal), the lack of rewarding suggests they do not value the expressive or relational signaling function of positive incentives. They treat the game primarily as a norm-enforcement setting rather than a relationship-building or gratitude-signaling environment. Their type is thus: “punish-to-enforce, not reward-to-encourage.”

The player never uses the reward mechanism, despite having multiple opportunities and despite the presence of cooperative peers. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost to positively reinforce others, even when others’ behavior could be perceived as helpful or generous. Their non-use of rewards alongside their willingness to punish cooperators implies they do not seek to build alliances through positive incentives or signal gratitude. They do not appear to experiment with rewards even once, so learning-by-doing or curiosity about prosocial reinforcement is absent. Whether they avoid rewards because of perceived inefficiency, lack of interest in others’ outcomes, or confusion about the mechanism is unknown.

The player never uses the reward option, despite the presence of consistently high contributors. They do not spend to positively reinforce cooperation or to express gratitude. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for social signaling or for supporting group-beneficial behavior through rewards. Given that they themselves move into a cooperative pattern, their non-use of reward suggests they expect cooperation to be its own justification and do not see added value in explicit positive reinforcement under these parameters. It may also indicate that they perceive the reward technology as inefficient or not worth the cost, but this is speculative. Behaviorally, they are a “non-rewarder”: they cooperate directly but do not invest in meta-strategies that shape others’ behavior via rewards.

This player consistently refrains from using the reward option, even when others contribute fully and when reward could, in principle, reinforce high cooperation. They exhibit no positive reciprocity via the formal reward mechanism and appear insensitive to opportunities for costly gratitude or bonus incentives. This suggests they either see the reward technology as inefficient, unnecessary, or strategically dominated compared to punishment, or they simply prioritize enforcing minimum standards over recognizing extra-good behavior. Their social preference profile, based on observed choices, is asymmetrically focused on sanctioning defectors rather than supporting cooperators. There is no indication that they use rewards to build alliances, to signal appreciation, or to try to lock in a cooperative equilibrium. Whether they would reward in environments where it is cheaper or more effective is unknown, but in the given context their type behaves as a “non-rewarding enforcer,” relying exclusively on negative incentives to uphold cooperative norms.

The player never uses rewards, despite repeated access to the mechanism and exposure to both cooperative and non-cooperative peers. This consistent non-use suggests they either discount the value of positive incentives or view them as inefficient given the unfavorable cost-benefit ratio of the reward technology. They appear unwilling to sacrifice personal payoff to benefit others, even when those others behave cooperatively. Their sanctioning style is thus one-sided: they rely exclusively on negative incentives and do not engage in gratitude signaling, alliance-building, or reinforcement of high contributors through rewards. This implies a norm system oriented around punishment for perceived wrongs rather than recognition of good deeds. Strategically, they may believe that punishment alone is sufficient to shape behavior, or that rewards are too weak or too costly to matter. In terms of personality inference, they present as critical rather than appreciative: quick to impose costs on selected others, and consistently uninterested in applying resources to make cooperative peers better off. Whether this reflects a general preference for negative over positive control, skepticism about others’ responsiveness to rewards, or a simple payoff-maximizing dislike of “wasting” coins on others is unknown.

The player never uses the reward option, even toward consistently high contributors or when group cooperation improves. This abstention holds despite the presence of multiple peers who match their cooperative pattern, indicating that they do not engage in positive reciprocity via the formal rewarding mechanism. Given that rewards are relatively inefficient (costlier to give than to receive), their nonuse may reflect a sensitivity to the unfavorable exchange rate, a general reluctance to spend on others beyond their public-good contribution, or a belief that contributions themselves are sufficient signals and do not need further reinforcement. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling or strategic use of rewards to stabilize cooperation; they rely solely on their own contribution behavior rather than shaping others’ behavior through incentives. Whether they might reward under more favorable reward technology or in a different institutional setting is unknown.

The player never uses reward, even when facing other consistent high contributors. This suggests a reluctance to spend additional resources to reinforce cooperation, despite being strongly cooperative themselves. Possible interpretations include: they view their own contribution as sufficient pro-social behavior; they find the cost–benefit trade-off of reward unattractive; or they pay limited strategic attention to the incentive mechanism. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling or attempts to differentially encourage high contributors versus low contributors. In combination with their non-punishing stance, this points to a “contribute but don’t intervene” profile: they support the public good directly through their own behavior but do not attempt to manage others’ behavior via rewards or sanctions.

They also never use costly rewards, even when many others cooperate strongly. This points to a preference for conserving resources over second-order pro-sociality via direct incentives. Given the relatively poor efficiency of the reward mechanism in this environment, their avoidance may reflect a rational cost-benefit assessment: they support cooperation by modeling and verbally endorsing full contributions, not by subsidizing others. Their strategy for norm reinforcement is thus non-monetary: they share simple guidance and personally cooperate at the maximum level, but they do not engage in explicit payoff transfers. There is no observable tendency toward gratitude signaling or selective rewarding of especially cooperative peers. Instead, they appear to rely on group-level understanding and imitation rather than formal incentive structures.

The player also never uses costly rewards, even when others contribute at very high or maximal levels. This suggests they rely on mutual high contribution as its own reward rather than spending personal resources to bolster or thank cooperators. They may see the reward mechanism as too inefficient or unnecessary when group cooperation is already strong, and they prefer to preserve their own payoff rather than subsidize others further. Their cooperative reinforcement is expressed through verbal commitment and matching behavior, not through formal positive incentives. Whether they would reward in longer or more uncertain settings is unknown, but in this context they appear to be a non-rewarding conditional cooperator who supports cooperation mainly by aligning their own contributions and using light, low-cost communication.

The player never uses the reward option despite its availability in both stages and the presence of many highly cooperative co-players. Even when others clearly embrace a strong cooperation norm in both talk and behavior, the player refrains from positive reinforcement. This indicates a strong asymmetry: they are willing to pay to harm others but not to help them. It suggests a preference structure in which supporting cooperators is not valued enough to justify even a small personal cost, while punitive actions are seen as worthwhile. They do not engage in gratitude signaling or strategic rewarding to shape future behavior, even in situations where such behavior could, in principle, support high group returns. Overall, they fit a “non-rewarding, punishment-focused” profile: they rely exclusively on negative sanctions and do not invest in reputational or relational capital through rewarding.

They also never use rewards despite the presence of very high contributors. Their cooperative expression is through direct contribution, not through second-order incentives. The absence of selective rewarding suggests they are not focused on strategic reinforcement, reputation-building, or signaling gratitude via the formal mechanism. If they value others’ high cooperation, they do so silently and without material acknowledgment.

They also never use reward, even when many group members contribute at high or maximal levels. Given that the reward technology is inefficient, their abstention may reflect instrumental reasoning: they might see direct contribution as a better use of resources than costly transfers that benefit others less than they cost the giver. It may also indicate a general reluctance to engage in second-order interventions (whether positive or negative), preferring to express support through own contribution rather than targeted reinforcement. There is no visible attempt to selectively encourage borderline cooperators or to express gratitude to high contributors via rewards. Because they neither reward nor punish, their type looks like a “pure contributor” rather than a “social engineer”: they adjust their own behavior to norms but do not invest in shaping others’ incentives. Whether they would use reward in an environment where it is more efficient or more clearly signaled is unknown.

They also avoid using the reward mechanism, despite the presence of many high contributors. Alternatively, they may assume that mutual high contributions and the game’s redistribution are sufficient recognition, and additional material rewards are unnecessary. Their main way of reinforcing cooperation is through their own contribution choice and cooperative communication, not through explicit monetary rewards. Their willingness to engage in strategic reinforcement beyond that—through targeted rewards—is low or absent in this context.

They never use the reward option, even when surrounded by many very high contributors and a strong cooperative consensus. They also never use the reward mechanism, even in the presence of many high contributors. They do not appear to engage in costly gratitude signaling or strategic rewarding to stabilize cooperation. They also never use the reward option, despite the presence of many highly cooperative peers. They never use the reward option, even in an environment where many others match their high contributions and where public acknowledgment via rewards could, in principle, reinforce cooperation. The player does not use rewards, even when others behave cooperatively and when they themselves benefit from group cooperation. They also entirely avoid using reward, even when many peers contribute generously and move toward a nearly universal high-contribution norm. The player does not use the reward mechanism at any point, even though many others behave in clearly cooperative ways that could invite positive reinforcement. They are highly willing to spend on rewards, even though the reward technology is inefficient. The player never uses the reward option, even when surrounded by high contributions. They never purchase rewards for anyone, including themselves or others, despite repeatedly seeing that others sometimes reward and that the mechanism is symmetric in cost and benefit. The player is a frequent and often heavy user of the reward mechanism. For much of the game, they spend a substantial portion of their payoff on rewarding others, including in early rounds where there is no prior interaction record, and again in the late game. They are clearly willing to incur personal cost to reinforce cooperation. This pattern is consistent with an “altruistic enforcer” or “pro‑social investor” type who uses rewards as a standing tool to support cooperative norms rather than as a precisely calibrated, tit‑for‑tat signal. What is clear is that they are comfortable spending significantly on reward even when they themselves are not especially rewarded in that same phase, implying that they are not using reward only as direct reciprocity for what they personally receive.

The player is a frequent and sometimes heavy user of the reward mechanism, even though its direct material effect is neutral in this design. The player uses the reward mechanism actively and repeatedly, at a non-trivial cost, which indicates a clear willingness to invest resources into social reinforcement.
