The player is an active user of the reward mechanism and is willing to spend a meaningful share of their payoff to reward others. They do not reward in every round, but when they do, they sometimes invest substantially rather than symbolically. Their reward behavior is selective and appears contingent on others’ cooperation level: they never reward clear noncontributors, and they are most willing to reward when the group’s contributions are high and stable. They treat reward as a positive reinforcement tool rather than a pure exchange: at times they reward even though they do not receive much or any reward in return, indicating they are not narrowly transactional. However, their reward intensity fluctuates: in some stretches they stop rewarding despite continued high contributions around them, suggesting either that they believe norms are already established and no longer need reinforcement, or that they are pacing their spending to manage costs. Late in the game, they substantially increase their reward spending, consistent with a strategic “last push” to recognize or strengthen cooperators when there is no longer a need to conserve resources for future rounds. Overall, they appear willing to pay to sustain or encourage a cooperative environment, but they do not reward mechanically every time others cooperate; their rewards are intermittent, non-random, and plausibly targeted.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even though they receive rewards themselves. This holds across varying group contribution patterns and changing amounts of reward coming to them. They do not differentially reward high contributors or withhold reward from free-riders; they simply abstain entirely from rewarding. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal resources on direct peer-to-peer incentives, even when contributions from others are high and reward would be normatively or strategically justifiable. It is unclear whether this is driven by narrow payoff-maximization (keeping all residual coins), a general aversion to intervention in others’ payoffs, confusion or disinterest regarding the reward tool, or a belief that the group already coordinates sufficiently without additional incentives. Since they consistently invest fully in the public good but never pay to reward, their cooperative orientation does not extend to costly second-order cooperation via rewarding. They behave as a pure contributor rather than an enforcer or norm “sustainer” through rewards.

This player is an active and generally generous rewarder, often spending a meaningful portion of their available coins to reward others. They almost always make use of the reward mechanism when they are doing so during the middle of the game, indicating a strong willingness to positively sanction behavior they deem desirable. Given that they themselves always fully contribute, and that many others also contribute fully, the most plausible trigger for their rewarding is high contribution, i.e., they reward cooperators rather than defectors. Their reward use varies in intensity over rounds—from periods of moderate spending to occasional very high spending and brief periods of no spending—suggesting that they adjust reward allocations based on evolving impressions of who is cooperating or on how grateful they feel toward particular partners. They do not use reward to gain a material edge, since the technology makes rewards cost‑equivalent, and they sometimes sacrifice substantial immediate payoff to provide them; this behavior is consistent with a pro‑social, norm‑enforcing type rather than a strictly strategic investor. They also appear willing to temporarily reduce their reward spending, possibly to observe whether strong cooperation persists without constant reinforcement, then resume rewarding when they feel it is warranted. Overall, they behave like a supportive, sometimes intense “rewarding cooperator” who sees positive sanctioning as part of sustaining a cooperative environment.

This player is a heavy and selective user of rewards. They are clearly willing to incur substantial personal costs to reward others, and they do so in multiple, distinct phases rather than just once. Their willingness to spend on rewards is not constant; it occurs in bursts, suggesting targeted strategic or normative motives rather than a flat rule. The triggers for rewarding appear linked to peers’ cooperative behavior: they remain a consistent high contributor and then periodically spend heavily to reward, likely focusing on those who also contribute fully or reliably. They do not appear to use rewards to punish or “correct” defectors (which is not available in the mechanism anyway); instead, their pattern is more consistent with positive reinforcement of cooperation. The fact that they sometimes reward nothing even while others are contributing suggests they are not simply rewarding every cooperator each round, but rather timing and concentrating rewards, possibly to highlight especially valuable cooperative partners or to manage their own cumulative payoff. Near the end of the game, they significantly increase their reward spending, consistent with a desire to provide a final, strong cooperative signal or to reciprocate accumulated goodwill. Overall, they behave as a generosity-oriented, norm-enforcing rewarder who uses rewards to stabilize or acknowledge cooperation rather than to directly improve their own monetary payoff.

The player is an active, sometimes heavy, user of the reward mechanism. They are willing to spend substantial portions of their payoff on rewarding others, and this willingness persists throughout the game rather than being confined to early “investment” rounds. They clearly use reward as a deliberate tool rather than as an occasional impulse. The reward pattern strongly suggests targeted reinforcement of cooperative behavior: they continue to reward in rounds where most others contribute fully, and they reduce or suspend rewarding when collective contributions and received rewards dip, then resume when cooperative norms strengthen again. This indicates that they aim to bolster and stabilize a cooperative culture rather than simply reciprocate one-off favors. They appear relatively unconcerned with the fact that the reward technology is payoff-neutral at the individual dyadic level (cost equals benefit). They still invest real resources in rewarding, which points to non-monetary objectives such as sustaining group cooperation, expressing approval, or building reputational capital. Their behavior fits a “prosocial incentivizer” type: a player who uses institutional tools to support cooperation even when short-run private returns are limited.

The player is a frequent and sometimes heavy user of the reward mechanism. They are clearly willing to sacrifice payoff to reward others, and they do so in many rounds across the entire game. Because reward is cost-neutral at the margin in this setup (the cost per unit equals the benefit per unit), their use of reward still reflects an active choice: they could keep everything but instead deliberately reallocate some tokens to others. They tend to reward more in periods where overall cooperation is high, and they do not systematically withdraw all rewarding in response to the presence of a few non-contributors. This suggests they use reward mainly to reinforce cooperative behavior rather than to punish by omission. They occasionally reduce reward spending for short stretches but later increase it again, which looks more like adaptive fine-tuning than abandonment of rewarding as a tool. Their willingness to continue spending on reward even when they are already a strong cooperator indicates that they value recognizing or encouraging others’ cooperation, not only signaling their own type. Their reward behavior appears group-directed rather than narrowly transactional, though the exact targeting strategy (e.g., only rewarding full contributors vs. also rewarding partial/non-contributors) is unknown from the aggregate data.

For most of the game, this player completely refrains from spending on rewards. They receive rewards from others multiple times yet do not use the reward mechanism in a reciprocal or strategic way. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur direct costs to influence others’ behavior via positive incentives. Late in the game, they suddenly make a single, very large investment in rewarding others, spending heavily in one instance while still contributing fully. Because the reward technology has no multiplier advantage and costs them as much as it benefits the recipients, this move is not payoff-maximizing for them. It instead suggests a momentary choice to recognize or strengthen cooperation, possibly a delayed expression of gratitude or a normative gesture toward high contributors. However, this intense reward episode is not repeated, and before and after it they revert to not rewarding at all. This points to a generally reward-averse or at least highly restrained rewarding style, punctuated by one exceptional bout of generosity. They do not appear to use rewards systematically to shape others’ contributions; if anything, the big reward move is symbolic or affect-driven rather than part of a consistent strategy. Overall, they are a stable cooperator who rarely rewards, with only one conspicuous but isolated reward spree.

The player has a strong and consistent tendency to reward cooperative behavior. They repeatedly reward peers who contribute fully and withhold rewards from the persistent free-rider. When only some peers contribute, they direct rewards exclusively to contributors; when all others free-ride, they give no rewards at all. This indicates a clear, consistent rule: “I reward those who contribute and not those who don’t.” They use reward more often than punishment and are willing to spend a nontrivial portion of their payoffs doing so, even after enduring repeated punishment themselves. This suggests they value reinforcing cooperation more than maximizing immediate earnings. There are periods where they refrain from using rewards, indicating they are not mechanically rewarding every time; they may be balancing costs or testing whether cooperation persists without reinforcement. They never appear to reward strategically to curry favor with defectors or to buy leniency; rewards are never directed toward the free-rider. Instead, they selectively reinforce partners who already cooperate, consistent with a prosocial, reciprocity-focused orientation. They also continue to reward cooperative peers even after being punished, implying that their rewarding rule is stable and not easily derailed by personal grievances.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite multiple opportunities, and despite repeatedly observing stable high contributions from some group members. Given that reward is relatively efficient (each unit is a net gain for the recipient), their consistent non-use suggests a strong preference against positive reinforcement as a tool. This could reflect one or more of the following, all low confidence: a belief that rewarding is unnecessary because high contribution is the “default” expected behavior; a preference for punishing norm violations over rewarding norm adherence; or a desire to conserve coins for own payoff or for occasional punishment. However, none of these can be confirmed directly—only inferred from their complete abstention from rewarding. They also accept rewards from others when offered, but do not reciprocate. This absence of reciprocal rewarding, even when they themselves are high contributors and benefit from others’ generosity, suggests that gratitude signaling through rewards is not part of their behavioral repertoire in this setting. Overall, their reward “type” is: pure non-rewarder—relies exclusively on punishment and contribution choices for social control and expression, and does not allocate resources to positive incentives under any observed circumstances.

This player never uses the reward option, even though many others contribute generously and would be natural candidates for positive reinforcement. They do not spend resources to express gratitude, build alliances, or encourage high contributors. Given that the reward technology is individually cost-neutral, their non-use suggests either inattention to the mechanism, a reluctance to engage in any targeted interventions (positive or negative), or a belief that contributions themselves are sufficient signals. There is no evidence they use rewards strategically to steer behavior or to reciprocate kindness. Overall, their enforcement style is completely passive: they neither punish defectors nor reward cooperators.

They never use the reward mechanism despite repeated opportunities. Even high contributors who match their own cooperative behavior do not receive rewards from them. This indicates a strong preference for negative over positive reinforcement, or a belief that rewarding is not cost-effective or necessary. Given the symmetric cost-benefit ratio of reward (one-to-one in cost and benefit) and their heavy use of punishment, it appears they value deterrence and retribution more than encouragement. They may also implicitly assume that high contribution is the expected default, not something to be positively reinforced. There is no evidence of strategic alliance formation through reward.

They never use the reward option, despite consistently observing high contributions from many others and small instances of reward within the group. This is consistent with a strategy that does not invest in positive reciprocity or norm reinforcement. Given that rewarding is cost-neutral in expectation in this design, their refusal to reward suggests either a general unwillingness to expend effort or resources for others’ benefit or a lack of interest in building alliances or goodwill. There is no indication that they consider using rewards to soften others’ hostility toward them or to encourage more favorable treatment. Their non-use across all situations indicates a stable, non-rewarding type: they neither punish nor reward, treating the sanction system as irrelevant to their own strategy aside from bearing its costs.

They never use reward, despite having multiple opportunities and numerous consistently high contributors around them. This indicates a clear preference for negative over positive incentives. Reasons are uncertain: they might see reward as inefficient given the one‑to‑one cost/benefit, as unnecessary when high contribution is simply the expected norm, or as less salient than punishment in shaping behavior. Their strategy is thus asymmetric: they enforce norms through costly punishment but do not invest in reinforcing good behavior via reward.

They almost never use the reward mechanism and display no interest in positive reinforcement, despite many peers fully cooperating. This indicates a clear preference for negative sanctions over prosocial rewards as a tool for shaping behavior. The fact that rewarding is payoff‑neutral in expectation yet is still avoided suggests that their focus is not on efficiency but on expressing disapproval and enforcing norms through punishment. They do not appear to engage in alliance‑building or gratitude signaling via rewards; their cooperation with other high contributors is not complemented by positive transfers.

The player never rewards anyone, even though many group members match or exceed their high contributions. This persistent non-use of rewarding tools suggests they are not motivated to engage in second-order cooperation via positive incentives. They neither selectively reward high cooperators nor attempt to build alliances or reinforce norms through gifts. They do not show curiosity about how to use rewards, nor any discussion of them, so their underlying reasoning is unknown. They might perceive rewards as wasteful because they are one-to-one in cost and benefit, or they may simply prefer a minimalist strategy focused purely on contributing. Their approach reflects a pure contributor who cooperates directly but does not invest in metacooperative mechanisms.

They never use the reward option, even though many others contribute highly and maintain cooperation. They do not reward high contributors, consistent cooperators, or fellow non-punishers. This suggests that they avoid spending on social tools altogether, not only on sanctions. Possible interpretations include: (a) a focus on preserving their redistributed payoff by not sacrificing coins for influence, (b) a belief that reward is unnecessary or ineffective, or (c) an overall disengagement from second-order cooperation (supporting others who cooperate). Because they systematically ignore both punishment and reward tools, their type is best described as “pure first-order cooperator” with no observed interest in active social reinforcement.

The player never uses the reward option, despite being a high contributor in a group where many others also contribute highly and despite being rewarded once themselves. This suggests that they either find the reward mechanism strategically weak (given its 1:1 efficiency) or they are not motivated to spend personal resources on positive reinforcement. Their cooperation seems to be enforced through expectations and punishment rather than via active encouragement. It is unknown whether they would reward in a different environment with cheaper or more effective rewards; in this setting, they reveal a clear preference for saving their own payoff over costly gratitude or prosocial bonuses.

They use rewards, but sparingly and in a somewhat idiosyncratic way. They reward both clear cooperators and at least one persistent low contributor, so their reward is not simply “pay high contributors.” This suggests they may use rewards to signal favor, sympathy, or alliance rather than to strictly reinforce pro-social contributions. They are willing to pay non-trivial amounts to reward multiple players in some periods, even though the reward returns no net gain under the given technology. This again demonstrates low payoff focus and a readiness to spend on social expression. Their choice to reward a known free-rider while punishing strong contributors indicates they may value contrarian or anti-majority players, or they may be reacting to earlier social treatment rather than to raw contribution levels. Rewarding and punishing in the same periods suggests they treat the two tools as complementary levers for expressing differential approval rather than using reward as a substitute for punishment.

They never use the reward option, even though many players contribute highly and some explicitly try to enforce cooperative norms. Given that rewarding here is cost-neutral in net (it costs as much as it transfers), their failure to reward suggests either disinterest in targeted positive reinforcement, a general preference to avoid all sanctioning activity, or lack of attention to/valuation of the mechanism. They do not selectively favor high contributors; their cooperative stance is expressed only through their own contribution, not through meta-level incentives.

They never use the reward option, despite having multiple opportunities and being surrounded by many high contributors. This is not obviously due to cost‑ineffectiveness alone, since they are willing to incur substantial costs for punishment. Their revealed preference is to rely solely on negative incentives. They act as if under‑contribution deserves costly correction, while high contribution is simply expected baseline behavior that does not merit extra payment. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to build coalitions or to reinforce high contributors; either they undervalue positive reinforcement or they find it unnecessary or inefficient for enforcing norms compared with high‑leverage punishment.

The player is minimally willing to use reward and does so sparingly. They deploy reward once, toward a highly cooperative partner who also appears engaged in norm enforcement. This indicates that they conceptually support positive reinforcement of “good citizens,” but they are not a systematic rewarder. Given the low efficiency of the reward mechanism relative to punishment, their limited use may reflect a pragmatic focus on impactful actions rather than broad generosity through the reward channel. After that single instance, they do not continue rewarding, even when cooperation levels remain high, suggesting that reward is a secondary tool for them, used mainly to signal approval of a salient ally-like behavior rather than as an ongoing incentive scheme.

They never use the reward option, despite being aware of it. There is no indication that they consider positive reinforcement as a tool, either strategically or expressively. Given their aversion to punishment and low engagement with the mechanism overall, it is plausible (though uncertain) that they see the redistribution dynamics as sufficient and do not feel a need to spend personal resources to shape others’ behavior through rewards. Their underlying attitude toward rewarding others is unknown beyond the absence of action.

They never use the reward option, despite being strongly prosocial in contribution behavior. Given that rewarding is exactly cost-neutral in this mechanism, their refusal to reward cannot be explained by simple payoff maximization alone. Possible interpretations include: they see punishment as the primary and sufficient enforcement tool; they do not think reward is necessary when high contributions are already the norm; or they simply focus their limited attention on punishing deviations rather than reinforcing good behavior. They do not experiment with reward even when others are highly cooperative, suggesting a stable preference structure where sanctioning bad behavior is more salient than actively subsidizing good behavior.

This player never spends any of their own coins to reward others, even though they themselves receive rewards in multiple rounds. They do not reciprocate rewards, do not selectively reward high contributors, and do not attempt to use rewards strategically to shape group behavior. Their reward behavior is entirely inactive: they ignore this mechanism throughout. This reveals several plausible features of their type: - They prioritize preserving their own payoff once contributions are made, treating additional costly actions as unnecessary, even though they are willing to sacrifice their endowment to the public good. That is, they are altruistic via contributions, but not via targeted, costly rewards. - They do not use rewards to express gratitude or enforce norms, despite regularly cooperating. This suggests that they either (a) do not care about interpersonal signaling in this environment, (b) do not notice or do not deem the reward mechanism important, or (c) adopt a minimalist strategy: contribute fully and ignore all secondary mechanisms. Which is true is unknown. - They do not attempt to solve free-rider or coordination problems through rewards, even when the group’s cooperation level drops. They show no sign of strategic reinforcement: they neither reward consistent cooperators nor attempt to revive cooperation after downturns. Their combination of full contribution and zero rewarding implies a simple, one-dimensional strategy: cooperate through the primary channel, but avoid spending any extra resources on secondary tools, even those designed to support cooperation. This is consistent with a “pure public-good contributor who does not engage in metagame incentives” type.

This player is an active, targeted rewarder who uses the reward mechanism regularly and at nontrivial cost. They are willing to spend a meaningful portion of their payoff on rewards across many stages, including in the middle and later parts of the game, which indicates that prosocial or strategic reinforcing motives matter to them beyond immediate earnings. They neither ignore the reward option nor apply it uniformly. Their spending on rewards varies over time, from zero in some rounds to substantial investment in others. This variability suggests that they condition reward decisions on what they infer about others’ behaviors and the group’s cooperative state. When the group appears strongly cooperative, they are often more willing to pay to reward; when cooperation is adequate but perhaps fragile, they sometimes use rewards to bolster it; in some cooperative rounds they nevertheless withhold rewards, implying that they are selective rather than mechanically generous. Because identities of rewarders are hidden and they cannot see others’ final payoffs, their use of rewards likely serves two functions: (1) encouraging continued contributions by reciprocating to perceived cooperators, and (2) fine‑tuning their own payoff structure when they believe the marginal cost of rewarding is acceptable relative to the expected stability or improvement of group cooperation. There is no evidence that they try to punish via withholding rewards; rather, they seem to concentrate positive incentives on cooperative peers while simply ignoring low contributors. They also receive rewards in several rounds, which may reinforce a belief that others reciprocate and that reward exchanges are part of a cooperative subculture in the group. Their continued willingness to reward even when they have not been especially rewarded just prior suggests that their rewarding is not purely tit‑for‑tat gratitude, but more aligned with a generalized reciprocity or norm‑support motive. Overall, their reward persona is that of a “selective cooperative investor”: they invest resources to support and stabilize cooperation, especially among perceived high contributors, but they do so strategically and inconsistently rather than as unconditional altruism.

The player never uses the reward mechanism. They spend nothing on rewarding others in any context, including when the group is highly cooperative and when they personally receive rewards from others. They also never reciprocate rewards directly, at least not via the available mechanism. This suggests a strong saving or payoff-maximizing stance with respect to the side mechanism: they appear to view the extra reward option as unnecessary or inefficient, even though it is break-even in cost-benefit terms. They do not use rewards to signal gratitude, to strengthen alliances, or to strategically encourage cooperators, despite being repeatedly rewarded themselves. If they feel gratitude, it does not translate into material action. Overall, they prioritize contributing to the public good itself and keeping their remaining payoff, rather than spending on costly social reinforcement. Their cooperative norm is implemented only via contributions, not via prosocial side-payments. Whether this reflects indifference to interpersonal signaling, uncertainty about the mechanism, or a deliberate efficiency principle is unknown, but their revealed type is: unconditional full cooperator, non-rewarding, and payoff-preserving with respect to optional reward tools.

The player never spends their own coins on the reward mechanism despite multiple opportunities and despite receiving rewards from others in several rounds. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost to incentivize others, even when those others are clearly cooperating at high levels. Given that the cost and benefit of reward are symmetric on the player’s screen, the refusal to use rewards is not driven by an obvious efficiency loss within the mechanism as described to them; instead, it likely reflects either a preference to avoid extra complexity/effort, a belief that rewards are unnecessary given already high contributions, or a view that it is not their role to “manage” others’ behavior. They appear willing to let the contribution pattern evolve without actively steering it through positive incentives. They also do not seem to reciprocate direct reward they receive. Being rewarded does not trigger them to start rewarding others. This suggests that, although they appreciate cooperative environments (as shown by their own contributions), they do not express gratitude or social reinforcement via the formal reward channel. Any pro-social motivation they have is channeled into contributing to the public good itself, not into second-order cooperation via costly rewards. In sum, their reward style is “non-rewarding cooperator”: they contribute heavily to the public good but abstain entirely from using the explicit reward technology, indicating either a cost-focus on second-order actions, skepticism about the value of reward systems, or a simple preference for direct cooperation over meta-level incentives.

This player is an active but selective rewarder. They repeatedly spend on rewards throughout the game, but not in every round, and never at extremely high levels. Their reward pattern indicates a willingness to incur personal cost to support or reinforce cooperation, while still exercising some restraint. They appear to direct rewards toward apparent high contributors and to do so more often when group contributions are relatively strong or when a stable cooperative core is visible. When cooperation within the group is high and widespread, they sometimes withhold reward, suggesting that they do not feel compelled to “pay” for what already looks like a well-established norm. When cooperation is mixed or weakening, they are more inclined to use rewards, likely as a reinforcement or encouragement mechanism. They also reward in situations where they themselves receive reward, including in later rounds, which may indicate a tendency toward reciprocal or gratitude-based rewarding. However, they do not escalate reciprocation to excessive levels; their spending remains moderate. There is no evidence that they attempt to use reward as a precise, optimizing investment tool; rather, it functions as a moral or social signal: support those who cooperate, and occasionally reciprocate kindness. They do not use reward as a replacement for cooperation. They never reduce their contributions to “fund” more rewards, and they never offset frustration with freeriding by cutting their own giving; instead, they sometimes respond with positive reinforcement toward cooperators while keeping their own contributions maximal. This combination characterizes them as a prosocial, norm-enforcing rewarder who values sustaining cooperation even at a personal cost, but who is not mechanically generous every round and shows some situational judgment in when to reward.

The player never spends any of their own coins on rewarding others despite having many opportunities and being part of a largely cooperative group. They are a pure non-rewarder in terms of outgoing transfers. This holds even when the group is highly cooperative and when they personally benefit from others’ high contributions. There is no evidence of using rewards strategically to sustain cooperation or to reinforce particular players. In contrast, they receive rewards multiple times, and especially in some later periods they are the target of substantial positive transfers. This means they experience rewards but do not reciprocate them. They accept the benefits of others’ reward behavior without mirroring it, indicating that gratitude signaling via the formal reward mechanism is absent or at least not expressed. Given that reward is cost-neutral in this setup (each unit costs them the same amount it gives to others), their refusal to reward suggests that even a break-even, socially beneficial act is not attractive enough if it does not increase their own payoff. They may believe that their individual rewards would be too small to meaningfully change others’ behavior, or they may see no personal strategic return to paying for rewards in an environment that is already quite cooperative. Alternatively, they may simply prioritize simplicity and payoff maximization, ignoring the reward stage altogether. Overall, the reward persona is “reward-averse free-rider on others’ generosity”: they do not invest in positive sanctioning, even while benefiting from others who do, and they show no pattern of conditional or strategic rewarding in response to peers’ contributions or to being rewarded themselves.

They never use the reward option, despite having many opportunities to reward high contributors and despite receiving rewards themselves. This indicates that, while they strongly cooperate in the contribution stage, they do not invest in second-order cooperation through costly positive reinforcement. Possible interpretations include: they view rewarding as unnecessary when cooperation is high; they are narrowly focused on the contribution decision as their main cooperative act; or they are generally cost-averse in the sanction/reward stage even while being generous in the contribution stage. Their behavior characterizes a “non-reciprocating cooperator” with respect to costly rewards.

The player does not engage in any costly reward behavior, even in an environment dominated by full cooperation and despite themselves being repeatedly rewarded by others. This suggests that their positive social preferences take the form of contributing to the public good and enforcing norms via punishment, rather than via direct interpersonal rewards. They do not appear to use rewards either to reinforce cooperative partners, to reciprocate kind treatment, or to signal gratitude or alliance. Given that the per-unit cost and effectiveness of reward and punishment are symmetric, their non-use of rewards appears to be a genuine preference rather than a simple cost asymmetry. Their social strategy is therefore asymmetric: they are willing to incur costs to sanction defectors, but not to individually uplift cooperators.

The player is highly willing to reward others and uses the reward mechanism more frequently than punishment. They reward almost exclusively those who are cooperating fully, particularly when the group is highly cooperative. They occasionally reward specific individuals repeatedly, suggesting attempts to encourage or acknowledge consistent cooperators. Their use of rewards is often broad, at times spreading rewards across many or nearly all active cooperators, even at substantial personal cost. This pattern implies a prosocial, reciprocity-based strategy: they seek to reinforce high contributions and signal approval, not to buy immediate material advantage. They sometimes reward individuals who previously defected but have returned to full cooperation, indicating a readiness to positively reinforce norm compliance after a transgression. The player appears to see reward as a tool both for sustaining a cooperative climate and for expressing gratitude or endorsement of others’ cooperative choices.

They are willing to use rewards, but in a light, selective way. They reward only players who are contributing fully, and never reward defectors. Rewards are scattered across several different high‑contributors rather than concentrated on a single partner, suggesting they are rewarding the behavior (full cooperation) rather than building a narrow alliance. They use only small reward amounts and do not try to “buy” loyalty or coordinate reciprocity; the timing of rewards does not systematically track how much they themselves are rewarded. This looks like expressive or normative reinforcement: they like to acknowledge cooperative behavior and to support a high‑contribution norm, but they are cost‑conscious and do not invest heavily in it.

They are very restrained with rewards and only use them once. When they do, they direct small rewards to several peers who are clearly cooperating, not to themselves or to the norm violator. This looks like an attempt at positive reinforcement for those consistently following the group’s cooperative rule. However, given the highly inefficient reward technology and the substantial personal cost they incur for a modest benefit to others, they seem to treat reward as a symbolic gesture rather than an ongoing tool: afterward they revert to using no rewards at all, even when others contribute fully or when someone suggests rewarding the previously non‑contributing player. This indicates they recognize the cooperative norm and are willing to spend a little to express approval and support, but are generally payoff‑conscious enough not to make costly rewarding a repeated strategy.

They almost never use the costly reward mechanism themselves, despite receiving occasional rewards from others. This is notable because they explicitly talk about encouraging good behavior in chat, yet they operationalize that mostly through punishment, not positive incentives. Their rare references to “encouraging” better behavior appear in communication, not in actual reward allocations. That suggests that, in practice, they see negative sanctions as the primary lever to shape behavior under this technology: punishment is cheap and powerful, reward is expensive and weak. The payoff structure heavily favors punishment, so strategically they prioritize deterrence and discipline over explicit gratitude. They do not seem to adopt tit-for-tat rewarding of those who reward them; there is no visible reciprocity via the reward channel. This again supports the view that they treat the reward tool as economically inefficient and choose not to deploy it even when they might like, in principle, to reinforce cooperation. Overall, they are a “punishment-dominant enforcer” rather than a symmetric punish-and-reward conditioner.

They never use the reward option at any point, even though many others occasionally do. They also receive small rewards from others in some rounds but do not reciprocate. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend on positive incentives, possibly because the reward technology is inefficient (cost exceeds benefit) or because they see full contribution as a basic expectation that does not merit explicit material reward. Their cooperative norm seems to be “everyone should just contribute,” not “I will pay extra to thank you for doing so.” There is no evidence they use reward strategically to shape others’ behavior or to build alliances; they rely entirely on their own consistent cooperation and, occasionally, on others’ punishment of defectors. Whether they conceptually approve or disapprove of rewarding is unknown; their behavior only shows that they prioritize conserving their own payoff over subsidizing others, even when they are very pro-cooperation.

They never spend to reward others despite multiple opportunities and despite others rewarding them at least once. This is consistent across cooperative and less cooperative phases. They ignore the reward mechanism even when most or all others contribute fully and some group members explicitly talk about rewarding good behavior. Given the very poor efficiency of rewards in this game, it is plausible that they consider it not worth the cost, but this is inference with low confidence because they never verbalize this reasoning. Behaviorally, they are a pure punisher, not a reciprocal rewarder: they accept being rewarded by others but do not reciprocate. This suggests that they view norm compliance (high contribution) as a baseline expectation that does not merit extra positive transfers, whereas deviations from that baseline sometimes justify costly punishment.

They are notably generous with rewards, despite the reward mechanism being inefficient. They frequently reward consistent cooperators and especially active norm advocates. Reward targets include players who always contribute, encourage others to contribute, or help coordinate deductions against free‑riders. They also reward some individuals they have already rewarded before, indicating a pattern of reinforcing perceived “good citizens.” At one point they even reward the chronic non‑contributor during a phase when that player finally contributes, aligning with another player’s verbal call to encourage the good behavior; this suggests they use reward to shape behavior and signal forgiveness when norms are followed. Rewards are often given in small units to multiple people in the same round, rather than concentrated on a single ally, indicating a diffuse, community‑oriented reinforcement style. They are willing to pay a non‑trivial cumulative cost on rewards over the game, which points to a genuine preference for expressing appreciation and reinforcing group cooperation, not only instrumental signaling.

They also never use reward, even in rounds where others deviate from full contribution or later move back to high cooperation in ways that might be strategically worth reinforcing. Given the very low efficiency of reward technology, it is possible they judge it as too costly, but they do not make any explicit remark about this nor attempt targeted token rewards. The absence of reward use, even when they themselves shift briefly to contribution and receive small rewards from others, suggests they are not motivated by reciprocal gratitude or by norm-reinforcement behavior. Their rare cooperative acts are not accompanied by efforts to encourage others, nor to signal allegiance to the group. They do not behave like a conditional cooperator who rewards or reciprocates. Overall, their stance toward both punishment and reward is “non-interventionist”: they opt out of the secondary mechanisms entirely, acting purely through their own contribution level and absorbing whatever others do.

They almost never reward, despite having many opportunities. When they finally do reward, they spend a relatively large amount in a single episode and distribute small rewards broadly across multiple players, including prior low contributors and the ex‑free‑rider who just starts cooperating. This looks like a one‑off, deliberate act to positively reinforce a cluster of behaviors: continued contributions, recent switches to cooperation, and perhaps participation in the social dynamic. It is not narrowly profit‑maximizing: the reward technology is inefficient, and they sacrifice substantial own payoff to send these signals, indicating that warm‑glow or fairness sentiments are in play. The decision to reward the former free‑rider shortly after explicitly arguing that they are real and losing points suggests an attempt at rehabilitative encouragement rather than pure punishment. They do not develop a repeated reward strategy; instead, they primarily rely on punishment for norm enforcement and bring out reward only for a salient moment of cooperation and “good behavior.” This indicates they see reward as a special, costly signal to encourage or recognize improvement, rather than a routine tool.

They never use the reward option, even when others engage in positive reinforcement and occasionally propose rewarding cooperative behavior. They show no explicit approval or disapproval of rewarding; they simply ignore this tool. Given that rewarding is costly and relatively inefficient in this environment, and they already have an aversion to deducting coins from others, they may see all post-contribution interventions as undesirable or confusing. There is no evidence that they attempt to strategically encourage good behavior through rewards, nor that they reciprocate when they themselves receive small rewards.
