They never use the reward option at any point, even though many others are contributing at the maximum level and some peers are expending considerable resources to reward high cooperators. This persistent choice implies they either find the reward technology too inefficient or see no strategic reason to spend on positive incentives when high cooperation is already widespread. Given that they do not punish either, this is not just a substitution from negative to positive enforcement; rather, they opt out of both. Their social strategy therefore does not rely on directly managing others’ behavior via formal reward and punishment. They appear to free-ride on others’ costly norm enforcement and encouragement efforts: they benefit from the high contributions and from the climate that punishers and rewarders help maintain, without paying into those enforcement mechanisms themselves. There is no evidence that they ever attempt to signal alliance, gratitude, or leadership through rewards. Their type is that of a non-enforcer who focuses their decisions on contribution levels and personal payoff, leaving the social engineering of the group to others.

They use rewards more often than punishment and in a nuanced, targeted way. They reward clear high contributors at times, consistent with reinforcing cooperative behavior. They also reward a previously low contributor after that player increases their contribution, consistent with encouraging norm improvement rather than simply praising the highest cooperators. Later, they extend rewards to multiple players at once, including some whose behavior is not extreme in either direction, indicating that they may sometimes reward to acknowledge effort, foster goodwill, or stabilize cooperation rather than purely for strict incentive optimization. Overall, they are willing to sacrifice their own payoff to provide positive reinforcement, even when the reward mechanism is personally costly and relatively inefficient.

The player is generally reluctant to use the reward option and does so only once, directed at a peer who is clearly cooperative. This isolated use of reward is consistent with a desire to acknowledge or reinforce high contributions, but the low frequency suggests that they view reward as inefficient compared to punishment, given the less favorable payoff leverage. They do not try to build elaborate “alliances” via mutual rewarding, even when others reward them. Instead, they revert quickly to a pattern of no rewards, even in the presence of sustained high cooperation by the group. Their default orientation is to cooperate themselves and occasionally penalize defectors rather than to incur additional costs to positively incentivize cooperators.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite having numerous opportunities where rewarding would be cheap relative to the surplus they gain from others’ contributions. This holds even when the group is strongly cooperative, when others are clearly spending large amounts to reward cooperators, and when they themselves receive nontrivial rewards. They do not reciprocate rewards or participate in sustaining the reward norm. The absence of any reward behavior indicates several likely traits: they are strictly self‑regarding in payoff terms, they are unwilling to incur even small costs to support or signal approval of cooperation, and they do not attempt to strategically steer others’ behavior via positive reinforcement. Their decision is consistent and absolute — there is no marginal or selective rewarding of especially generous or especially supportive peers. They do not appear to experiment with reward as an instrument at all. Given how much they benefit from others’ reward spending, their refusal to reward suggests either (a) a clear understanding that reward is individually costly with no guaranteed direct return, or (b) a very low intrinsic value placed on fairness, reciprocity, or gratitude relative to material payoff. They behave as if any coin spent on reward is a wasted coin, even when that stance risks long‑run erosion of cooperation.

They are a very low-frequency, low-intensity rewarder. Most of the time they spend nothing on reward, even when others clearly contribute and even when other players are spending substantial amounts to reward the group. When they do reward, it is modest and infrequent rather than systematic or extreme. There is no clear pattern of using reward as an explicit strategic tool to shape others’ contributions (e.g., they do not reliably increase reward when cooperation declines, nor do they ramp down reward when others defect). Their few reward expenditures are more consistent with occasional, weak expressions of appreciation than with a deliberate incentive scheme. They show no sign of “competitive rewarding” or status-seeking via large reward outlays; instead, they preserve their own payoff and allow others to be the primary reward financiers. Overall, they value cooperation but appear reluctant to incur substantial extra cost to reinforce it via reward.

This player is an extremely heavy user of the reward mechanism, often spending a very large share of their available payoff to reward others. They repeatedly accept substantial personal cost to transfer benefits to peers, indicating a strong prosocial or norm-enforcing orientation that values shaping others’ behavior. They appear to use rewards broadly rather than narrowly targeting a few partners, suggesting that they aim to reinforce group-level cooperation rather than build bilateral exchanges. Their spending remains high even when it is clearly unprofitable in the short run; this implies that they treat rewarding as a tool to sustain or escalate group cooperation or as an intrinsic expression of approval, rather than a strict investment expecting one-to-one returns. They do receive rewards from others but not in proportion to what they spend, yet they continue to spend heavily, consistent with weak expectations of direct reciprocity. Over time, their reward intensity fluctuates but remains consistently above the group’s typical level, modeling them as a “super-rewarder” who uses positive incentives to try to stabilize high contributions. They do not visibly match reward spending to fine-grained differences in peers’ contribution histories (since almost everyone except a few consistent free-riders contributes fully most of the time), so their trigger for rewarding is likely either simple cooperation (contribute = reward) or a desire to share surplus widely when the public good is high. They rarely scale back reward use even when others defect or when they themselves defect, suggesting they separate their own contribution choice from their use of rewards and may view rewarding as a standing responsibility or coordination signal.

This player is extremely frugal with rewards. Across the entire game, they spend almost nothing on rewarding others, even when surrounded by heavy rewarders and clear high contributors. Their default is to keep all or nearly all distributable coins rather than use them to reinforce prosocial behavior. When they do spend on reward, the amounts are minimal and infrequent, suggesting that rewarding is not central to their strategy or identity. There is no clear pattern that they reward more after being personally rewarded; the data instead show them often receiving substantial rewards while not reciprocating materially. This points to a type who is comfortable being a “reward taker” rather than a “reward giver.” They do not appear to use reward strategically to shape others’ contributions: they continue contributing highly without investing in the incentive system that others are trying to create, even when the environment is plainly reward-sensitive. Their occasional small rewards might reflect momentary gratitude or experimentation with the mechanism, but not a consistent norm-enforcement motive. Overall, they prioritize maximizing their own payoff over spending to sustain cooperation via rewards, relying on others’ willingness to pay those incentive costs.

This player never spends on rewards, even when surrounded by many high contributors and frequent, substantial rewarding by others. They accept incoming rewards but do not reciprocate via the reward channel. This holds both in highly cooperative phases and in more mixed or free-rider-heavy phases. There is no sign of selective rewarding of high contributors, no attempt to use rewards to encourage under-contributors, and no reaction to being rewarded themselves. Their reward behavior is thus purely passive: they benefit from others’ generosity but do not mirror it. This may indicate that they view reward spending as inefficient or unnecessary, that they prioritize conserving their own payoff over active social reinforcement, or that they adhere to a narrow norm in which contributing is their duty but rewarding is optional. Without variation, their underlying motive for never rewarding remains unknown, but they clearly do not engage in strategic reinforcement, gratitude signaling, or “buying” cooperation from others.

They use reward sparingly and in a controlled, low-intensity way. They nearly always spend a small, nonzero amount on rewards, but never escalate into the very high spending patterns visible in some peers. Their own total incoming rewards are consistently positive, indicating others regularly see them as a desirable partner, yet they do not “match” or mirror the scale of others’ generosity. This restraint suggests they view rewarding as a minor tool rather than the main driver of cooperation. Their reward behavior appears loosely contingent on cooperative climate and perhaps on visible prosociality, but not retaliatory or strongly strategic. They reduce or skip rewarding when group contributions and/or overall cooperation drop, and they are more willing to spend when the group is more cooperative and others are also using rewards. They do not show signs of targeted “rewarding the under-rewarded” or punishing via omission; instead, their pattern is consistent with broadly supporting the cooperative norm at low cost. They do not appear to chase status or reciprocal favor by dramatically increasing rewards when they themselves receive more; their own intake of rewards does not provoke large jumps in their spending. Both their ceiling and floor of reward spending are narrow: they never go to extremes, upward or downward, suggesting a preference for moderation and budget control. They seem to treat reward spending as a small, steady signal of support for cooperation rather than as a flexible lever to finely tune others’ behavior.

This player never spends any of their own coins to reward others, even though they are repeatedly informed about the option and can see clear, sustained cooperative behavior from multiple peers. Their willingness to reward is effectively zero in this environment. They do receive rewards from others on several occasions, and those rewards are targeted rather than random, indicating that at least some peers interpret the focal player’s occasional contributions as cooperation worth encouraging. However, the player never reciprocates these social investments. The absence of any outgoing reward despite incoming rewards shows that they do not use the reward mechanism either as a gratitude signal or as a strategic tool to sustain cooperation. This suggests a strongly self-regarding cost–benefit calculus: they are unwilling to incur even a cost-neutral or purely symbolic expense to influence others’ behavior or express appreciation. They are not trying to shape group norms or build a reputation as a supporter of cooperators; instead, they treat rewards only as windfall gains when others choose to give them. There is no behavioral evidence that they view reward as a coordination device, a way to stabilize alliances, or a means to enforce a cooperative norm. If they recognize the strategic potential of rewards at all, they choose not to use it.

This player never uses the reward mechanism at all, even though they themselves receive rewards in multiple periods. They shows no willingness to incur any personal cost to reinforce others’ cooperative behavior, and no evidence of gratitude-based or norm-enforcing rewarding. Reward decisions are not used strategically: they neither attempt to encourage high contributors nor to build alliances by rewarding those who support them. The absence of reward use persists under varying conditions: different overall contribution levels, different patterns of who cooperates and who defects, and different histories of being rewarded. This indicates a robust unwillingness to spend on others, consistent with a strictly self-regarding payoff orientation. They does not appear to use rewards as a tool for conditional cooperation, future-oriented incentives, or reputation building. There is no observable pattern that would suggest selective rewarding (e.g., only top contributors, only those who reward them, or only those matching some fairness ideal). Their latent criteria for rewarding others, if any exist, remain unknown; behaviorally, the operative rule is effectively “never spend to reward.”

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even though they repeatedly receive rewards from others. This indicates a very low propensity to engage in costly positive reinforcement. Possible motives include a narrow payoff-maximizing stance with respect to the reward mechanism (treating reward as a pure cost with no expected benefit), an expectation that contributions alone are sufficient to signal cooperativeness, or a preference to avoid role differentiation (not taking on the extra “rewarder” role in the group). Which of these dominates is unknown. They do not selectively reward high contributors, do not respond to their own reward receipt by rewarding back, and do not appear to use reward as a strategic tool to stabilize cooperation or build alliances. Their pattern fits a “non-rewarding cooperator”: someone willing to incur the cost of contribution for the public good but not willing to pay extra, even at neutral efficiency, to shape others’ behavior. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling through reward or of using reward to sanction or encourage specific individuals. In environments where reward is optional and individually costly, this type is likely to rely solely on their own cooperative example rather than on active incentive design.

This player is generally restrained in using the reward mechanism and does not treat it as a default tool. They sometimes do not spend on reward even when many others clearly cooperate, indicating that they do not feel compelled to systematically or proportionally reward all cooperators. When they do choose to spend on reward, they do so in non-trivial amounts rather than token gestures, suggesting that they view rewarding as purposeful rather than symbolic. The timing of their reward use aligns with the presence of other clear cooperators in the group, so the most plausible trigger is observing cooperative behavior and possibly appreciating it or trying to encourage it. However, their reward behavior is inconsistent over time; they do not lock into a steady “reward all cooperators” or “never reward” rule. This implies that they may be selectively reinforcing, perhaps experimenting with whether rewarding affects how much they themselves are rewarded or how much others contribute, though their own contribution rule never changes. They do not appear to use reward defensively or retaliatorily (e.g., no evidence of withholding reward from specific cooperators who might not have rewarded them), but the granularity of targeting is unknown. Overall, they are a cautious, episodic rewarder who is willing to sacrifice some payoff to recognize or support cooperation, yet does not treat rewarding as central to their strategy.

This player is a frequent and active user of the reward mechanism. They repeatedly spend on rewards, sometimes at non-trivial cost, and they do so primarily in periods when they themselves are cooperating. When they experiment with free-riding, they stop rewarding entirely, indicating that, in their mind, rewarding is bundled with being a cooperator; they do not reward when they are not also meeting the cooperative standard themselves. Their reward-giving appears aimed at reinforcing cooperation rather than at pure reciprocity or strategic profit. The technology is payoff-neutral (each unit of reward costs exactly what it gives), so using it is not individually profitable; nonetheless, they repeatedly choose to incur the cost. This suggests they value the signaling and coordination function of rewards: they likely use rewards to encourage contributors, to express approval, and to help stabilize a cooperative norm. They do not show evidence of using rewards to “price” cooperation finely (e.g., scaling reward precisely by how many others contributed), at least not in a way that can be unambiguously inferred. What is clear is that they turn reward on and off in a way that tracks their own normative stance: when they act as a cooperator, they are willing to pay to recognize and reinforce cooperative peers; when they step outside that norm by not contributing, they withhold rewards entirely. Overall, they are a pro-social rewarder who treats reward as a tool for norm support and positive reinforcement, not as a self-interested investment.

The player uses reward sparingly and selectively rather than as a systematic tool. Most of the time, they do not spend on rewards even when many others are cooperating, which suggests that rewarding is not their default response to observed contribution. When they do reward, they incur a nontrivial cost, indicating some willingness to reinforce behavior or to express appreciation, but this willingness is limited and intermittent. There is no clear evidence that they use rewards in a nuanced, strategic way (for example, to differentially target consistent cooperators vs. newcomers), because the data do not reveal their specific targets; thus their precise reward rule is unknown. However, the timing—after long-run, stable full cooperation in the face of mixed group behavior—suggests that rewards may be triggered more by sustained patterns of cooperation than by single instances. They do not appear to rely on reward as their primary mechanism to manage others’ behavior; instead, their core strategy is to cooperate unconditionally and occasionally add reward as a secondary, modest reinforcement or a form of social acknowledgment.

This player is an active and relatively heavy user of the reward mechanism. They frequently spend nontrivial amounts of their own payoff to reward others, including in rounds where they are themselves defecting and in rounds where rewards come at a noticeable opportunity cost. They thus reveal a genuine willingness to invest in social incentives, not just to maximize immediate own payoff. Their rewards appear selectively targeted toward high contributors and persistent cooperators rather than distributed randomly. The use of reward is not purely proportional to their own contribution: they sometimes reward while free-riding, which indicates an attempt to shape or sustain others’ cooperation even when they are not currently contributing themselves. This pattern is consistent with a “managerial free-rider” tendency: they may see rewarding as a cheaper way (psychologically or strategically) to maintain a cooperative environment while occasionally taking advantage of it. They increase reward spending substantially at least once, even though each unit of reward is cost-neutral at the individual level (same cost as benefit). This escalation suggests that they are trying to send strong positive signals or to reinforce particular partners, rather than merely matching minimal norms of gratitude. They seem comfortable with asymmetric generosity: they sometimes invest more in rewarding others than they receive back in the same period, implying that they are not operating on a strict quid-pro-quo ledger. Strategically, they likely view reward as a tool for: (1) encouraging consistent co-operators, (2) expressing approval and possibly building reputational capital with those players, and (3) compensating for their own occasional free-riding by supporting the group indirectly. They do not use reward defensively or sparingly; instead, they treat it as an integral instrument of their overall play.

This player never spends on rewards, despite having repeated opportunities and receiving rewards themselves. They therefore behave as a “non-rewarding cooperator.” They do not use the reward mechanism either to thank contributors, to strengthen cooperative norms, or to signal alliances. This is not because rewarding is structurally inefficient in this game (the net effect of reward is neutral for total payoffs), but their behavior shows no tendency to engage in even neutral-cost signaling via rewards. The absence of any rewarding, even in periods when many others contribute and the player is personally rewarded, implies that they either (a) do not value symbolic reciprocity via the reward channel, (b) view reward as strategically unnecessary, or (c) simply do not attend much to this mechanism. They clearly are willing to promote group welfare through contributions, but they do not extend that prosociality into second-order cooperation (rewarding cooperators). Thus, their pro-sociality is “first-order only”: they help by contributing but do not use personal funds to shape or reinforce others’ behavior. They also do not appear to experiment with rewarding, even once, which suggests a relatively stable preference against using this tool rather than a transient oversight. There is no evidence that they attempt selective or targeted rewards (e.g., favoring consistently high contributors or those who rewarded them); such strategic reinforcement is absent.

The player **never spends any coins on reward**, despite repeated opportunities and clear evidence of others rewarding them. This strongly signals that they do not value positive reciprocity enough to pay for it. They appear to treat the reward mechanism as a one-way benefit to themselves rather than a tool they should also deploy. Their refusal to reward persists across cooperative and defective phases of their own behavior and across periods when they are being rewarded and when they are not. There is no sign that they try to **reinforce high contributors**, build coalitions, or strategically encourage cooperation via rewards. They also do not use reward to “thank” others or to reciprocate kindness, even when that kindness is directed specifically at them and identifiable. This indicates that **gratitude signaling is very weak or absent** in their decision calculus, at least when it has an explicit monetary cost. From a strategic standpoint, this looks like a **strict cost–benefit reading of the reward technology**: since each unit of reward costs them as much as it gives the other, they seem to perceive no instrumental advantage in using it. They behave as if any coin spent on rewarding is strictly wasted from their own perspective, ignoring potential long-run cooperative gains. This implies either **short time horizons**, skepticism that rewards can change others’ behavior, or a general reluctance to incur personal cost for social influence. Overall, their reward behavior is best described as **non-rewarding, non-reciprocating, and individually payoff-maximizing with respect to the reward stage**.

The player is an active and relatively heavy user of reward, clearly willing to pay to influence others. They frequently spend a nontrivial share of their resources on rewarding, even when this reduces their own immediate payoff. This indicates a strong motivation to shape behavior and/or relationships, not just to maximize short-term earnings. Their rewards appear primarily directed toward higher contributors, consistent with a norm-enforcing or cooperation-reinforcing style. They do not reward indiscriminately; the pattern suggests they are selective, using reward as a signal of approval and as an incentive to sustain contributions from key cooperative partners. They are also willing to invest in reward even in periods when they themselves are not perfectly cooperative, implying that they may view reward as a tool to keep the cooperative engine running, rather than as something reserved only for when they personally “deserve” it most. They sometimes reduce or suspend rewarding activity, suggesting their willingness to pay for reward is conditional: if the cooperative environment weakens or if their own payoff priorities temporarily shift, they pull back. This conditionality is consistent with a strategic approach to reward rather than purely altruistic gift-giving. Given that reward is cost-neutral per unit (cost equals benefit), the fact that they still use it extensively indicates they are focused on its strategic and relational dimensions: directing benefit to specific individuals, creating gratitude or alliances, and marking trustworthy partners. They appear comfortable using reward as a social technology to cluster with cooperators and differentiate them from free-riders.

The player is consistently willing to spend on reward and appears to use it selectively rather than indiscriminately. They do not mechanically reward in every period, suggesting they treat rewarding as a strategic signal rather than a fixed obligation. Their use of reward coexists with continued high contributions, which implies they view reward as a complement to cooperation, not a substitute for it. Given that they cannot punish, their reward spending plausibly targets cooperators to reinforce desirable behavior or to express approval; there is no evidence they reward defectors. They sometimes reward even when group contribution levels are mediocre, consistent with a “support the good apples” mindset rather than requiring a perfectly cooperative group before investing in incentives. The fact that they occasionally withhold reward despite high group cooperation suggests they care about efficiency and perhaps about personal payoff: rewarding has a net-zero effect on total coins but does not increase social surplus, so their intermittent restraint is consistent with balancing prosocial signaling against personal cost. Overall, they behave like a conditional rewarder: inclined to reward cooperative patterns, but willing to pause or adjust intensity depending on the broader context and perhaps their own payoff position.

This player is a heavy, persistent user of the reward mechanism, especially in the early and mid phases of the game. They repeatedly spend a substantial portion of their available payoff on rewarding others, even though the reward technology is strictly cost-neutral (one-to-one cost-to-benefit). This indicates that their motivation to reward is not driven by personal monetary gain from the reward channel itself. They appear to use rewards as a social tool to reinforce cooperation norms: rewarding those who contribute, especially when cooperation is relatively high, and scaling back or suspending rewards when cooperation is more mixed or declining. Their temporary cessation of rewarding coincides with a phase where cooperative behavior among peers is relatively high and possibly self-sustaining, suggesting they sometimes economize on reward use when they perceive cooperation as “on track,” but generally return to rewarding afterward. This pattern implies that they view reward as an instrument to stabilize or revive cooperation rather than a constant fixed habit. Because reward costs them exactly what it benefits the recipient, their continued use of it implies they derive intrinsic satisfaction (or “warm-glow”) from supporting cooperators, or they believe that reinforcing others will protect or enhance future group cooperation in a way that is worth the cost. Their willingness to spend heavily indicates a strong pro-social orientation and a readiness to sacrifice personal payoff to support those who align with their cooperative standard. They likely target rewards primarily toward high contributors and withhold rewards from clear non-contributors, using reward as a positive selection mechanism rather than trying to convert free-riders through gifts. The fact that they do not abandon reward use after low reciprocation suggests their rewarding is driven more by principle and forward-looking norm enforcement than by short-term tit-for-tat gain.

The player is extremely willing to spend resources on reward, even at significant personal cost. They repeatedly spend large amounts to reward others, especially early and mid‑game, and they participate in high‑stakes mutual reward schemes within a sub‑group. Their reward behavior is not purely reactive; they proactively propose fixed per‑round reward exchanges and commit to sending substantial amounts to allies. They use reward both as a coordination tool (to stabilize a cooperative “team”) and as a social signal of inclusion and appreciation. At times they reduce or skip rewarding, but when this happens it appears to be driven by time or coordination constraints or by strategic adjustments to whom they consider “in the team,” rather than by a shift toward self‑interest. They selectively exclude players they deem inconsistent or untrustworthy from reward plans, indicating that their rewarding is conditional on perceived reliability and reciprocity.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite repeated opportunities and extensive group-level discussion of reward exchanges, alliances, and “teams.” This is consistent throughout the game. They do not join explicit reward cartels nor engage in tit-for-tat reward practices, even when others are strongly encouraging such behaviors and naming groups for reciprocal transfers. This suggests a strong reluctance to pay private costs for social or strategic reward, even when the marginal return on reward is greater than one. Whether this is due to a deliberate payoff-maximizing calculation (“keep all reward coins and free-ride on others’ reward spending”), lack of understanding of the reward mechanism, or simple inattention/low effort is unknown. They effectively behave as a “pure contributor, reward-free-rider”: they provide public-good contributions but do not participate in the secondary reward economy.

The player never spends any coins on rewards at any point, even though they are surrounded by intense reward activity, explicit coordination on mutual rewarding, and repeated invitations (implicit) to join a reward “team.” They do not attempt to incentivize high contributors, repay those who reward them, or use rewards strategically to form alliances. Their decision rule around rewarding appears to be: participate in the public good through contributions but stay out of the secondary reward network. This could reflect a desire to avoid the cognitive and logistical overhead of tracking partners and repayment obligations, a belief that high contributions alone are sufficient to sustain cooperation, or an implicit evaluation that the reward mechanism is not worth the complexity or cost (motive is unknown). They also do not engage in token or minimal rewarding; their stance is all-or-nothing, and in this case, consistently nothing. They thus behave as a pure contributor rather than a contributor-rewarder hybrid.

They are a heavy and proactive user of the reward mechanism, frequently spending substantial amounts on rewards, sometimes at considerable personal cost. Their rewards are primarily directed at members of a loosely defined alliance (especially those who coordinate on rewarding each other) and at players they feel they “owe” based on prior exchanges. They repeatedly offer rewards to others, solicit requests (“anyone else want reward?”), and try to ensure that all intended teammates receive transfers, sometimes overshooting (“doing 100 for everyone”) to correct earlier shortfalls. They care about reciprocation: they respond to explicit claims that someone is owed by promising larger amounts in subsequent rounds. However, their rewarding is not purely tit-for-tat; they also show generosity and forgiveness when coordination is imperfect, and they keep rewarding even when they experience confusion about who did what. They attempt to follow alliance rules (e.g., fixed reward amounts for a circle of players), indicating a strategic use of rewards to solidify a coalition and maintain mutual high contributions, but they sometimes struggle with time/complexity and then compensate afterwards, which suggests a strong internal fairness norm rather than precise strategic accounting.

They never spend any coins on rewards across the entire game. This is consistent and absolute: even when others form explicit reward rings, talk about who is in or out, and discuss repayment obligations, the player does not participate. They also never verbally commit to rewarding anyone. There is no direct evidence that they ever receive rewards from others (their personal payoff numbers contain no reward income), so they are not part of any mutual-reward cluster. Their non-use of rewards suggests one or a combination of: low attention or motivation to micro-manage payoffs, unwillingness to pay any personal cost for targeted reciprocity, or confusion/disinterest regarding the reward interface. There is no evidence that they strategically withhold rewards to punish or to signal anything; they simply ignore the mechanism.

They are extremely willing to use the reward mechanism and, in aggregate, spend very large amounts on rewarding others. This begins immediately: they adopt heavy rewarding from the outset and sustain high spending in many rounds. They do not use reward sparingly or only when cheap; instead, they behave like a “prosocial spendthrift” who is comfortable sacrificing own payoff to support a mutually rewarding structure. Their reward decisions appear tied more to coalition maintenance than to fine-grained marginal incentives. They repeatedly invest heavily in rewarding even when the economic return from reward (given its cost and others’ behavior) is uncertain from their own payoff perspective. They do, however, occasionally choose not to reward at all in some rounds. Because they never state in chat whom they reward or why, those omissions could reflect time pressure, strategic pauses, or an attempt to free-ride on the established “reward club”; the underlying motive is unknown. Overall, they treat reward as a primary lever to sustain a cooperative in-group: they repeatedly pay substantial costs in the reward stage even though contribution behavior is already near maximal for almost everyone. They do not direct reward as explicit punishment or withholding toward defectors; instead, reward appears to be used for alliance-building and mutual boosting among high contributors and/or a named “team” of players (even though they never verbally claim membership).

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite multiple opportunities and explicit, ongoing coordination of reciprocal reward “teams.” They receive small rewards in a couple of instances but do not reciprocate, even to those who reward them directly. They also do not try to buy entry into the reward alliance, and they ignore open invitations or coordination attempts to establish mutual reward patterns. This indicates a strong reluctance to engage in costly reward, regardless of the favorable reward technology or social pressure. Whether this is due to confusion about the mechanism, intentional payoff maximization, or simple inattention is unknown. Instrumentally, they act like a pure contributor who free-rides on others’ reward structures: they provide public-good contributions but avoid the extra cost of rewarding, even when that might build alliances or reinforce cooperation. They also never use small, symbolic rewards (e.g., one unit) as social signals, reinforcing the impression that they are opting out of the reward layer altogether.

The player is an extremely active and generous rewarder, often spending large amounts to reward others. Their rewards are primarily used to (a) reciprocate past rewards, (b) cement membership in a chosen “team,” and (c) enforce informal contracts (“I send you X if you send me X”). They explicitly track who has received their rewards and expects symmetric treatment, telling others when they “owe” repayment. They are willing to invest heavily and repeatedly in this reciprocal reward network, even when the mechanics are confusing. When they perceive someone as unreliable or opportunistic (e.g., a player who under-rewards or contributes little), they advocate excluding that person from the reward circle. They also offer individualized side‑deals (one‑to‑one reciprocal reward tests) as a method of screening for trustworthiness. Overall, they use rewards as a strategic tool for alliance building and selective reinforcement rather than as a blanket gift to all high contributors.

They never spend any coins on rewards, despite abundant opportunities and explicit social pressure to use the reward mechanism within “teams.” This is stable across the game: their reward spending is always zero. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal costs to reward others, even when the group is using rewards heavily and when reward exchanges are framed as mutually beneficial or reciprocal. Because they never reward anyone, it is impossible to see any nuanced patterns such as rewarding only high contributors, only allies, or reciprocating. The simplest description is that they effectively ignore the reward tool, acting as a pure beneficiary and contributor but not as an active encourager of others via costly reinforcement. This may reflect a priority on maximizing personal payoffs, confusion or discomfort with the mechanism, or indifference to the social reward coalition; which of these applies is unknown.

This player is an active and relatively heavy user of rewards. They are clearly willing to spend nontrivial amounts on rewarding others, even when the reward mechanism is individually payoff-neutral and the game has no explicit punishment option. Their reward use is selective rather than indiscriminate: rewards cluster in situations where certain peers stand out as consistently high or relatively high contributors, and where the player appears to be trying to support or encourage that cooperative cluster. They seem to use rewards primarily as a tool of strategic reinforcement rather than pure generosity. When they are themselves contributing a lot, they tend to spend on rewarding others, consistent with an attempt to stabilize or amplify a cooperative equilibrium. When they switch into low-contribution phases, they generally cease rewarding, which indicates that the motivation to reward is tied to their own cooperative stance and their perception that a cooperative regime is viable or worth “investing in.” There is no clear evidence that they reward low contributors in an attempt to “pull them up”; they appear more likely to direct rewards toward already-cooperative individuals (high or improving contributors), which is consistent with building or maintaining a cooperative in-group. The repeated willingness to spend substantially on rewards despite no net gain suggests they value shaping the group’s behavior or expressing approval, not just maximizing short-term earnings. Their reward strategy appears conditional on recent contribution patterns and on the presence of clearly identifiable high contributors. They behave as if they believe rewards may encourage continued high cooperation or at least signal alliance and appreciation to cooperative peers. When they doubt that cooperation is sustainable, they become sparing or entirely inactive with rewards, conserving payoff instead.

This player is an active user of the reward mechanism and appears to use it both as a social signal and as a strategic tool to shape group behavior. They repeatedly bear nontrivial costs to reward others, so they clearly value reinforcing cooperation even when it does not improve their own payoff in the strict, immediate sense. They do not treat reward as a rare or exceptional action; it is a recurring part of their strategy. Their rewarding is selective rather than indiscriminate. They direct rewards primarily toward relatively high contributors, and the timing of their rewards aligns with instances where several peers make clearly above-average contributions. This suggests that their primary trigger for rewarding is observed cooperative effort, not random experimentation or favoritism. They appear to use rewards as “approval signals” to encourage those who are already contributing and perhaps to stabilize a cooperative core. The player is willing to spend on rewards even in environments where average contributions are mixed or modest, indicating that they may hold a belief that positive reinforcement can gradually shift norms, not just respond to already ideal behavior. At the same time, their reward use is not constant; they sometimes choose not to reward even when others contribute substantially. This implies that they may be calibrating reward use by recent payoffs or by whether they perceive their previous rewards as having any effect on group behavior. There is no evidence of using reward to “buy” personal reciprocity in a narrow sense; their reward expenditures often occur without them receiving reward in return, and they continue to reward despite this. It is unlikely that this is a purely instrumental attempt to influence future behavior, since horizon length is unknown and the game ends; it aligns more with expressive or normative signaling: showing who they see as more deserving.
