When they finally reward, they do so in a highly selective, one-off manner tied to a broader moral classification of others, rather than as a continuous reinforcement of high contributors. This positions them as a “sparing rewarder”: willing to use positive incentives symbolically when making a strong endline statement, but not inclined to spend steadily to encourage cooperation.

This player uses reward frequently, even though the reward technology is inefficient for increasing others’ payoffs. They reward players who contribute high amounts, and they do so in a way that appears consistent over time: persistent high contributors receive repeated rewards, and moderate-but-cooperating contributors sometimes receive recognition as well. They almost never reward low contributors. Their pattern suggests that they view reward as a tool to reinforce cooperative norms and to show approval rather than as a way to maximize group efficiency in a narrow payoff sense. They are willing to sacrifice quite a lot of their own payoff to reward others, even in rounds where they themselves earn relatively little. This implies that they value establishing or maintaining a cooperative culture and perhaps a reputation (even under anonymity of individual actions) more than maximizing personal earnings. Their use of multiple reward units to the same player in a single round shows they sometimes emphasize strong positive reinforcement when someone’s contribution strongly matches their norm of “full cooperation.” They do not appear to use reward strategically to curry favor or avoid punishment, since they continue to reward even after suffering punishments and without obvious payoff benefit. The behavior is consistent with a prosocial, norm-reinforcing type.

This player is an active, selectively generous rewarder who uses rewards as a normative and relational tool rather than a pure payoff-maximizing instrument. They repeatedly incur personal cost to reward others, even when doing so cannot rationally increase their own material payoff given the one-to-one reward cost and their already maximal contributions. This reflects a willingness to sacrifice private earnings to acknowledge and encourage prosocial behavior. They do not reward mechanically every round; instead, their reward spending fluctuates. The pattern suggests they are responsive to perceived cooperation levels and to the broader cooperative climate. When facing a clear defector alongside a consistent cooperator, they continue to cooperate and still spend on rewards, which strongly implies that their rewards target cooperators rather than defectors. While the interface hides identities of rewarders and who receives what, the player persists in rewarding, indicating they may view rewards as generalized support for “good group members” rather than as explicit bilateral trades. Their use of reward appears to serve several roles: - **Positive reinforcement of norms:** They behave as if rewarding is a way to uphold a “full contribution” standard and to sustain the cooperative equilibrium, even though the direct financial return is weak or negative. - **Gratitude and approval signaling:** They spend on rewards when others also contribute fully, suggesting they want to express approval and possibly gratitude for shared cooperation, independent of strict instrumental benefit. - **Resilience to exploitation:** Even after observing a peer free-ride, they do not turn off rewards or contributions; instead, they continue to participate in the reward system. This points to a forgiving or resilient personality that does not quickly retaliate or withdraw positive engagement. Strategically, they appear to treat rewards as a long-run investment in group cohesion rather than as a calculated, short-term optimization tool. They accept lower own payoffs to maintain a cooperative, supportive atmosphere, aligning with a “prosocial, norm-enforcing cooperator” type who endorses both high contribution and positive reinforcement, and who is reluctant to punish or disengage even when others underperform.

This player is effectively a non-rewarder. Across the interaction, they never spend their own coins to reward anyone, even when surrounded by consistently high contributors and even after receiving rewards themselves. Their non-use of the reward mechanism is not easily explained by a belief that reward is financially inefficient, since here the cost and benefit are matched one-for-one. A purely monetary maximizer would be indifferent, and someone interested in shaping others’ behavior might try using reward at least once. Their consistent abstention indicates either that they discount the impact of reward on future behavior, that they view rewarding as outside their role or responsibility, or that they prefer to free-ride on whatever motivational effects others’ rewards provide. Receiving rewards does not change their stance: after being rewarded, they do not reciprocate by rewarding back or by rewarding others more broadly. This suggests that gratitude signaling via the explicit reward channel is not part of their behavioral repertoire. If they “thank” others at all, they do so only implicitly through continued contributions, not by spending to transfer coins. They also do not attempt strategic reinforcement of specific partners. Even when there are clearly high and low contributors, they never target the high contributors with reward to stabilize cooperation or to encourage particular individuals. This implies either that they see the group as a homogeneous mass (not worth differentiating), or that they prefer to let the contribution and redistribution mechanism stand on its own without adding another layer of incentives. Taken together, their type on the reward dimension is “passive beneficiary of others’ incentive efforts.” They accept rewards when given but never initiate them, they do not use the mechanism to shape norms or build alliances, and they appear to rely solely on their contribution choices (not costly rewards) to express their cooperative stance or respond to group behavior.

This player is extremely reluctant to spend on rewarding others. Across the game, they almost never incur the cost to reward, despite often being a high contributor in groups that also contain other high contributors. Their default stance is to keep all remaining coins rather than use them to reinforce cooperation. When they do spend on rewards, it happens very rarely and at a low level relative to the total number of opportunities, suggesting a strong bias toward self-payoff preservation once the contribution stage is complete. Their sparse reward use does not clearly map onto consistent observable triggers such as a sharp increase in others’ cooperation or a clear “all cooperate” group configuration. Because rewards are anonymous and symmetric in cost and benefit, reward usage here is not strategically efficient in a narrow sense; the fact that the player almost always avoids it suggests either a calculation that rewards add little marginal benefit to group behavior, or a preference structure where contributing to the public good is morally salient but engaging in costly interpersonal reward is not. There is weak evidence that they sometimes experiment with or respond to the reward institution when they themselves receive substantial rewards from others: on a small number of occasions they spend a modest amount on rewarding after having been rewarded relatively generously. However, this relation is low-confidence: the timing is sparse, and the data do not show a robust pattern of tit-for-tat gratitude or systematic reciprocity. Overall, they do not appear to use reward as a strategic lever to shape others’ future contributions or to send strong social signals. Their social reinforcement style is thus “cooperate-by-default, reward-rarely.” Incentive-wise, they seem to separate the two stages: in contribution, they accept personal sacrifice to increase the public fund; in reward, they behave closer to a self-interested type, keeping their payoff instead of further investing in others. This asymmetry suggests that their core cooperative identity is expressed through direct contributions to the shared project, not through targeted, costly interpersonal incentives. Their use of rewards is best characterized as minimal, occasional, and non-systematic, with no clear conditional rule detectable from the transcript.

They almost never spend on reward and clearly treat the reward mechanism as economically inefficient. For most of the game they spend nothing, even when others are handing out substantial rewards. This indicates that they prioritize maximizing their own payoff within a cooperative framework and do not see costly rewards as necessary to sustain cooperation in a high-performing group. On a small number of occasions they do spend minimally on reward, but these instances are rare and modest; there is no clear pattern of targeted strategic reinforcement (for example, no clear reactionary rewarding of former defectors or heavy contributors). Because rewarders are anonymous, their sparse spending suggests they are not trying to cultivate an individual reputation but may occasionally reward out of generalized goodwill or a sense of fairness when everyone is cooperating. They accept rewards from others without comment and never reciprocate in a visible, systematic way. Overall, they are a “cheap” cooperator on the reward dimension: strongly cooperative in contributions but highly reluctant to incur personal costs to further reward others when cooperation is already high.

For much of the game, they do not spend on rewards at all and explicitly frame the reward technology as inefficient: they understand that each unit of reward costs them more than it gives the recipient. This indicates they attend to payoff structure and initially lean toward not using costly incentives when they seem individually suboptimal. However, later they spend substantial amounts on rewards in a few rounds, despite no obvious change in others’ contribution behavior. Because individual reward targets are not observable, their exact pattern is unknown, but the timing suggests they may be using rewards as a form of end‑game generosity or gratitude toward the group rather than as a strategic lever to change behavior. Their own reward receipt is inconsistent across rounds, yet they still eventually give sizeable rewards, which implies they are not strictly reciprocity‑driven. Overall, they are cautious and payoff‑sensitive about reward use early, but willing to incur significant costs later, likely to express appreciation or reinforce a positive group experience rather than to optimize earnings.

They never spend any of their own coins on rewards in any round, despite the presence of a functioning reward mechanism and substantial reward activity by several peers. They nevertheless receive rewards from others multiple times, indicating that they benefit from the system but choose not to reciprocate materially. This pattern suggests that, in this environment, they view the reward mechanism as inefficient or not worth the cost relative to its impact on payoffs, and they are not motivated to use it for reciprocity or gratitude signaling. Their earlier statements focus solely on maximizing bonuses through contributions, never mentioning rewards as a tool for coordination or enforcement. They also do not adjust contribution behavior in response to gaining or not gaining rewards, further indicating that rewards are irrelevant to their strategy. Their “type” therefore is a non‑rewarding, efficiency‑driven cooperator who prefers to conserve private payoff rather than invest in costly positive sanctions, even when others are generous.

They never spend anything on rewards, despite repeated opportunities and despite others sometimes using rewards generously, including “gift‑splurges” by certain players. Their own behavior is 100% cooperative but 0% rewarding. This indicates a clear reluctance to incur personal costs solely to transfer payoff to others, even when those others are fully cooperative and when norm‑reinforcing rewards are common in the group. The behavior is consistent with a payoff‑conserving or efficiency‑minded stance: since the reward mechanism has a net negative efficiency effect (it costs more to give than the recipient gains), they appear to treat it as generally not worth using. They also do not use rewards symbolically (e.g., to thank high contributors), even though they sometimes receive small rewards from others. Overall, they rely on contribution behavior itself, not costly prosocial side‑payments, as their main cooperative signal. Their use of the reward mechanism appears purely instrumental and they choose not to deploy it at all.

They never spend any coins on rewards, despite repeated opportunities and visible examples of others using rewards to thank or encourage cooperators. This suggests a strong reluctance to incur personal costs for second‑order cooperation in this environment. They appear to treat reward as individually irrational given the unfavorable exchange rate, and they do not engage in symbolic or “cheap” rewarding even in final rounds, despite the anonymity of rewarders and a highly cooperative group. Receiving rewards from others in several rounds does not induce any reciprocation or reward activity from them. Their pattern fits a “first‑order cooperator, second‑order free‑rider”: they contribute fully to the public good but free‑ride on others’ willingness to reward and sustain norms. If they have a strategy about rewards, it is to ignore the mechanism as an inefficient use of resources.

They never spend any resources to reward others, despite the availability and use of this mechanism by multiple peers. Their choice remains unchanged across distinct environments of contribution and reward behavior by others. This indicates a strict separation between their willingness to contribute to the public good and their willingness to engage in targeted positive reciprocity. They do not use rewards to encourage high contributors, to reciprocate reward, or to build cooperative reputations. This could reflect one or more of: a preference to keep payoff resources once contributed decisions are made, a belief that the reward mechanism is inefficient or irrelevant, low engagement with strategic signaling, or simply indifference to the social leverage that rewards could provide. Overall, they are a pure contributor and a non-rewarder.

This player is willing to spend on rewards but does so selectively and at a moderate level rather than indiscriminately. They clearly recognize and use the reward mechanism, including directing some reward to themselves, which indicates attention to both social signaling and personal payoff. Their own self-reward shows they are not purely altruistic; they are willing to use available tools to boost their own outcome when it does not require social approval. At the same time, they also allocate reward to others, indicating some desire to acknowledge or reinforce behavior they view as positive. The pattern of reward is not extensive relative to their high level of contribution, suggesting that reward is a secondary tool rather than their primary cooperative strategy. They do not engage in reward-based retaliation (since punishment is unavailable), and there is no sign that they spend reward resources to counter free riders indirectly (e.g., by over-rewarding cooperators to offset defectors). Their reward behavior looks more like targeted appreciation and mild strategic self-gain than a systematic attempt to reshape group incentives.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when others around them spend resources to reward high contributors. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur any personal cost to shape others’ behavior or to express approval. Rewarding is not part of their strategic toolkit; they either do not value the potential future benefits of reinforcing cooperation or do not believe their individual rewards would matter enough to justify the cost. They also do not appear to use reward as a signal of gratitude or alliance formation. High contributors and active rewarders receive no reinforcement from this player. This suggests that social signaling, reputation-building, and norm-enforcement via positive incentives are low priorities. Their approach to reward is strictly self-regarding: if it costs them, they abstain. There is no observable pattern of selective or strategic reward (for instance, rewarding particularly high contributors or those who might reciprocate). As such, their “trigger” for rewarding appears to be effectively absent; they act as a pure non-rewarder.

They never use the reward mechanism, even when surrounded by many high contributors and visible reward activity by others. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur any personal cost for social reinforcement, even for clearly cooperative peers. They do not appear to use reward strategically to influence future behavior, build goodwill, or reciprocate group norms. Their default stance is to preserve their own payoff rather than trade off even a small amount to shape the social environment. This points to a narrow, short-term payoff focus and a low propensity for altruistic or strategic rewarding. Whether this reflects disbelief in the effectiveness of rewards, indifference to others’ welfare, or simple inattention is unknown, but the behavioral pattern is strictly non-rewarding.

This player does not spend any resources on rewarding others, despite having the opportunity and seeing others make both high and low contributions, and despite themselves being a high contributor. They receive rewards from others but do not reciprocate or use reward to reinforce either cooperative partners or norm violators (via differential rewarding). This suggests a general reluctance to engage with the reward mechanism, rather than a targeted or strategic use of it. Their non-use of reward could stem from several possible motives, which cannot be distinguished with high confidence: a preference to avoid additional strategic complexity; a belief that reward is inefficient or not worth the cost; or a focus on contributing directly to the public good rather than shaping others’ behavior. They appear not to use reward to signal gratitude, build alliances, or encourage high contributors. Overall, they are a non-rewarder / abstainer from positive sanctions, even while being highly cooperative in contributions.

This player is generally cautious with reward but becomes selectively willing to spend when peers’ behaviors diverge more clearly. They do not reward by default and do not reward in proportion to others’ exact contribution levels; instead, they reward only in situations where they likely infer especially noteworthy cooperative behavior or possibly to encourage specific players. Their reward pattern is sparse and targeted, indicating that they view rewarding as a tool to shape behavior rather than as a routine expression of generosity. The fact that they are willing to incur a nontrivial personal cost to reward at all implies some weight on social preferences or norm reinforcement, but the low overall intensity suggests they continue to prioritize preserving their own payoff. They do not reward themselves (not possible here) and receive no reward from others, so their decision to reward is not a straightforward reciprocity response. They instead appear to use reward strategically to support others’ cooperation, albeit in a minimalist way, rather than as a broad signal of friendliness or gratitude.

This player never uses the reward option, even when many others do and when there are clear high contributors present. They show a zero-reward pattern across opportunities, which indicates a strong reluctance to spend resources on rewarding, regardless of others’ contributions or how they themselves are treated. Their behavior suggests they do not use reward as a strategic tool to reinforce cooperation, even when that might credibly encourage pro-social partners. They also do not use reward as an expression of gratitude or as a social signal; they remain completely inactive in this mechanism. Possible motives (all low-confidence, as internal reasoning is unobserved): - They may prioritize conserving coins over influencing others. - They may view rewarding as inefficient or unnecessary given the short horizon. - They may be socially reserved or disinterested in forming explicit alliances via reward. What is clear from behavior alone is that they separate contribution from reward: they are willing to sacrifice to help the group via contributions, but not willing to spend any extra to differentially benefit individuals through the reward channel.

This player is selectively willing to incur costs to reward others, but only after observing an additional round of behavior. Initially they refrain from rewarding anyone despite clear high contributions by several others. Later, they begin to use rewards in a targeted way. Their rewards go to players who exhibit strong cooperative behavior, not to themselves or to clear free-riders, indicating that the trigger for rewarding is substantial contribution to the public good rather than reciprocity of being rewarded or simple equality. They use rewards sparsely and at low cost, which suggests they value the idea of reinforcing cooperation but keep that secondary to their own payoff. The pattern is consistent with strategic reinforcement rather than impulsive generosity: they appear to use rewards to acknowledge particularly cooperative behavior and possibly to signal approval and encourage such behavior, while avoiding broad or unconditional rewarding. This is a weak, selective prosocial-rewarder profile.

They never spend any coins on rewards. This is true even though they frequently contribute at the maximum, sometimes while others do not, and even though the reward technology is individually beneficial in expectation (giving up 4 to give 5). This suggests one or more of the following motivations, with uncertain weighting: a preference for keeping their own payoffs; skepticism about the marginal effect of rewards in this context; inattention or low interest in that mechanism; or a normative view that cooperation itself is expected and does not merit extra payment. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to encourage laggards or to strengthen bonds with other cooperators, despite having clear candidates to reward. The absence of any reward use, combined with their steady cooperation, indicates that they prioritize simple, norm-based contribution over more complex incentive engineering.

They never spend any coins on rewards. This holds throughout, even when they benefit heavily from others’ cooperation and when reward is known to be slightly socially efficient (cost–benefit ratio above 1). For them, the reward mechanism is effectively ignored as a tool for norm enforcement or gratitude. The absence of rewarding persists even when they themselves receive rewards on multiple occasions. They neither reciprocate to benefactors nor use reward to reinforce high contributors. This rules out a strategy of selective or “cheap” reciprocity via reward. Instead, their behavior suggests a personally payoff-maximizing stance toward the reward mechanism: any cost, however slightly efficient socially, is avoided. This implies they do not actively invest in sustaining cooperation via institutional tools; they are willing to ride on others’ use of rewards but will not spend to maintain norm compliance. From a strategic standpoint, they act as a “reward free-rider.”

This player never spends any coins on rewards. Even when other players are clearly contributing fully and when some reward flows are present in the group, they refrain from incurring personal costs to reinforce cooperation. Their refusal to use the reward mechanism persists even after they themselves receive rewards, which rules out simple ignorance of the tool. This suggests they either view rewarding as not worth the personal cost, or they prioritize maintaining their own payoff over actively shaping others’ behavior. They do not appear to use rewards instrumentally (for example, to encourage waverers or to thank consistent cooperators). Their approach fits a “non-enforcer cooperator”: they contribute to the public good but leave the burden of costly norm enforcement to others.

They never spend any coins on rewards in the entire game. This is consistent and stable despite clear opportunities and a technology that is mildly efficient. Their revealed preference is to keep all their own payoff rather than incur costs to positively reinforce cooperators or to strategically “buy” more future cooperation. This suggests either skepticism about the effectiveness of the reward mechanism, a strong concern for not losing additional coins on top of what they already sacrifice by cooperating, or low intrinsic motivation to reward others even when they appreciate their contributions verbally. They do not appear to use rewards instrumentally (e.g., to shape gorilla’s behavior) or symbolically (e.g., to thank high contributors), even after being rewarded themselves; they compartmentalize material and verbal responses, relying on chat for social signaling rather than the costly mechanism. Their strategy is thus “cooperate and talk, but do not pay to reward.”

They almost never use the reward mechanism, despite being a high contributor surrounded by mostly high contributors. Their default is to spend nothing on rewards; only one round shows them spending on reward, and even then they do not establish any systematic pattern of rewarding cooperative peers. They receive rewards a couple of times from others, but do not reciprocate or build visible tit-for-tat reward exchanges. This strongly suggests that, relative to contribution, they see rewards as low-priority or inefficient, even though the tech is favorable. Their strategy appears to be to promote norms verbally and through their own contributions rather than by paying to reinforce others. They do not use reward as a tool to target the stubborn free-rider either, even when that player is clearly identified by the group; they simply ignore reward as a lever, indicating either that they view it as not worth the cost, or that they mentally compartmentalize “playing correctly” (contribute) from “meta-tools” (reward).

The player does use the reward mechanism, but infrequently and selectively. Most rounds they choose not to spend on rewards despite many others rewarding quite actively. When they do reward, they spend nontrivial amounts in concentrated bursts rather than distributing small amounts broadly, indicating that rewards are deliberate interventions, not routine “tips.” Because the log does not show exactly whom they reward, only that they sometimes devote resources to rewarding, the most we can say is: they are willing to incur personal cost to influence behavior, but they do so sparingly. Given that they themselves are a consistent high contributor, and that most reward activity in the group targets contributors, it is plausible (but not certain) that their rewards are directed toward encouraging cooperative or helpful players rather than to curry favor with defectors. Their late-game reward spending, despite cumulative payoff deficits relative to free-riders, suggests that any rewarding they do is more aligned with reinforcing group norms than with maximizing their own final total. Strategically, they treat reward as a supplementary, occasional tool rather than as a central lever for coordination or reciprocity.

Willingness to reward: The player never spends any of their own coins on rewards, despite multiple opportunities and despite the presence of clearly higher-contributing peers. Their revealed preference is to avoid any costly pro-social transfers. Triggers (or lack thereof): Even in situations where a peer’s contribution clearly increases the group fund and arguably benefits the player, they do not respond with rewards. This holds regardless of whether they themselves cooperated or defected in that round. Thus, neither gratitude for others’ cooperation nor guilt about free-riding translates into costly rewarding behavior. Strategic reinforcement: There is no behavioral evidence that the player attempts to use rewards as a strategic tool to reinforce cooperation. They behave as if the reward mechanism is irrelevant to their strategy. They free-ride on others’ rewarding behavior instead, accepting incoming rewards (when given by others) but never reciprocating with their own costly rewards. Gratitude signaling: Although the player receives rewards from others in several rounds, they do not mirror this with any outgoing reward behavior. This suggests: - Low priority on expressing gratitude via costly signals. - Willingness to accept benefits from others without engaging in reciprocal rewarding. Any internal sense of appreciation, if present, does not manifest in action. Beliefs about the reward system (inferred): The player likely views rewarding as individually unprofitable: the cost to them is salient, while the benefit (increased cooperation by others) is either doubted or discounted. Because they cannot observe others’ final payoffs and cannot identify who rewards whom, they may conclude that their own rewards would have limited marginal effect. This belief, combined with their payoff focus, yields a strictly non-rewarding stance. Personality/style in reward domain: In future PGGs with similar reward mechanics, this player: - Does not initiate rewarding behavior, even toward consistent cooperators. - Accepts rewards but treats them as windfalls rather than as part of a reciprocal exchange. - Relies on others, if any, to bear the cost of sustaining cooperation through rewards. They are best characterized as a “reward free-rider”: they exploit the presence of a reward system when others use it but never invest in it themselves.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, even when other group members contribute fully and sometimes reward them. This reveals an extremely low willingness to incur personal costs to encourage or acknowledge cooperative behavior. They show no observable gratitude via the reward channel: being rewarded does not lead them to reciprocate toward high contributors or toward likely rewarders. Their use of the reward mechanism is purely abstention: they allow others to bear both the contribution and reward costs, while they capture private benefits from public contributions and from incoming rewards. This positions them as a “second‑order free‑rider” as well: they free‑ride not only on contribution but also on the costly act of rewarding cooperation. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to stabilize cooperation (for example, by selectively reinforcing consistent contributors). They do not attempt to shape others’ behavior through incentives, even in phases where the group is relatively cooperative. This suggests either that they view the reward technology as not personally profitable, or that they simply adopt a minimal‑effort, purely self‑regarding rule like “never pay for others.” Because they receive rewards from others yet never return them, it is plausible (though not certain) that they either (a) feel no social obligation to reciprocate, or (b) treat rewards as anonymous windfalls that do not call for response. In new PGG environments, this type is expected to withhold both contributions and rewards, regardless of the availability or generosity of reward mechanisms, and to rely entirely on others, if any, to provide both public goods and cooperative incentives.

This player is an **active, selective rewarder who uses rewards as a strategic and expressive tool rather than purely as payoff maximization**. They are clearly **willing to spend meaningful resources on rewarding others**, even when this is costly relative to their own round payoff. This willingness appears tied to peers’ cooperative behavior: high contributors are more likely to be rewarded, especially when the group outcome would otherwise be discouraging. The player uses reward as a way to **reinforce or encourage contribution** rather than distributing it indiscriminately. Reward decisions are **not purely self-interested** in a narrow sense. At times, they sacrifice immediate payoff to increase others’ earnings, even when there is no visible guarantee of future reciprocity and no visibility of who is rewarding whom. This implies they value **supporting or “thanking” cooperation** in its own right, and/or they hold a belief that rewarding cooperators can stabilize contributions over time even if responses are uncertain. However, their reward behavior is **not rigidly consistent**. They do not always reward when others cooperate, and they sometimes withhold rewards even in cooperative environments. This variability suggests that they are both experimenting with the impact of rewards and conserving resources when they judge additional incentives to be redundant or ineffective. The pattern is consistent with **strategic reinforcement**: reward when cooperation looks fragile or underappreciated, spend less when cooperative behavior seems already established or when others are unresponsive. They do not exhibit any clear pattern of rewarding defectors, and there is no evidence that they use rewards as “bribes” for low contributors. Instead, their observed pattern fits a **pro-social, conditional rewarder** who aims to recognize or bolster cooperative partners rather than to manipulate non-cooperators. Taken together, this player’s reward behavior portrays a **cost-accepting encourager**: someone who is willing to pay to express approval of cooperative actions and to try to nudge the group toward higher contributions, while still balancing that impulse against their own material outcomes.

They never spend any resources on rewards throughout the game. Despite the presence of very high and very consistent contributors—and clear, repeated pro-social reward behavior by others—they do not once incur the cost required to reward anyone. This implies a strong reluctance to sacrifice own payoff for the sake of reinforcing others’ cooperation. They do not use the reward mechanism either as a gratitude signal, as an investment in future group cooperation, or as a means of coordinating with other rewarders. Even when they benefit heavily from others’ generosity and the group’s high contributions, they withhold reward entirely. Strategically, this places them as a pure consumer of the incentive environment created by others: they let others undertake the costly task of rewarding cooperators, then respond only through their own contribution choices (and even there, inconsistently). There is no evidence that higher reward activity by others triggers more generous behavior from them via reward; the reward channel is behaviorally inactive for this player. Their unwillingness to reward is consistent with a narrowly self-payoff-focused orientation and/or skepticism about the effectiveness of costly positive incentives. Whether this stems from a principled stance (e.g., “never pay to influence others”) or simple indifference to others’ outcomes is unknown, but the behavioral implication is that they contribute nothing to the maintenance of social incentives via reward.

This player is a heavy and persistent user of the reward mechanism. They very frequently spend on reward, often at substantial personal cost, including in cases where this clearly lowers their own material payoff relative to others. They do not appear to focus on cost-effectiveness of reward: the reward technology is inefficient, yet they repeatedly invest in it. Their reward-giving is not tightly contingent on any obvious short-term pattern of others’ contributions; they continue to reward even when the group’s contribution level is unstable and when free-riding persists. This suggests they use reward primarily as a normative signal of approval or as an expression of a cooperative ideal, rather than as a narrowly strategic device to maximize future payoffs. They do not systematically withdraw reward after seeing others fail to converge to high, stable cooperation; instead, they keep returning to rewarding behavior across the game, with only occasional pauses. Their readiness to absorb large costs to reward implies strong pro-social or principled tendencies, low sensitivity to exploitation risk in the context of reward, and possibly a belief that consistent positive reinforcement may eventually shape others’ behavior, even if that is not clearly confirmed in the data. They also accept and incorporate received rewards without altering their own giving standard, suggesting that gratitude does not change their already-maximal contributions but may help sustain their commitment to rewarding.

This player never uses the reward option. Across all observed situations—including extreme cases where some players repeatedly sacrifice substantial payoff to reward high contributors—they still choose not to spend anything on rewarding. They does not engage in strategic reinforcement of cooperation, even though others demonstrate a clear informal norm of using rewards to support high contributors and, occasionally, to favor specific partners. The player also does not use rewards to thank or align with those who transfer rewards to them; even after receiving reward income, they continues to avoid reciprocation. Their zero-reward stance suggests that: - They prioritizes payoff maximization narrowly at the individual level, declining even “symbolic” or low-cost rewards. - They does not adopt the social norm of rewarding high contributors that emerges among others. - They either does not believe that their rewards would meaningfully shape others’ behavior, or does not care to shape it, or both (confidence moderate; this is inferred from consistent non-use of a widely used tool). There is no evidence of targeted or selective rewarding; their policy is simply non-participation in the reward mechanism.

This player is an extremely heavy and persistent user of costly rewards. They are willing to spend large portions—and sometimes the entirety—of their private payoff to reward others, even when this is clearly disadvantageous materially. They repeatedly sacrifice significant own-payoff to provide relatively small benefits to others, indicating strong pro-social or norm-enforcing motives that override simple cost-benefit calculations. Their rewards are not random: they are closely tied to others’ contribution behavior. High contributors, particularly the consistently maximal cooperator, receive frequent and substantial rewards from this player. Low contributors or non-contributors generally do not receive reward support from them. This suggests that reward is used as a positive sanction to endorse and reinforce cooperative norms, rather than as a social gift or reciprocity for being rewarded personally. They also show a pattern of using reward even in rounds where their own contribution is low or zero. When they temporarily contribute little themselves, they still direct reward toward the high contributor. This implies that reward is not merely reciprocation for others’ kindness toward them personally, but is consciously aimed at sustaining or acknowledging group-beneficial behavior. In those moments, they act more like a “sponsor” of the cooperative type than a purely mutual-exchange partner. The player does not appear to adjust reward spending downward in response to poor personal payoffs or to the apparent inefficiency of the reward technology. Instead, they often escalate or maintain high reward spending over time. This indicates either (a) a strong intrinsic satisfaction from rewarding cooperators or (b) a belief—perhaps optimistic—that sufficiently strong positive reinforcement can shift group norms toward higher cooperation, even if that has not clearly materialized yet. Their reward strategy is thus more principled than reactive: they reward to uphold a standard, not because it visibly “works” in a narrow instrumental sense. Gratitude toward those who reward them is present but not dominant. They do receive rewards from at least one partner and sometimes appear to reciprocate, but their reward pattern is more tightly linked to the recipient’s contribution level than to the recipient’s spending on rewards. Overall, they behave like a “moral rewarder”: they pay to support high contributors, tolerate high personal cost to do so, and only weakly condition reward on personal gain.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite repeated opportunities and despite many peers contributing heavily. This is not a cost-efficiency issue—reward is cost-neutral—but they still consistently refrain. They also accept being rewarded without explicit reciprocation in some periods, suggesting they do not treat reward as a strict tit‑for‑tat ledger but as a tool to sustain group cooperation and signal approval for contributors. Over time, they reduce or stop spending on rewards in at least some later situations, which likely reflects adaptation to perceived inefficiency or fatigue with subsidizing others, but they never flip to rewarding defectors. Overall, they use reward as positive reinforcement for norm‑compliant behavior and as a collective substitute for formal punishment of norm violators.

Their use of rewards is selective, norm-based, and clearly conditional on cooperation and perceived fairness. Early on, they state that they reward everyone except the persistent free-rider, explicitly tying reward to contribution behavior and trying to use reward exclusion as a soft sanction. They view reward as a tool both to support cooperators and to pressure defectors rather than as a purely altruistic transfer. They do not reward themselves (self-reward is impossible here) and they do not appear to reward the free-rider despite that player’s consistently higher payoffs, which indicates they are not chasing reciprocity from exploiters; they draw a qualitative line between cooperators and defectors. They are cost-tolerant with respect to rewarding: in later rounds they spend nontrivial amounts on reward, even when they have previously complained about not getting much reward themselves. This suggests they are willing to incur efficiency losses to sustain cooperative norms. They also seek information about who is continuing to reward the free-rider, indicating concern that reward be aligned with contribution norms; they want the reward system to reinforce cooperation properly and are frustrated when others “misuse” it.

They are very generous and systematic with rewards, frequently spending substantial coins to reward many different cooperative players within a round. Reward decisions track cooperative behavior: high contributors and visible norm supporters repeatedly receive rewards from them, and they expand the reward set when the persistent free-rider finally contributes, signaling approval and positive reinforcement. Rewards are distributed not only to a tight inner circle but also broadly to many cooperators, suggesting an attempt to sustain a culture of high contribution, not just pay back personal favors. They seem comfortable investing heavily in rewards even when they cannot observe detailed payoffs, implying they treat rewards as both gratitude signals and strategic reinforcement for pro-social norms. Their reward behavior is complementary to their punishment style: punish clear free-riders, reward clear cooperators, and use both levers to shape group behavior rather than to maximize their own short-term income.

They are an active, generous user of rewards, especially toward clear cooperators and coalition partners. When they do reward, they often spread rewards widely across nearly all full contributors who have been aligned with the cooperative norm, rather than favoring a narrow subset. This suggests they use rewards as a social recognition tool for groupwide cooperation, not just as a transactional exchange. They also employ more targeted rewards for specific allies who support high contributions and punitive strategies against free‑riders, signaling appreciation and reinforcing pro‑social leadership. Their reward timing often coincides with notable cooperative milestones (e.g., when a chronic defector finally contributes, or when many players sustain full cooperation). In those moments, they are willing to spend a significant amount on rewards even when the direct monetary return is modest or negative relative to keeping coins, indicating they value relational and normative reinforcement. Strategically, they see rewards as complementing punishment: punishment is used to deter and discipline free‑riders; rewards are used to encourage and affirm cooperators. They do not attempt to “bribe” the main defector with rewards; instead, rewards flow to those already following the norm. This pattern fits a “pro‑social norm entrepreneur” type: they try to shape the environment so that cooperation is both materially and socially rewarded, while defection is both costly and socially disapproved.

The player is an active and flexible rewarder, using positive incentives across many rounds on multiple targets. They reward both consistent cooperators and, importantly, the main defector in specific moments when that defector cooperates or appears to shift. This shows an attempt at carrot-and-stick regulation: punishment when defection persists, reward when cooperation appears. They also at times reward players who had just contributed zero or reduced amounts, especially when there are social or strategic reasons (e.g., attempting to encourage inclusion, to reinforce a momentary deviation toward cooperation, or possibly to experiment with how incentives affect behavior). Their rewards are dispersed across several group members rather than concentrated on a single ally or clear partner, which suggests they are not building a narrow reciprocity coalition but instead trying to reinforce pro-group or norm-improving behavior broadly. They spend nontrivial amounts on reward over the game, indicating a willingness to pay to shape others’ actions, not just to express approval. They do not publicly announce their rewarding strategy, but their pattern is consistent with someone who uses positive reinforcement as complement to punishment—an “institutionalizer” type who tries to stabilize cooperation through both sanctions and rewards rather than relying solely on one tool.

They are very willing to reward and, by the middle and later parts of the game, use rewarding at scale. They give positive transfers to essentially all other active cooperators in several rounds, even when this is personally costly. Their reward behavior is clearly tied to cooperative or group‑benefiting actions: they reward consistent full contributors, those who publicly support cooperation, and those who finally switch from defection to cooperation. They also once reward a player who had previously been a problem defector once that player contributes fully, indicating a readiness to forgive and positively reinforce behavior change. Rewarding appears to serve two functions: (1) reinforcing cooperative norms and group cohesion, and (2) expressing gratitude and solidarity. They are willing to spend substantial coins to broadcast approval widely, not just to key allies, which suggests that they value a cooperative climate in itself and not only narrow reciprocity. They continue to reward even when deductions against them increase, implying that their positive reinforcement is not contingent on being personally favored. Strategically, they seem to treat reward as at least as important as punishment for sustaining cooperation: they alternate between sanctioning the chronic free rider and generously rewarding the cooperative majority, thereby shaping an environment where helping the group both avoids sanctions and earns extra benefits. This pattern is consistent with a “prosocial norm enforcer” type who uses both sticks and carrots, with a slight bias toward inclusive, forgiving reinforcement once others align with cooperative expectations.

They virtually never use the explicit reward mechanism despite repeatedly benefiting from others’ high contributions. There is no positive reinforcement directed toward high contributors, even though they clearly value the cooperative equilibrium and praise it verbally. Their “rewards” are instead purely verbal: thanking people indirectly via enthusiasm about group gains and celebrating when the free-rider finally cooperates. The absence of paid rewards appears strategic rather than due to ignorance: they understand all mechanisms and think actively about relative costs and payoffs, but choose to spend coins on punishment only. This suggests they prioritize deterring and penalizing norm violators over incentivizing cooperators, perhaps because they perceive the cooperative norm as already well-established and self-enforcing among most players. Gratitude is expressed in chat (“finally,” “peer pressure ftw”) rather than in costly actions. In new environments, this type is therefore best described as a vocal norm enforcer who relies on punishment and social pressure, not on material rewards, to shape others’ behavior.

They also never use the reward mechanism, despite repeatedly receiving rewards themselves. They experience both deductions and positive transfers but do not reciprocate in either direction, suggesting that they do not treat the secondary mechanism as a reciprocity channel. They do not explicitly discuss reward use or its effectiveness, even as others talk extensively about punishment; thus the absence of reward from them looks like a general preference not to spend on social tools, rather than a selective focus on negative sanctions. Given that they are a steadfast full contributor, their refusal to reward high contributors is not due to disagreement with cooperative norms but likely due to either (a) a strong focus on maximizing their own final payoff once their cooperative decision is made, or (b) a belief that full contribution is simply the baseline expectation and does not warrant extra reinforcement. Overall, they behave as a “pure public-good provider”: they contribute at maximum, rely solely on the contribution stage to influence outcomes, and refrain from paying to modify others’ incentives via rewards.

They use rewards frequently, but narrowly focused on reinforcing a specific high-cooperating, socially central player. Across the game, they repeatedly reward this same cooperative partner in different phases, including when others start contributing less. They never reward themselves (not allowed) or spread rewards across all cooperators; instead, they single out one consistent contributor, likely perceived as both norm-abiding and prosocial in chat. Their rewards are sometimes modest and sometimes substantial, indicating genuine willingness to pay to strengthen that relationship or signal appreciation and solidarity. They do not condition their rewarding on immediate changes in contributions by others; they continue to reward the same player even after group behavior fluctuates, suggesting they value that individual’s reliability rather than using rewards purely as short-term incentives. They do not use reward as a carrot toward the persistent defector; they focus reward on reinforcing “good citizens,” not converting “bad apples.” Overall, they are an active, relationship-oriented rewarder who uses positive reinforcement selectively to support and recognize core cooperators.

This player effectively does not use reward strategically. Across the game they never allocate positive transfers to others, despite being embedded in a consistently high-contribution group and seeing many peers who persistently contribute large amounts. They thereby pass up opportunities to reinforce desirable behavior, even when such reinforcement might be cheap relative to the group’s high earnings. At the same time, they occasionally receive rewards from others but never reciprocate. The absence of reciprocation, even in periods when they themselves are contributing at high levels, suggests that gratitude or reciprocal kindness is not a strong driver of their behavior. They do not appear to treat reward as a coordination or signaling device; instead, they ignore that part of the mechanism almost entirely. Taken together, this points to a type who is either uninterested in or skeptical about positive incentives as a tool for shaping group behavior. When they intervene socially, they do so through punishment alone. Their lack of reward use, even when others demonstrate high cooperation or offer them rewards, further reinforces the view that they are oriented more toward expressing dissatisfaction and leveling payoffs than toward building or sustaining cooperative norms via positive reinforcement.

The player is sparing and strategic with rewards. They reward rarely and only when some players stand out as particularly cooperative relative to others. The recipients tend to be high contributors in contexts where many others are contributing less, indicating that the player uses reward as a positive reinforcement tool for exemplary cooperation rather than a general subsidy for all decent behavior. They do not engage in broad, reciprocal reward-giving in response to being rewarded themselves; any such reciprocity is either absent or too subtle to infer with confidence. The low overall use of rewards, contrasted with a clear willingness to punish, implies a stronger orientation toward negative enforcement of norms than toward building alliances through positive incentives. At the same time, the existence of some rewards shows they are not purely punitive: they acknowledge and support high contributors when it seems especially important to encourage them. In summary, they is a reluctant but purposeful rewarder, using positive incentives selectively to bolster standout cooperators rather than as a primary tool of influence.

This player uses rewards sparingly and in a highly selective, exploratory way. Early on, they give small rewards to a subset of cooperative peers. Sometimes they reward those who contribute at or above the perceived group standard, and at least once they reward a very low contributor as well. This shows that rewards are not strictly reserved for the most cooperative actors; instead, the player appears to be experimenting with positive reinforcement in multiple directions, possibly to see whether specific individuals adjust their contributions after being rewarded. They do not maintain a consistent “reward high contributors” policy: in many rounds with strong group cooperation, they provide no rewards at all, even to those who repeatedly contribute generously. This suggests that rewards are not part of a stable norm‑enforcement strategy but are used occasionally when the player feels like testing how others respond, or when they feel transient appreciation or interest. Gratitude signaling toward those who previously benefited them is possible but unprovable from the data; the observable fact is only that they sometimes reward cooperators in a context of high contributions. Compared to their readiness to punish, their willingness to reward is lower and less persistent. They show no evidence of systematically pairing “carrot and stick”; rather, they sometimes reward without punishing, sometimes punish without rewarding, and often do neither, even in similar environments. This points to a personality and strategy that lean more heavily on sanctioning and experimentation than on sustained positive reinforcement. Overall, they act as a low‑frequency, opportunistic rewarder who uses small, one‑off rewards to probe or nudge rather than to build a long‑term reputation as a generous supporter.

The player almost never uses costly reward, with a single, small reward given once across the entire interaction. This isolated act goes to a peer whose prior behavior includes very low contributions. Since that reward does not align straightforwardly with standard “reward the high contributors” logic, its motive is ambiguous and confidence in interpretation is low. Possible interpretations (all low confidence): - It may be an attempt at encouragement or conditional forgiveness: paying a small amount to nudge a low contributor upward. - It may reflect a noisy or mistaken choice, not tightly linked to a consistent prosocial strategy. - It could be a weak signal of friendliness or experimentation with the mechanism, not sustained thereafter. Outside that instance, the player refrains from rewarding even clear high contributors, indicating they do not systematically reinforce cooperation or distribute surplus out of gratitude. They also do not respond in kind to receiving small rewards themselves, so reciprocal rewarding is absent. As a type, they are effectively non-rewarding in strategic terms. Their default is to keep all reward resources for themselves, treating the reward mechanism as rarely worth using. The lone reward instance suggests minimal curiosity or a one-off test rather than a stable preference for prosocial reinforcement.

The player is a selective but recurrent rewarder, using positive incentives as their only active social tool. They allocate rewards exclusively to others, never to themselves, so all rewarded transfers are genuine prosocial expenditures. They direct rewards toward players who appear to be contributing at least moderately and sometimes very generously, often focusing on those who are either stable cooperators or who may need encouragement to maintain or increase contributions. They do not reward every high contributor, nor do they use a strict rule such as “reward the top contributor(s).” Instead, they scatter small amounts among different cooperative peers over time. This pattern is consistent with an attempt to reinforce or acknowledge cooperative behavior from multiple group members rather than building a dyadic exchange with a single partner. The costliness of this behavior indicates a willingness to sacrifice some personal payoff to support or stabilize cooperation. They never mix reward and punishment; their social influence strategy is purely positive. This suggests a preference for supportive rather than punitive norm shaping. There is no explicit evidence that they expect direct reciprocity from particular reward recipients (recipients are anonymous), so their rewarding may be partly driven by a generalized reciprocity or “good citizen” motive, though this is uncertain. Strategically, they seem to use rewards as low-level signals of approval and as a way to nudge group norms toward higher cooperation while avoiding conflict.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite frequently observing high contributors and despite occasionally being rewarded themselves. They do not attempt to reinforce good behavior with positive incentives, even when punishment is costly and reward is comparatively symmetric in cost/impact. This suggests several possible, but uncertain, underlying motivations: they may see high contributions as merely expected rather than praiseworthy; they may believe punishment is more efficient or salient for shaping behavior; or they may prefer not to redistribute their private payoffs positively at all. From behavior alone, the safest inference is that they rely exclusively on negative enforcement to regulate the group and do not engage in gratitude signaling or strategic bonus-giving. They also do not seem to adjust their own strategy in visible response to being rewarded. The lack of rewarding behavior, combined with consistent high contributions and heavy use of punishment, points to a “strict enforcer” type: cooperative, norm-focused, and willing to sanction defectors, but uninterested in using rewards as a tool.

This player never uses the reward option, even in environments where many others contribute generously and where they themselves benefit substantially from high group cooperation. They therefore do not act as a positive sanctioning type who invests in encouraging high contributors. Their strategy seems to treat the contribution decision as the main lever of cooperation and efficiency, rather than using side payments. The absence of rewarding persists despite them receiving occasional rewards from others, suggesting that they do not reciprocate prosocial transfers in kind. This does not mean they are ungrateful in a psychological sense, but behaviorally they do not convert gratitude into costly bonus transfers. They likely view direct contributions to the public good as more natural or more efficient than individualized rewards, or they may see rewards as unnecessary given already high cooperation levels. It is also possible they are uncertain about how effectively anonymous rewards can shape behavior and thus avoid spending on them; this is speculative and thus low confidence. Overall, they are a “pure contributor” rather than a “sanctioning cooperator”: they rely on their own contribution level to express and support cooperation, and abstain from using institutional tools—both punishment and reward—to manage others’ actions.

This player consistently declines to use costly reward, even when others cooperate fully and even when one peer bears the full cost of contributing while another free-rides. There is no evidence that they try to positively reinforce high contributors or express gratitude via rewards. The absence of rewarding persists despite the availability of anonymous, relatively efficient rewards and despite clear examples of cooperative behavior by others. This pattern suggests that they either view costly reward as unnecessary, too inefficient, or strategically less valuable than simple reciprocation through their own contributions. They appear to rely on contribution choices and occasional punishment, not on prosocial incentives, to shape group behavior. Their approach to cooperation is thus more sanction-oriented than reward-oriented, though sanctions are themselves used sparingly.

The player never rewards others, including those who consistently cooperate. They do not use rewards to support existing cooperators or to try to influence the partial free-rider. Given the moderate inefficiency of rewards in this setting, it is unclear whether they avoid reward because of its costliness, because they view contributions as expected behavior rather than reward-worthy, or because they prefer to keep their role limited to contributing. There is no evidence of strategic rewarding to build alliances or signal gratitude. Overall, they behave as a pure contributor: they add to the public good but do not invest additional resources to shape others’ behavior through positive incentives.

They never reward anyone, including periods where others clearly behave cooperatively and generate high group payoffs from which they benefit disproportionately as a free-rider. This absence of rewarding persists even when rewarding could be interpreted as gratitude, norm reinforcement, or strategic encouragement of continued cooperation. Their refusal to reward under any circumstance reveals that they place very low value on positive reciprocity when it requires a personal cost. They do not appear to use reward as a tool to stabilize beneficial group behavior, relying instead on others’ intrinsic or habitual cooperation. Overall, they treat the reward channel as irrelevant to their strategy.

The player never uses the costly reward option and receives no observable reward from others. They show no willingness to incur a cost to reinforce cooperation, even when surrounded by high contributors whose behavior they benefit from. This suggests they place little to no value on reciprocity via positive incentives and do not attempt to strategically encourage future cooperation by others, despite the availability of that mechanism. Their non-use of rewards is consistent with a narrow payoff-maximizing orientation: paying to reward offers no direct material benefit in this short interaction, and they abstain entirely. There is no evidence of gratitude signaling, norm enforcement, or selective rewarding of high contributors; their stance is effectively “take the benefits, pay nothing extra,” both in contribution and in reward behavior.

They choose not to use the reward mechanism at any point. Given their explicit acceptance of the idea that contribution alone is sufficient for mutual benefit and the parallel argument that additional transfers are inefficient, their non-rewarding behavior fits a “pure efficiency” stance: they prioritize keeping resources rather than spending them to differentially reinforce others. They do not use rewards to thank high contributors, correct low contributors, or signal alliances. This indicates a type that relies on contribution norms, not on costly interpersonal incentives, to sustain cooperation. Whether they would ever reward in environments where rewarding is highly efficient or strongly norm-driven is unknown, but in the observed setting they consistently abstain.

They never uses the reward mechanism, even when others are contributing substantially and reward is available. This suggests they is highly cost-averse to prosocial reinforcement and does not sacrifice personal payoff to acknowledge or strengthen cooperative behavior. They appears to accept or benefit from others’ cooperation without reciprocating via rewards, implying a one-sided view of group benefit: rewards from them are not part of their strategy. There is no evidence they uses rewards for signaling gratitude, enforcing norms, or strategic reinforcement. Overall, their reward stance is strictly non-participatory and individually profit-maximizing, with no observable inclination to invest in others’ welfare or in future cooperation through costly positive incentives.

The player consistently chooses not to use the reward mechanism. Given that they are informed that reward is individually costly and that some peers explicitly argue against using it, the player’s behavior aligns with a calculated choice to avoid efficiency losses from reward transfers. They do not use reward even when many others cooperate strongly, suggesting they are not inclined to pay to reinforce good behavior or to express gratitude through costly signals. Their stance appears instrumental: they rely on direct contribution decisions and group norms rather than on secondary incentive tools. Whether they are philosophically opposed to reward or simply view it as strategically dominated is unknown, but behaviorally they are a “non-rewarder,” even when cooperation is high.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when many others contribute highly and when informational messages highlight that withholding reward is more cost-effective. Their behavior aligns with a purely payoff-maximizing view of the reward option: since rewarding is individually costly and does not change past contributions, they abstain entirely. There is no evidence of using reward as gratitude, norm reinforcement, or strategic signaling. They also appear indifferent to whether they themselves are rewarded; no behavioral change or communication follows the absence of incoming rewards. This positions them as a non-rewarder type: they rely on contribution choices alone to influence outcomes, treat costly positive sanctions as dominated, and do not invest in building or sustaining norms via rewards, even in highly cooperative environments.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when many others behave cooperatively, and even when they themselves are a high contributor who could be a potential target of reward by others. This implies a strongly efficiency-oriented stance toward rewarding: they behave as if the cost of transferring coins is not worth the benefit to recipients, given the transformation rate. They do not appear to use rewards for norm enforcement, gratitude signaling, or building reputation, even when cooperation is high and visible. Their lack of rewarding persists across different group contribution patterns, indicating a stable “no reward” policy rather than conditional rewarding based on others’ behavior. Whether this is driven by explicit cost–benefit calculation, indifference to social approval, or a belief that rewards are unnecessary when cooperation is already profitable is unknown, but their behavior is consistent with a player who focuses their resources exclusively on the public good when it is efficient and ignores costly side-mechanisms.

The player does not use the reward mechanism at all in this setting. Given the available information, the most consistent interpretation is that they judge the reward technology as inefficient relative to simply contributing and keeping their remaining coins, and therefore refrain from allocating any resources to rewarding. Their earlier reasoning about the public good’s multiplier shows that they attend to relative returns, so their lack of rewarding is plausibly a deliberate cost–benefit decision rather than oversight. They do not engage in symbolic or gratitude-based rewarding even when others match their high contributions, implying that they prioritize payoff efficiency over expressive reciprocity. They also do not attempt to use rewards strategically to shape others’ future behavior, either because the game is very short or because they see the reward mechanism as structurally dominated by direct contribution under these parameters. Their propensity to reward in more favorable or cheaper reward environments is unknown.

This player is a systematic non-rewarder. They consistently refrain from using the reward mechanism, even when others contribute fully and even though they express appreciation for receiving rewards in principle. Their stated reasoning frames reward as an inefficient transfer: because it is more costly to give reward than the receiver gains, they perceive it as collectively wasteful once everyone is already cooperating. They therefore separate two dimensions: they endorse gratitude as a sentiment but reject costly signaling of that gratitude when it reduces group and personal payoffs. Their willingness to cooperate without any attached reward shows that they do not need reinforcement to sustain high contributions, and they do not attempt to use rewards strategically to shape others’ behavior. Overall, they treat reward as dominated by direct contribution and private retention of coins in a high-return environment, and they explicitly advocate that others also avoid using the reward option for reasons of cost-effectiveness.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite clear opportunities and visible heterogeneity in others’ contributions. This consistent non-use suggests they regard rewarding as an inefficient allocation of their own coins, even when they benefit from others’ generosity. Their behavior aligns with a strictly instrumental view of incentives: they free-ride on the cooperative efforts of high contributors without bearing extra costs to encourage or thank them. They neither reward to enforce norms nor to signal gratitude, which implies that social approval, reciprocity signaling, or norm-building are secondary to maintaining their own payoff. They thus appear as a “non-rewarding beneficiary”: willing to enjoy public-good benefits and others’ cooperative efforts, but unwilling to incur additional personal costs to reinforce prosocial behavior in the group.

The player never uses the costly reward mechanism, even when others clearly contribute at high levels and some peers explicitly discuss the relation between contribution and reward. This indicates a strong reluctance to pay an efficiency-costly price to transfer coins to others under a reward technology that is individually and collectively inefficient. Their behavior aligns with the publicly discussed norm that rewarding is not cost-effective once players already contribute efficiently: they appear to adopt the “contribute fully, do not reward” rule in practice, though they do not verbally endorse it. They do not use reward either to express gratitude, to reinforce others’ high contributions, or to sanction low contributors positively. This implies that their primary lever for influencing group outcomes is their own contribution level, not targeted incentive adjustments. They treat the reward mechanism as dominated by simply keeping coins once cooperation is already at or near the desired level. Their willingness to reward under different, more efficient reward technologies is unknown, but in this setting they are a strict non-rewarder on cost-effectiveness grounds rather than out of apparent spite or disengagement.

The player systematically avoids using the reward mechanism, even when others contribute generously and even when rewarding would be relatively efficient in improving cooperators’ payoffs. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur any personal cost purely to reinforce others’ behavior, suggesting low propensity for costly positive reciprocity through explicit reward. They do not appear to use rewards as a strategic tool to stabilize cooperation, to signal alliances, or to reciprocate others’ generosity. Their stance is consistent over time and across different cooperation environments, implying a stable preference for keeping the reward channel inactive rather than experimenting with it. Whether this reflects indifference to others’ payoffs, skepticism about the marginal impact of rewards, lack of attention to the mechanism, or a deliberate “no side-payments” principle is unknown; only the revealed choice not to spend on rewards is clear.

They never spend to reward others, despite repeated opportunities and the presence of cooperative peers. This is strong evidence that they are highly reluctant to incur personal costs for social enforcement or encouragement, even when reward is relatively efficient and others’ cooperation is clearly visible. Their non-use of reward persists across both low- and high-cooperation environments, suggesting that their default is to free-ride not only on the public good but also on any sanctioning or encouragement systems. They appear to rely on others (if anyone) to provide rewards while they focus on maximizing their own payoffs. There is no behavioral indication that they use reward strategically to shape future group behavior, build alliances, or reciprocate good treatment. Any concern for gratitude, reputation, or reciprocal reward seems weak or absent, at least under these parameters. Their incentive structure prioritizes direct monetary gains over indirect, socially mediated incentives.

The player uses the reward mechanism sparsely and selectively. They are willing to incur a personal cost to reward others, but only under very favorable cooperative conditions. This implies they view reward as something to deploy when the group is behaving very cooperatively and likely as a way to reinforce or acknowledge strong contributions rather than as a constant tool. Their use of reward aligns with strategic reinforcement: they reward in situations where nearly all others cooperate at a high level, which is precisely when reward can serve as a signal of approval and possibly maintain the cooperative equilibrium. They do not spend on rewards in mixed or low-cooperation states, which suggests they are not purely altruistic rewarders and do not attempt to “buy” cooperation in adverse conditions. Instead, they appear to use reward as a conditional bonus for clear norm compliance. Because they never receive any rewards, there is no evidence that gratitude or reciprocity toward specific rewarders drives their use of the mechanism. Their reward behavior seems driven by group-level patterns rather than interpersonal reciprocity. Overall, they are a cautious, efficiency-minded rewarder: willing to spend but only when cooperation is already high and the targets are clear.

They show no willingness to use costly reward, even when others match their high contribution level. They never spend to positively reinforce cooperative behavior. This indicates a one-sided enforcement strategy that relies exclusively on negative incentives. The absence of reward despite clear opportunities suggests they either do not value positive reciprocity, see reward as unnecessary when others simply “do what they should,” or prioritize conserving resources for punishment rather than for encouragement. Their normative frame appears to be that full cooperation is the baseline expectation, not something to be specially rewarded.

They never use rewards, even toward very high contributors who match their own full-cooperation standard. This indicates a reluctance to spend resources to positively reinforce others’ behavior, despite clear opportunities. Their non-use of rewards suggests either a strict self-sacrificing focus on contribution itself (preferring to allocate all resources directly to the public good when possible) or a belief that rewarding is unnecessary, ineffective, or too costly. They do not attempt to build alliances or guide norms through positive incentives in the observed setting. Whether they would reward in longer or more repeated interactions is unknown.

They never use the reward mechanism, even when others contribute at equal or higher levels. This abstention suggests they are not inclined toward costly positive reinforcement of cooperative behavior in this environment. Possible underlying reasons include viewing high contributions as expected rather than reward-worthy, perceiving rewards as strategically unnecessary, or wishing to conserve resources. Their profile is not that of a “rewarding altruist”; instead, they rely on their own contribution decisions rather than shaping others via positive incentives.

They never use reward, even though there are clearly very high contributors they could reinforce at low marginal cost. This indicates a reluctance to spend private resources to positively sanction others, even when their contributions are aligned with their own cooperative behavior. Their stance looks individually payoff-protective on the sanctioning dimension: they are willing to cooperate via contributions but not willing to incur extra costs to shape others’ behavior with rewards. This could reflect a belief that high contributors will continue without explicit reinforcement, a principled stance against intervention in others’ payoffs, or simple preference to keep sanctioning tools unused. In functional terms, they act as a “pure contributor” rather than a “norm-enforcer” or “reciprocal rewarder.”

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when others contribute substantially and repeatedly. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal costs for prosocial reinforcement. They do not engage in positive reciprocity through rewards, even in situations where several peers act cooperatively. From their behavior, it appears that they either (a) judge the reward technology as too inefficient to justify its use, (b) do not prioritize norm-supporting behavior, or (c) pay little attention to reward as a strategic tool altogether. Which of these is primary is unknown. What is clear is that gratitude signaling through costly reward is effectively absent.
