Given the reward technology is weak (cost exceeds benefit), their inaction can be read as payoff-conscious: they are willing to sacrifice coins heavily for punishment (which has strong impact) but not for reward (which is inefficient). Behaviorally, they appear to believe that positive reinforcement is not worth its cost in this environment, or at least not as effective as sanctioning. There is no evidence of attempts to build alliances or signal gratitude via rewards; their cooperative and normative intentions are expressed through contributions and punishment only. Their type is therefore “pure punisher” rather than “carrot-and-stick”; they prefer the stick.

They are very sparing with rewards. For most of the game they do not reward anyone, even though others start using rewards more frequently. This again suggests a cost–benefit orientation and reluctance to spend on side mechanisms unless they see clear social or strategic value. On one occasion, they reward a highly active, enforcement-minded cooperator (the “police” player who spends heavily on punishing the bird). This can be interpreted as gratitude signaling and reinforcement of costly norm enforcement. They do not state this explicitly, but it is plausible low-confidence inference given timing and target. They do not systematically reward high contributors or those who share information. Their limited use of reward seems more relational and symbolic than strategic: a one-off recognition of a fellow participant’s role, rather than an attempt to construct a generalized reward norm. They never reward the disruptive “bird,” regardless of that player’s contributions or reward behavior toward others, suggesting they are unwilling to use reward in ways that might be perceived as endorsing deviance or trolling. Overall, they view reward as nice but non-essential, and they are willing to spend on it only in small, targeted bursts.

They never reward anyone, despite: - Repeated instances of others rewarding cooperators or “topping up” punishers. - Their own cooperative behavior being rewarded at least once. - Active group discussion about using rewards to support “justice” or compensate victims. This indicates that they are not a “rewarding cooperator” either. Instead, their prosociality is expressed exclusively through their own contributions, not through second-order prosociality. Possible motives, inferred from behavior: - **Efficiency / frugality with side-mechanisms**: Reward technology is weak (more costly to giver than beneficial to receiver). They may implicitly view reward as an inefficient way to promote cooperation, and therefore avoid it entirely. - **Norm focus on first-order contribution**: They seem to believe that the primary way to do good in this environment is to contribute fully, not to manage others’ payoffs afterward. - **Non-participation in coalition-building**: By not rewarding allied punishers or high contributors, they avoid participating in political dynamics (e.g., “topping up” the self-appointed police, or signaling alliances). They remain individually cooperative but neutral in group factionalism. Taken together, they act as a pure contributor: they do not use reward as a strategic tool or as an expression of gratitude, even when it is socially salient to do so.

For most of the game, they do not use the reward mechanism at all, even when others are actively rewarded for high contributions or for punishing perceived deviants. This again points to a conservative approach to spending on social tools: they prioritize keeping their own earnings rather than using them to shape others’ incentives. However, there is one clear deviation: in a later round they do reward at least one other player (evident from others noting “bunny and cow rewarded”). The context is one in which some players have been randomly targeted or “wronged” by the suspected bot‐like player, and others discuss boosting them. Their lone use of rewards coincides with that narrative, suggesting that when they do reward, it is not for strict norm enforcement but for compensating perceived unfair harm or for solidarity with victims. Even in that case, they use reward very sparingly; they do not begin a pattern of regular rewarding afterward. This makes them a “reluctant rewarder”: default is to keep coins, with rare, low‑intensity deviations when motivated by fairness or empathy. They do not appear to use reward instrumentally to sustain high contributions (they never systematically reward top contributors), nor to curry favor; the isolated reward use is more consistent with moral expression than with strategic incentive design.

They never use rewards either. They do not positively reinforce high contributors, nor do they compensate those spending heavily on enforcement, despite clearly benefiting from the latter. They also do not reward those who defend them or ignore their low contributions. This pattern fits a consistently payoff-focused stance: they avoid both positive and negative social spending and do not appear motivated by gratitude, solidarity with punishers, or attempts to shape others’ strategies via incentives. They appear to trust that others will continue to cooperate and sanction with or without their contributions to the incentive system, so they keep all such budget for themselves. In terms of “type,” this is a pure non-enforcer: they neither invest in punishment nor in reward, even when strategic use could increase long-run cooperation or reduce the likelihood of being targeted.

They use rewards rarely and in a targeted, expressive way. They reward a highly active “policing” player, explicitly “topping up” someone who spends heavily on enforcing norms, and later reward that same figure again, which looks like gratitude and moral support more than strategic incentive. They do not spray rewards across all high contributors, even though almost everyone contributes maximally; instead, they use rewards as social signals toward specific individuals whose behavior they admire. They never attempt to use rewards instrumentally to manipulate the “angry bird,” nor to buy cooperation from under-contributors. Their reward pattern suggests they view it as a way to recognize costly norm enforcement and maintain morale in a group enforcer, not as a primary mechanism for managing public-good behavior. Overall, they are more comfortable with normative appeals in chat than with systematic reward schemes.

They never reward anyone, despite: - Repeated chat appeals to “top up” enforcers or support justice-doers. - Multiple visible instances of others using rewards to compensate high contributors or replenish “police” players. - Receiving rewards themselves from the bird. This makes them a pure non-rewarder: they neither reciprocate kindness nor strategically reinforce good behavior. Even when someone publicly reveals having low coin balances due to heavy punishment spending, this player does not step in with a reward, suggesting that compassionate or reciprocal motives are weak relative to conserving their own payoff. The fact that they do accept rewards (they receive several bonus coins over the game) without ever returning any creates a clear asymmetric pattern: they absorb positive externalities but do not generate them through the reward mechanism. They also do not use rewards tactically to build alliances or signal trustworthiness, even when suspected of being in cahoots with the bird. In new environments, this implies a type who cooperates via contributions but not via side-payments or relational gestures.

They also never use the reward mechanism. Even though they repeatedly observe others rewarding and being rewarded, and themselves receive occasional rewards from the parrot, they do not reciprocate or strategically top up any allies. This is not because the reward interface is unknown—they comment on outcomes that require understanding who has been rewarded. Instead, it appears they simply decide that spending coins to reward is not worthwhile under this technology, or they are keeping a strict “don’t spend on meta-actions” stance. The player never uses the reward option, even when there are consistent high contributors and even when they themselves benefit from others’ cooperation. This suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on positive reinforcement. They do not appear to seek to build alliances or signal gratitude via costly rewards. Their cooperative moves are not accompanied by attempts to shape others’ behavior through incentives; instead, they treat contribution and redistribution as the main levers of interaction. This may reflect a cost-minimizing mindset, indifference to direct reciprocity tools, or uncertainty about the strategic value of rewarding; which of these is primary is unknown.

This player never spends to reward others despite repeatedly observing high contributions by several peers and personally receiving rewards themselves. Their pattern is thus “cooperate but do not positively sanction,” distinguishing their prosocial contributions from prosocial incentives. They appear unwilling to incur additional personal costs beyond contribution, even to support or reinforce cooperative partners. This could reflect a belief that contribution is already sufficient as their cooperative duty, a preference to conserve resources for themselves once the public fund is determined, or skepticism about the efficacy or necessity of rewards. Their sole use of the sanctioning system is punitive, and even that is minimal, indicating they treat the reward/punish mechanism cautiously and only employ it when they feel a strong reason; that reason is never positive reinforcement in this game.

The player is willing to spend on rewards and uses them selectively to reinforce cooperative behavior. They reward high contributors while still free‑riding themselves, which indicates a strategic or norm‑signaling use of rewards rather than purely reciprocal gratitude. At times they reward multiple cooperative peers simultaneously, suggesting they care about broadly supporting the cooperative cluster rather than forming a narrow dyadic alliance. The magnitude of rewards remains small relative to potential payoff, consistent with a cautious or budget‑conscious approach: they acknowledge and support cooperation but do not invest heavily in it. They never combine reward with simultaneous punishment, preferring exclusively positive sanctioning. Overall, they act as a low‑intensity positive reciprocator and norm supporter: using reward to encourage cooperation, including cooperation they are benefitting from without yet fully matching.

They never use the reward mechanism, despite the presence of other clear high contributors and despite receiving rewards themselves. This indicates that they separate their own contribution norm from active pro‑social reinforcement. They appear unwilling to incur extra cost even to benefit cooperators, suggesting that while they internalize a norm of contributing, they do not extend that norm to second‑order cooperation (rewarding others). It is unknown whether this reflects a belief that contributions are their own responsibility, a desire to conserve resources, or low attention to the incentive mechanism. What is clear is that they do not use rewards strategically to build alliances or condition future group behavior.

They are selectively but genuinely willing to reward, and they understand the positive efficiency of mutual rewarding. Early on, they experiment with broad, near-uniform rewards across the cooperative group, accepting a short-term personal cost to reinforce a fully cooperative environment. Over time, they reduce reward usage, possibly because others do not consistently reciprocate or because the group already maintains full contributions without it. When someone expresses feeling disadvantaged by others’ lack of rewarding, they respond both verbally and behaviorally by directing additional rewards to that person, which functions as targeted support and acknowledgment. Near the end, they explicitly advocate for a coordinated, symmetric reward scheme that would increase everyone’s payoff if universally adopted, showing that they view rewards as a tool for mutual gain rather than as gifts or favoritism. They do not use rewards strategically to manipulate defectors; instead, they reinforce cooperators and try to organize group-level reward norms.

They are a high, sometimes extreme, rewarder with a strong inclination toward inclusive generosity. They frequently spend significant resources to give positive incentives to others, often distributing rewards broadly and symmetrically across all cooperating peers. This behavior is consistent with a strategy of reinforcing the fully cooperative equilibrium and signaling goodwill rather than maximizing immediate personal payoff. At several points they experiment with giving no rewards or selectively rewarding just one or a few partners, suggesting some sensitivity to their own net earnings and to whether others are also rewarding. However, they repeatedly return to wide, almost egalitarian reward distributions, and explicitly describe themselves as giving to everyone. They do not appear to condition rewards on fine-grained differences in others’ behavior; as long as others are contributing fully and broadly aligned with the group, they tend to treat them similarly. The single defector receives no reward and instead is punished, showing that rewards are reserved for cooperators. Overall, they use reward as a primary tool to sustain cooperation and express solidarity, accepting short-run personal costs in exchange for maintaining a generous, high-trust environment.

They are a willing but selective rewarder. They frequently spend on reward, sometimes at substantial personal cost, and sometimes not at all. This pattern suggests that they use reward as a tool rather than a fixed habit. They appear more likely to reward when their own contribution is high; they rarely combine free-riding with heavy reward spending. They focus their reward spending in rounds where cooperative norms are relatively strong, as if reinforcing an already-cooperative cluster rather than trying to “buy” cooperation in a very noisy or low-contribution environment. They tolerate significant personal payoff reduction from rewarding in some rounds, which implies that they value norm reinforcement and/or relationship signaling over immediate earnings. However, there are also many rounds where they reward minimally or not at all despite others contributing; they are not mechanically pro-social. Their reward use looks contingent on their current emotional or evaluative state about the group (e.g., satisfaction with recent interaction patterns) rather than on a strict tit-for-tat rule.

The player is a frequent and substantial user of positive reward. They routinely spend a nontrivial portion of their available payoff to reward others, often in multiple units. This occurs both when reward is commonly used by others and when some players reduce or stop rewarding, implying that they have a strong internal norm for active reinforcement rather than merely following the crowd. Their trigger for rewarding appears primarily contribution-based: they reward those who contribute high amounts to the public good and withhold (or sharply limit) reward toward chronic low contributors. They sometimes maintain reward spending even when it does not immediately pay off in personal terms, indicating that they value the act of reinforcing cooperation at the group level or maintaining a pro-social identity. They also display some responsiveness to overall reward ecology: when others around them are very erratic or use reward in ways that look wasteful or self-harming, they reduce their own spending in certain stretches, suggesting a partial strategic check on costs. Nonetheless, they never fully abandon reward; they oscillate between “heavy rewarder” and “moderate rewarder” modes rather than turning reward off. This blend points to a type who genuinely endorses positive reciprocity but is not totally blind to cost.

The player is generally reluctant to spend on reward, and when they do, they use it sparingly and inconsistently. Many rounds pass with no reward from them, even when others are contributing a lot and engaging heavily in mutual rewarding. Occasional reward expenditures appear, but not at large or systematic levels, and they do not show a stable pattern of reinforcing particular partners or consistently rewarding top contributors. This indicates that they value keeping their own payoff more than paying a personal cost to shape others’ behavior, even though the reward technology is socially efficient. Their rare reward purchases may reflect either momentary gratitude or experimentation with the reward mechanism rather than a settled strategy of incentivizing cooperation. There is no clear evidence that they use reward strategically to build coalitions or to signal norms; their main stance is to free-ride on others’ rewarding activity.

The player is a persistent, fairly generous reward user, willing to spend substantial amounts across most of the game. They regularly incur nontrivial costs to give positive payoffs to others, including in periods where they themself are not being especially favored with reward. They do not appear to condition reward tightly on marginal changes in others’ contributions; instead, their reward behavior tracks a broad pattern of supporting generally high-contributing participants. They do not use reward in a sharply strategic, targeted way (e.g., systematically rewarding new or wavering cooperators, or pivoting reward away from those who reduce contributions), and there are stretches where they either reward very little or not at all without a clear structural reason. This points to a prosocial but somewhat noisy rule: they are willing to “chip in” via reward to maintain a cooperative climate, but their targeting and timing are not finely optimized. They do not appear to use reward as a precise tool to equalize payoffs or punish quasi-defectors; instead, it functions as broad encouragement rather than calibrated control.

They never use the reward mechanism at any point, despite frequent opportunities and despite others consistently contributing at the same high level as they do. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend own resources to provide direct incentives or expressions of gratitude, at least under a one-to-one cost-benefit structure where rewarding does not increase social surplus. Their non-use of rewards does not appear retaliatory or selective; it is a blanket strategy. This is consistent with a payoff-conscious cooperative type who sees reward as individually costly and socially neutral in this implementation, and therefore unnecessary. It may also reflect a belief that stable high cooperation among peers does not require additional reinforcement, or a preference for “silent” cooperation without extra signals. Their behavior provides no evidence of using reward as a tool for alliance-building, norm enforcement, or selective encouragement.

The player never uses the available reward mechanism despite consistently high contributions from others and a highly cooperative environment. This suggests a strong preference for conserving personal resources over engaging in costly positive reciprocity; they appear to believe that explicit rewards are unnecessary when cooperative norms are already in place. They do not use rewards strategically to stabilize cooperation or to encourage specific deviators back to the norm, even when minor deviations occur. This may indicate either that they see contribution behavior itself as sufficient mutual support, or that they do not integrate the reward tool into their strategy at all. Their non-use of rewards implies low propensity to engage in second-order cooperation (paying a cost to reinforce cooperative behavior) and a tendency to rely on direct contribution choices rather than meta-level incentives. Their behavior gives no evidence of gratitude signaling through rewards, nor of targeted reinforcement, so their reward strategy is best characterized as consistently inactive or unused.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even when others are clearly cooperating at high and stable levels and when rewarders are identifiable to recipients. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal resources on direct positive incentives or gratitude payments, even in a fully cooperative environment. Their strategy appears to be: contribute maximally, but do not pay extra to influence or thank others. This could reflect several possibilities (all with low confidence): they may view contribution itself as sufficient cooperation; they may not see additional benefit from rewarding in an already cooperative group; they may be conserving payoff; or they may be indifferent or uncomfortable with active, targeted social interventions. There is no evidence that their decision to withhold reward is punitive; it is uniformly applied and not contingent on others’ actions. They are thus best described as a “non-rewarding cooperator”: they help via public good contributions but do not invest in second-order cooperation through reward.

This player almost never uses the reward mechanism and appears highly reluctant to spend on rewarding. Across repeated opportunities, they generally choose not to transfer any of their payoff to others, despite facing multiple peers who contribute at the same maximal level as they do. They do not systematically reward high cooperators, even when those cooperators are clearly identifiable and stable. On a single occasion, they engage with the reward system in a trivial, payoff-neutral way that results in them both spending and receiving the same small amount. Given the structure of the game and the data available, the direction of that transfer is ambiguous: it may be self-rewarding if that is allowed, or a tiny reward to someone else who simultaneously rewards them. There is no clear pattern of them using reward to reinforce others’ cooperation, nor to build alliances or express gratitude. If they do reward someone else in that instance, it is minimal and does not reflect an ongoing strategy. Overall, they behave as if reward is either strategically unimportant, too costly relative to its benefit, or simply not salient. They do not appear to use it as a tool to shape behavior, to reciprocate, or to signal approval in any systematic way. Their default is effectively to ignore the reward option.

They never use rewards, despite persistent and often very high contributions by others. They ignore opportunities to positively reinforce cooperative partners or to signal gratitude or alliance. Given that rewards are relatively inefficient, this could reflect a payoff-focused calculation, but the absence of any token or symbolic reward also points to very low concern for prosocial signaling or relational strategies. They neither strategically reward to shape future behavior nor altruistically reward to acknowledge others’ generosity.

The player never uses the reward option despite repeatedly observing high contributions by most group members. They decline to reward those who consistently match their full contribution, and they do not reciprocate when others reward them on rare occasions. Given that they do use punishment extensively, this asymmetry suggests a strong preference for negative over positive enforcement. It may reflect a view that high contributions are expected baseline behavior and do not merit costly reinforcement, whereas deviation below that norm merits active sanction. It may also indicate sensitivity to the relatively poor cost-effectiveness of the reward technology compared to punishment, though this is inferred with low confidence. Overall, they are a zero-reward, punishment-focused enforcer.

They never reward anyone, despite the presence of consistently high contributors who match their cooperative effort. This is not because they lack opportunities or candidates; instead, the pattern indicates a strong asymmetry between punishment and reward. They appear to assign primary responsibility for maintaining cooperation to negative sanctions rather than positive reinforcement. The relatively low “leverage” of rewards compared to punishment may contribute to this choice, but they also ignore opportunities to provide even symbolic or low‑cost rewards. This suggests that they are a “pure punishing cooperator”: they cooperate fully and enforce cooperation through costly punishment, but do not use or apparently value material gratitude or positive reciprocity tools within the game.

The player never uses the reward option, even toward strong, consistent cooperators who might be natural reward targets. Given that reward is relatively inefficient mechanically, this may indicate that they pay attention to institutional efficiency and see reward as a poor use of resources compared with simply contributing. It may also reflect a general reluctance to intervene in others’ payoffs at all, whether positively or negatively, preferring to express prosociality solely through their own contribution level. Their absence from both punishment and reward channels suggests a strategy of “quiet cooperation” rather than active norm management. Whether they would reward in an environment where reward is more efficient than contributions or punishment is unknown.

The player never uses the reward option at any point, despite having multiple opportunities and being surrounded by several consistently high contributors. This consistent non-use can reflect several possible motives: they may view the reward technology as inefficient (costly relative to its impact), unnecessary given already high cooperation, or strategically unimportant compared to maintaining their own contributions. It could also signal a preference to avoid positive discrimination among cooperators, treating high contributions as a baseline expectation rather than something that merits extra reinforcement. Since they also refrain from punishing most of the time, their overall pattern suggests a “low-intervention” approach to social control mechanisms: they are willing to set an example via their own contributions but do not invest extra resources to shape others’ behavior through formal incentives.

They never reward any other player, even when others contribute fully and even when some players spend substantial amounts punishing defectors in a way that aligns with their own norm. This absence persists throughout, despite many opportunities to provide positive reinforcement. Given this, their cooperative enforcement style is one-sidedly punitive rather than balanced between reward and punishment. Their unwillingness to reward may partly reflect the low efficiency of the reward mechanism relative to punishment, but they do not appear to use reward even strategically to build alliances or encourage already-cooperative behavior. For this player, good behavior seems expected and not something that merits extra payoff; only deviations provoke costly action.

They never spend anything on rewards, despite having multiple opportunities and being rewarded once themselves. Their only explicit comment about reward questions its benefit, suggesting that they see no clear strategic or payoff advantage in using it. They do not experiment with small amounts, which indicates aversion to using mechanisms they do not see as clearly useful or payoff-improving. Their lack of rewarding is not clearly hostile; it looks more like a rational abstention driven by perceived ineffectiveness of the tool and the observation that early heavy rewarding by another player is not reciprocated or copied by the group. They also do not use reward relationally (for alliance-building or reciprocity) after being rewarded, further underscoring that they treat it as practically irrelevant to their strategy.

They never use the reward mechanism at all, despite multiple opportunities and clear observability of others’ contributions and rewards. There is no evidence of them experimenting with rewarding either cooperators or defectors. The absence of any reward spending, even when others discuss and use rewards, suggests they see little personal value in paying to influence others’ behavior in this environment, or they may not find the mechanism salient or worthwhile. They neither employ reward for reciprocal gratitude, for strategic reinforcement of cooperators, nor as a signaling device. Their behavioral pattern is consistent with treating reward as dominated or irrelevant rather than as a tool for shaping the game.

They never spend any coins on reward, despite reward being available throughout. They also do not encourage or endorse rewarding in chat. Their behavior is consistent with treating the reward mechanism as individually useless or at best neutral: they do not use it to build alliances, to reciprocate, or to shape others’ behavior. Even when reward is actively used by a few others, they remain non-participatory. This suggests a strongly self-regarding stance toward costly prosocial adjustments beyond the main contribution decision. They may implicitly view reward as an unnecessary expenditure that does not change others’ behavior enough to justify the cost, or simply as an extra layer they choose to ignore. In any case, they are not a rewarder type.

They almost never use the reward option and appear highly reluctant to spend on rewarding, despite at least one instance of someone rewarding them. The one time they do reward, they spend a small positive amount, which indicates they understand the mechanism and are not dogmatically opposed to using it. However, they do not engage in broad, systematic reward of cooperators or in reciprocal reward chains; rewarding is rare and low-intensity. Their choices suggest that they view the reward mechanism as having low instrumental value under this cost/benefit structure, and they are not strongly motivated by expressive gratitude via costly reward. They do not use reward strategically to build alliances or enforce cooperation in any persistent way.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite multiple opportunities and observable reward activity by other players. Their messages suggest skepticism about others’ rationality in contribution decisions, but they do not comment directly on the reward mechanism’s usefulness beyond acknowledging that the multiplier on contributions is “high.” Their consistent non-use of reward, even when cooperative partners exist and when rewarders and rewards are visible, indicates either that they find the reward institution strategically or economically unattractive, or that they prioritize their own payoff once the contribution choice is made. They do not use reward for alliance building, gratitude, or norm reinforcement. Their strategy is “talk and contribute,” not “reward to shape others’ behavior.”

They never spend any coins on rewards in any observed round, despite having multiple opportunities and seeing others experiment with rewarding. This strongly indicates that they either see no instrumental value in rewarding (given the technology and group behavior) or that they are unwilling to sacrifice private payoff for normative or expressive reasons. Their behavior is consistent with a simple rule: keep all reward resources for themselves, regardless of whether others contribute or reward. There is no sign that they use rewards as strategic reinforcement, as a signal of approval, or as a way to build alliances. They also do not even use small, low-cost “tokens” of reward, which suggests a robust reluctance to incur any cost for others’ benefit, at least within this incentive structure. Whether they conceptually understand reward dynamics is unknown; what is clear is that, behaviorally, they act as a strict non-rewarder.

Their default stance is to avoid spending on rewards; across most of the game they refrain from rewarding, even when many others cooperate. On one occasion, they do choose to reward, paying a noticeable cost without any direct evidence that this will be reciprocated. These rewards are directed toward specific others (identity and selection criteria are unknown), not spread evenly, implying some selective reinforcement of particular behavior or individuals rather than indiscriminate generosity. Because the reward technology is neutral (cost equals benefit), and they use it only once, they appear to treat rewarding as a special signal or one-off encouragement rather than a standing strategy. They do not adopt a “reward-for-reward” norm: they continue not to reward even after seeing another player incur large reward costs earlier. This pattern fits “reluctant, targeted rewarder”: generally conservative with costly positive sanctions, willing to use them sparingly when cooperation is strong and perhaps when they wish to signal approval or encourage certain partners, but not building an ongoing reward-based coalition.

They never spend on rewards in any round, despite having opportunities and despite receiving rewards themselves. They also explicitly express skepticism about the usefulness of the reward mechanism, stating that they see little or no point in rewarding. This indicates that their decision not to reward is deliberate rather than inattentive. Their non-use of rewards persists even when cooperation is fragile and when reward could, in principle, be used to encourage others. They appear to view contribution itself—and verbal encouragement—as their main cooperative action, rather than using costly reward as a strategic tool. Their stance suggests they are cost-conscious about side mechanisms that do not clearly improve their own or the group’s outcomes, or they simply consider reward redundant or ineffective in this group context.

They exhibit a strong initial willingness to reward, to the point of making a very large and personally costly reward investment early on. This is done before having any evidence of reciprocal rewarding from others, suggesting a hopeful attempt to create or signal a cooperative reward norm rather than a purely reactive strategy. When no one rewards them back, they immediately conclude that rewarding is “pointless” and stop entirely for the rest of the game. From that point on, they never spend on reward again, even when later rewarded by others. This pattern indicates that their reward behavior is driven by expectations of mutual reciprocity and social recognition: once those expectations are violated, they withdraw from the reward system completely. They also later try to use reward strategically as a bargaining chip (“you reward me and I will…”), showing that they treat rewards as a possible tool for contingent cooperation, not just as moral praise.

They are willing to spend substantially on rewarding others, despite the fact that the reward technology is personally costly and inefficient. They do not use rewards early, even when cooperation is high, suggesting that they do not see small, ongoing rewards as necessary to sustain already-strong cooperation. Instead, their major reward outlays appear concentrated in situations where cooperative behavior becomes more fragile or where a subset of players continues to contribute highly while others shirk. This pattern suggests they use rewards as a way to recognize and support loyal cooperators when they are under strain, rather than as a continuous incentive scheme. They show no sign of using rewards tactically to curry favor for future material gain; they continue to fully cooperate regardless of being rewarded or not. Their reward behavior reflects expressive gratitude and norm support rather than payoff-maximizing strategy.

This player never spends anything on rewards, despite having repeated opportunities and clear examples of high cooperators they could reward. They do, however, receive rewards multiple times. The absence of outgoing rewards, even toward consistently generous others, suggests they do not use the reward mechanism as a tool for norm reinforcement or reciprocity. Several interpretations are consistent with the data and cannot be fully disentangled: they may view reward as too costly given its inefficiency; they may not see reward as necessary when others are already cooperating; or they may be generally uninterested in active sanctioning systems. What is clear is that their cooperative stance in contributions does not extend into costly positive sanctioning. Their behavior therefore fits a “pure cooperator, non‑rewarder” pattern: they contribute highly but do not invest in incentivizing others. They rely on direct contribution choices, not metastrategic tools like rewards, to shape group outcomes.

The player never pays to reward anyone, despite being repeatedly exposed to a group where others are contributing generously and where the reward technology is clearly explained. They forgo rewarding even when they personally benefit a great deal from others’ high contributions and when reward could theoretically help sustain the cooperative norm that advantages them. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for prosocial signaling or norm support. They do not use rewards strategically to encourage others, to build alliances, or to express gratitude. Whether this is due to not valuing social reciprocity, not seeing reward as effective, or simply maximizing short‑term payoff is unknown; the only clear pattern is a consistent refusal to sacrifice coins for others’ benefit via reward.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, even though it is frequently used by others and even though some players repeatedly provide high contributions that could, in principle, be rewarded. They do not spend anything to build alliances, encourage cooperators, or reciprocate kindness. Since the reward technology is payoff-neutral at the dyadic level, their refusal to use it suggests an aversion to spending effort or coins on social signaling, not simply concern about losing net coins to help others. Their behavior is consistent with a purely self-focused payoff strategy that does not invest in others’ welfare or in longer-term relational incentives. They are a “non-rewarder”: they do not engage in positive incentives for cooperation.

They practically never use the reward option, despite frequent opportunities and the visibility of others using rewards. This is not simply cost sensitivity, because they repeatedly spend substantial amounts on punishment, which is much more costly to the target and still expensive to them. Their near-zero rewarding indicates that they do not rely on positive reinforcement to sustain cooperation, even when others contribute very generously. They appear to see negative sanctions as the primary instrument for norm enforcement, while viewing rewards as either unnecessary, strategically weak, or not worth the cost. There is no evidence that they strategically reward allies or high contributors to build coalitions or signal gratitude. Their social preference is thus asymmetrically focused on punishment rather than on combined carrot-and-stick strategies.

The player uses reward sparingly but deliberately. They reward only a few times across the game and always choose clear high contributors or especially cooperative peers. They never reward low or erratic contributors, even when those players are heavily targeted by others. Reward choices suggest an attempt to positively reinforce strong cooperation, possibly to acknowledge consistent contributors or to encourage an emerging cooperative cluster. However, they do not escalate into systematic “bonus” payments or broad mutual-reward cycles; they remain highly selective and low-intensity in reward use. This pattern is consistent with a frugal prosocial type: willing to spend a little to reinforce good behavior but not to run an expensive reward regime. There is no sign they use reward tactically to buy allies or to offset others’ punishment; the targets are already prosocial by contribution behavior.

They never use the reward option either, even though rewarding is frequently exercised by others in the group and they observe numerous instances of positive transfers. They do not pay to acknowledge high cooperators, nor do they use rewards to build alliances or reciprocate favor. Given that rewarding is payoff‑neutral at the margin and can be used strategically to cultivate cooperation, their total non‑use of this tool points to one or more of: (a) a general reluctance to engage in any costly social moves (even when the direct cost/benefit is balanced), (b) low interest in influencing others’ behavior via explicit signals, or (c) limited belief in the effectiveness of rewards in this environment. They are behaviorally detached from the group’s rich reward network: others frequently send rewards among themselves, yet this player neither initiates nor reinforces such ties. This paints them as socially non‑interventionist: they do not step into the roles of patron, ally, or cheerleader through the reward mechanism.

They use rewards fairly often and in a pattern that is clearly contingent on others’ cooperation levels. They reserve rewards for consistently high contributors or those who match or exceed the group norm, and they never reward clear under‑contributors. They frequently direct rewards to the same cooperative individuals across multiple opportunities, indicating an attempt to reinforce and encourage a stable coalition of high contributors. They sometimes use reward alone, and sometimes mix it with punishment in the same decision, suggesting they view reward and punishment as complementary tools: reward to support cooperators, punishment to correct defectors. They are willing to sacrifice substantial own earnings to provide these rewards, even when the reward technology is merely break‑even (no social multiplier). This implies they are not using reward purely as an instrumental payoff‑maximizing tool but as a normative or relational signal—supporting “good citizens” of the group. Their reward allocation seems strategic in a social sense (reinforcing desirable behavior and perhaps fostering allegiance), but not narrowly self‑interested in terms of immediate income.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, even though several others in the group repeatedly spend on both rewards and punishments. They choose not to reward high contributors or apparent cooperators, nor do they use rewards to build alliances or to signal approval. Given the reward technology here is payoff-neutral (cost equals benefit), their non-use might reflect: - A simple preference to keep decisions minimal and not engage with the secondary mechanism. - Low interest in explicit social signaling via monetary gifts, even toward cooperators. - A belief that contributions themselves are sufficient signals of prosociality, making extra rewarding unnecessary. - A weak strategic interest in shaping others’ behavior via positive reinforcement. Because they nonetheless contribute reliably and sometimes generously, their lack of rewarding does not indicate selfishness, but rather a non-interventionist style: they cooperate quietly on the primary task and refrain from “meta” interventions in others’ payoffs.

The player shows a clear willingness to spend on rewards, including in situations where reward is structurally weak (one-for-one transfers) and especially near the end. They allocate rewards selectively, often toward players who either contribute less than average or suffer heavy punishment, not just to the highest contributors. This reveals a nuanced, norm-expressive motive: they appear to use reward both as encouragement for lower contributors and as support or sympathy for those being punished severely. The heavy, costly reward expenditure in the final round—despite no future to shape—strongly suggests intrinsic satisfaction from signaling approval or kindness, or a desire to “correct” what they see as unfair outcomes, rather than a purely strategic attempt to change future behavior. They do not mechanically reward all cooperators; their pattern is attentional and targeted, consistent with a socially sensitive, context-responsive rewarder.

The player never spends any coins on rewarding others, despite having many clear opportunities where others fully cooperate or even continue to cooperate after having already rewarded them. Given that reward is exactly cost-neutral in this design, consistently refraining from rewarding strongly suggests that they prioritize keeping their own coins over engaging in reciprocal or norm-enforcing reward, even when it would not change net payoff. This points to either inattentiveness to the reward mechanism, a preference for simplicity (avoiding extra decisions), or a disinterest in social exchange via incentives. They do not use reward instrumentally to shape others’ future behavior: they never reward high contributors, never reward frequent rewarders, and never reward targeted individuals after being called out. They also do not use reward symbolically to express approval or gratitude. Their strategy around reward is effectively “never use it,” treating the game as if only contributions matter. This aligns with a narrow payoff/effort calculus and an apparent lack of interest in the expressive or relationship-building role of positive incentives.

They do reward, but sparingly and in a somewhat irregular pattern. They are willing to incur personal cost to give rewards, indicating that they value reinforcing good behavior or reciprocating kindness at least occasionally. However, most rounds they choose not to spend on rewards, even when others fully cooperate. Their reward behavior does not consistently track any single simple rule (such as “reward all cooperators” or “reward everyone when all contribute”). Instead, they sometimes reward in cooperative environments and sometimes refrain under very similar conditions. This suggests that while they understand and accept the mechanism, they are either conserving resources, experimenting, or are not strongly motivated to use rewards as a systematic enforcement tool. There is also no clear evidence that they use rewards to punish defectors indirectly (e.g., rewarding everyone except defectors); often they simply do nothing, even alongside clear free riding.

The player also never spends on rewarding others. They refrain from rewarding even when other players contribute very generously or when some players clearly bear disproportionate cooperative costs relative to others. This consistent absence of reward behavior suggests that they either (a) place very low value on expressing gratitude or reinforcing cooperation at a personal cost, or (b) view the reward mechanism as too inefficient to justify use, regardless of how cooperative others are. They behave as though any positive transfer that reduces their own payoff is unacceptable, even if it might encourage beneficial group behavior. They likewise do not seem to use reward instrumentally at the end of the game (where there is no future cooperation to affect anyway), which is consistent with them not valuing reputation‑like or expressive aspects of rewarding in this anonymous, one‑shot‑per‑partner context. Overall, they are “non‑rewarding” in both normative and strategic senses: they neither repay kindness at a cost to themselves nor attempt to buy higher future contributions. Any warm‑glow or reciprocity motives are either absent or too weak to overcome their focus on retaining coins.

The player never uses the costly reward option, even when others contribute very generously or when some peers appear to reward them. This suggests that they perceive the reward technology as inefficient (the cost per unit of benefit is worse than for punishment) or not worth the sacrifice of private payoff. They may also believe that high contributions are simply what is expected under the norm, and thus do not merit additional positive reinforcement. Their complete avoidance of reward while being willing to punish indicates an asymmetry: they rely on negative sanctions to discourage free riding but do not invest in positive incentives to encourage high cooperation. This points to a “punish-but-don’t-reward” enforcement style, focused more on deterring norm violations than on expressing gratitude or strategically strengthening alliances. Because they never reward, they do not visibly reciprocate rewards given to them; if others grant them bonus coins, it does not lead to a change in their use of the reward tool. Any internal sense of gratitude, if present, does not translate into costly positive sanctioning behavior in this environment.

The player is typically frugal with rewards and does not habitually use positive incentives. For most of the game, they refrain from rewarding even when others contribute very generously. This suggests that they prioritize conserving their own payoff over maintaining cooperation via costly rewards, especially given that the reward technology is efficiency-reducing. When they do reward, it is targeted and occurs only once, directed at peers who simultaneously provide very high contributions. This indicates that the trigger for rewarding is clear, above-average generosity relative to the rest of the group, not mere conformity to a moderate norm. Their reward use appears intentional rather than random: they choose exactly the players whose contributions stand out as most cooperative at that moment. Given that rewards are more expensive than the benefit they bestow, their willingness to use them at all suggests some value placed on reciprocating or reinforcing high cooperation, even when it is not individually optimal in narrow monetary terms. However, they do not continue this pattern in later opportunities, implying that they either do not see reward as an effective long-term tool or that they view that one rewarding episode as sufficient acknowledgment. Their reward behavior appears more like a one-time expression of appreciation or a brief attempt at strategic reinforcement of high-contributing peers than a systematic, ongoing policy. They do not reward consistent medium contributors, nor do they balance reward with punishment in a clear “carrot-and-stick” strategy. Overall, they are a mostly non-rewarding player with a capacity for selective positive reciprocity toward conspicuous cooperators.

This player almost never rewards; they explicitly choose no reward for most rounds despite seeing many cases where others contribute substantially. When they finally do use reward, it is targeted and limited rather than broad or proportional to contributions. Their rare use of reward appears to be a positive signal to specific co-players whose behavior currently aligns with a desired cooperative pattern. When the group is widely cooperative, they selectively reward a subset rather than the entire cooperative coalition, suggesting they value targeted reinforcement or alliance-style signaling more than generalized reciprocity. They do not seem to weigh the efficiency of reward heavily: given that reward is relatively inefficient compared to punishment, they nonetheless spend on it in at least one situation where cooperation is already high. This implies that the motive is not purely instrumental in terms of group payoff; it more likely reflects a desire to acknowledge or favor certain partners, or to experiment with whether extra encouragement will stabilize their preferred configuration. Because they pass up many opportunities to reward sustained high contributors, they are not a “gratitude maximizer” or a consistent reciprocity type. Reward is used sparingly, almost as a special tool when someone’s behavior stands out or the player wants to differentiate between co-players. Overall, they are a “selective, low-frequency rewarder”: reward is secondary in their toolbox, used mainly for occasional positive reinforcement or alliance-like gestures rather than as a primary device for sustaining cooperation.

They also never use the reward option. Even when surrounded by many consistently high contributors, and even when some others lavishly reward group members, they do not spend a single coin to positively reinforce good behavior. They likewise show no evidence of using reward strategically to build a coalition, reduce the chance of being punished, or foster reciprocity. This indicates a general reluctance to spend resources for the benefit of others, whether through positive or negative sanctions. Reward does not appear to function for them as a gratitude signal, alliance tool, or investment in future cooperation. In terms of type, they resemble a “pure free-rider on the sanctioning system”: they let others bear the cost of shaping group behavior while they simply adjust their own action levels. Their lack of reward behavior also suggests that prosocial emotions like gratitude or admiration, if present, are not strong enough (or not salient enough) to override narrow payoff considerations in this environment.

They never use rewards, despite seeing many others spend heavily to reward cooperators. This suggests they prioritize securing and preserving their own payoff over investing in long-run reciprocity through positive incentives, even when the reward technology is relatively favorable. They do not appear to “tip” high contributors or repay those who benefit them indirectly. Their giving is thus not coupled to a reinforcement strategy aimed at stabilizing cooperation via rewards. The consistent absence of rewarding behavior indicates that, for them, cooperation is its own decision problem: they contribute what seems appropriate without additionally paying to shape others’ behavior. They likely view rewarding as optional or inefficient, or they may believe that the prevailing high-contribution norm is strong enough without their intervention. Gratitude, if felt, is not expressed in costly transfers. In type terms, they are a “non-rewarding conditional cooperator”: they follow cooperative norms but do not invest extra to strengthen them.

This player also never uses reward, despite the reward mechanism being relatively efficient and heavily used by some other participants. They receive rewards multiple times yet do not reciprocate or join in rewarding high contributors. This suggests either indifference to gratitude signaling, a preference not to intervene in others’ outcomes, or a very strict cost-minimizing stance with respect to second-order actions (even prosocial ones). There is no evidence of targeted reinforcement of high contributors, coalition-building via mutual rewarding, or attempts to steer group behavior. Their strategy regarding reward is purely passive: they accept rewards but never spend to give them, even when many others do. As a type, they are a non-rewarder who decouples their own cooperation from any second-order prosocial signaling.

They never reward anyone, despite consistently high contributions from many group members and extensive reward activity by others. The reward technology is relatively efficient, but they still refrain from ever using it, suggesting that any value they place on others’ welfare, reciprocity, or strategic reputation-building is outweighed by their desire to conserve own payoff each round. They do not appear to adopt a “carrot-and-stick” strategy at all; instead, they ignore the reward channel and treat others’ rewarding behavior as irrelevant for their own actions.

This player is also very restrained with rewards. Strategic reinforcement is minimal; if they have a reinforcement strategy at all, it is one-off and symbolic rather than sustained.

This player consistently refrains from rewarding others. They never spend coins to increase others’ payoffs, even when some peers contribute very generously or spend heavily to reward or punish. This suggests that, in their cost-benefit calculus, positive reinforcement is not worth the personal expense, even though it is technologically more efficient than punishment. Several interpretations are consistent with this pattern: they may believe cooperation can be sustained via baseline conditional contributions and punishment alone; they may view rewards as too easily misallocated (since rewarders are anonymous and could be rewarding “undeserving” players); or they may simply prioritize keeping their own payoff high and regard rewarding as a luxury. Because they do not reward even high, stable contributors, they do not signal gratitude in the payoff stage. Their notion of fairness seems more about sanctioning negative deviations than about compensating or applauding positive ones. In new environments, this type continues to rely on contribution choices and selective punishment to shape behavior, not on explicit positive reinforcement. Overall, they are a “non-rewarding enforcer”: comfortable spending to punish norm violators, but averse to spending to reward cooperators, regardless of contribution level.

This player also never uses the reward mechanism, despite the fact that rewards are relatively efficient compared to punishment and many others invest heavily in rewarding. They abstain even when they themselves receive rewards, indicating they do not reciprocate via the mechanism. The consistent non-use of rewards implies either a general reluctance to spend on others beyond contributions, a belief that good behavior should not require side payments, or uncertainty or disinterest about managing others’ incentives. There is no evidence of strategic rewarding to build alliances, attract future favors, or nudge specific players. Their stance is effectively: contribute generously to the public good, but do not spend extra to privately subsidize or steer individuals.

This player never uses reward, despite repeated exposure to others spending substantial resources on rewarding high contributors and sometimes even low contributors. They show a willingness to reward, but this is even more selective than their punishment. They also combine reward with punishment in the same decision stage, directing opposite incentives to different individuals, consistent with a player who uses the incentive tools to shape the social landscape according to their personal judgments rather than following a simple “reward high contributors, punish low contributors” rule. They do not use rewards systematically to boost overall cooperation; instead, they deploy them in targeted interventions when motivated. This makes them an occasional, relationship- or event-driven rewarder rather than a general prosocial incentivizer.

They use rewards frequently but more selectively than punishment. This includes both consistently high contributors and those who increase or maintain strong contributions over time. Their rewards are small in number compared to their punishments, implying that they treat rewarding as a complementary reinforcement mechanism rather than the primary lever of norm enforcement. They are willing to pay nontrivial costs to give extra payoffs to high contributors even when it is not obviously profitable for them individually, suggesting a preference for positively reinforcing group‑beneficial behavior. There is no strong evidence that they use reward in a narrowly strategic, coalition‑building manner; rather, they seem to reward based on observable contribution levels, not on whether a player has previously rewarded or protected them. This points to a principled “support the cooperators” stance rather than personalized reciprocity or gratitude signaling. Their pattern fits a type who tries to shape the environment with both “carrot and stick,” but whose main emphasis remains punishing low contribution.

This player essentially does not use the reward mechanism. In new environments, they are best characterized as a strong cooperator who relies primarily on personal contribution and, at most, occasional punishment of extreme free riders, not as a player who uses rewards to manage or encourage others.

This player never allocates resources to reward others, despite being a consistent full contributor and despite facing a mix of high and low contributors in the group. Their decision to avoid rewards is stable across the game and does not respond to experiencing small incoming rewards from others. In future PGG settings, they can be modeled as relying solely on their own cooperative contributions, not on reward mechanisms, to influence group outcomes.

This player never uses costly rewards, even though most others cooperate at high levels and several peers match their own full-cooperation pattern. This indicates that their cooperative norm is internally driven rather than sustained by reciprocity expectations. Overall, they are a pure contributor who leaves the reward channel unused, relying solely on their own consistent cooperation rather than active social reinforcement.

The player is also a *non-rewarder* in practice. They never spend resources to reward others, even though many group members match their full-cooperation strategy. The player never rewards anyone, despite many others consistently cooperating at the same high level as they do. They show no inclination to incur even small costs to provide positive reinforcement. The player never actively rewards others, even though they receives rewards from peers. They never reward anyone, even when some partners consistently contribute more than they do or suddenly increase contributions. This player shows a clear willingness to reward high contributors, but does so in a low-intensity, symbolic way. They never use rewards, even in situations where others contribute more than them or sustain high cooperation. This player uses the reward mechanism sparingly and in a way that is hard to interpret with certainty. They never use it once cooperation is smoothly established. This player is willing to spend on rewards but only under particular conditions, and their pattern suggests low instrumental use of the reward mechanism. Overall, they are reward-tolerant but not reward-driven, and they quickly revert to a simple “contribute max, ignore rewards” heuristic once the group norm is strong.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, even when others reward them. Their zero use of the reward mechanism is consistent and unconditional. The player never spends on rewards and receives only occasional rewards from others. They never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite repeated opportunities and despite other players using the reward option at several points. They never spend any coins on reward throughout the game, despite consistently high contributions by most others and a generally cooperative climate. They eventually use the reward mechanism extremely heavily and in an economically irrational way, sacrificing large amounts of coins to send small rewards. Their willingness to “give all my coins” and then follow through with huge expenditures shows that once they decide to reward, they do so extravagantly. Overall, they treat reward as a toy or as a way to dramatize their disengagement from profit, not as a calculated cooperative tool.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others, despite many opportunities and despite receiving rewards themselves in multiple rounds. They never spend any coins on reward at any point. This is stable even when rewards are being discussed and when they themselves are rewarded by others. Their non-use of rewards does not change as the group composition or contribution levels fluctuate, suggesting a fixed preference or rule such as “never pay to reward under this cost structure.”

They never spend any of their own coins to reward others, despite repeated opportunities and the presence of identifiable high contributors. They never spend any coins on rewards, even though rewards are available and others are using them. They never spend any coins on reward, despite the mechanism being available each round and despite receiving positive rewards from others multiple times. They rarely use the reward mechanism, and when they do, they spend a moderate but non-trivial amount in two separate episodes rather than small, continuous amounts. This player is an unusually heavy and persistent rewarder despite the strongly inefficient reward technology. They frequently spend a substantial fraction of their payoff on rewards across the game. This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite being in strongly cooperative environments where many others contribute fully. They never use the reward mechanism, despite frequent opportunities and repeated high contributions from almost all others. They never use the reward option, even in environments with strong group cooperation where many others contribute fully. They never use the reward mechanism, even in periods of strong cooperation where rewarding could reinforce prosocial behavior. They never use reward, even when others contribute at the same high level as themselves. They never use reward despite near-universal high cooperation and eventually perfect group cooperation. This indicates a norm of “cooperation as duty,” not “cooperation as praiseworthy extra effort.” They also do not deploy reward strategically to encourage former free riders to keep cooperating once they improve, which suggests they either (a) do not think reward is needed when the group already converges to high contributions, or (b) are generally disinclined toward paying for positive incentives. Their sanctioning pattern (willingness to punish but not reward) is asymmetrical: they invest against norm violators but not in favor of norm followers, consistent with a personality type that emphasizes accountability and responsibility more than gratitude signaling or strategic bonding.

They never use the reward option, despite it being available every round. They never provide rewards, despite having many opportunities to reward consistent full contributors and despite others actively using reward. This is not due to lack of obvious cooperative targets: the group has multiple stable full contributors and some players are already rewarding and being rewarded. Their refusal to participate in the reward mechanism suggests either that they treat the public good contribution itself as sufficient acknowledgment, that they do not value tactical use of rewards to shape behavior, or that they are generally reluctant to spend coins on any secondary mechanism (punishment or reward). Their cooperation appears to be “non-incentive-managing”: they cooperate themselves but do not invest in institutionalized carrots or sticks.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite being in a highly cooperative group. This is not because reward is absent or obscure; others use rewards at various points. Their stance appears to be that maintaining and enforcing norms should go through punishment rather than positive reinforcement. They do not engage in strategic alliance-building via rewards, nor do they reciprocate the cooperative majority with bonuses. This reveals a “stick but no carrot” enforcement style: they uphold cooperation by sanctioning norm violators rather than by incentivizing or thanking cooperators.

They never allocate rewards to anyone, despite many players contributing fully and some others using reward as positive reinforcement. They forgo low-cost opportunities to strengthen cooperation or reciprocate kindness through the reward channel, even when they themselves receive rewards. This consistent non-use of rewards suggests they either do not value the reward mechanism, prefer to conserve coins once the initial punishment burst is over, or treat contributions as self-sufficient signals that do not require extra reinforcement. There is no selective rewarding of high contributors, no alliance-like reward patterns, and no final-round “thank you” rewarding. The absence of any reward behavior contrasts with their early willingness to spend heavily on punishment, pointing toward a sanctioning style that is skewed (when active) toward negative rather than positive incentives.

They never spend on rewards, even though they receive small rewards from others. This suggests either an efficiency‑minded stance that sees reward as a wasteful transfer (since it is 1:1 net neutral) or simply a preference to let contributions themselves be the main cooperative signal. They also do not verbally promise rewards or express intentions to use them, and they do not differentiate between high contributors with positive incentives. Their cooperative enforcement strategy is asymmetric: they rely on rare punishment for norm breaking and social reinforcement via chat rather than monetary rewards for norm adherence. Whether they would ever reward in different parameter regimes (e.g., more efficient rewards) is unknown.

The player is an active and relatively heavy user of rewards. They repeatedly spend non-trivial amounts to reward others, despite reward having zero net efficiency gain in this setup. This indicates that they do not treat reward purely as a cost-benefit instrument; instead, they use it normatively (to acknowledge cooperation) or strategically (to try to sustain or elevate group contributions). They target rewards in a way that appears roughly aligned with contribution levels: when others are all cooperating, they increase reward spending; when some defect, they do not reduce their own contributions but may modulate reward intensity. The pattern suggests a “positive sanctioning” style: they try to shape the social environment using approval instead of punishment (which is unavailable). They also continue to reward late in the game, which is inconsistent with a purely forward-looking incentive logic and more consistent with expressing gratitude, reinforcing norms, or enjoying the act of rewarding. Overall, they are willing to sacrifice personal payoff to support and recognize cooperators.

This player uses rewards actively and selectively. They are willing to spend their own coins to reward others, sometimes at a noticeable cost to their own round payoff. Since reward is exactly cost‑neutral in this environment (the cost to the rewarder matches the benefit to the recipient), their use of reward cannot be justified by direct material gain and instead reflects other motives: reinforcing cooperation, reciprocity, signaling appreciation, or attempting to shape partners’ future behavior. They do not reward mechanically every round, nor do they appear to reward defectors systematically. Reward use is intermittent and likely targeted toward players who contribute, especially when cooperation is fragile or when they themselves are cooperating. This suggests a strategy of positive reinforcement: they are willing to “pay to encourage good behavior,” consistent with a prosocial preference for supporting contributors, even though there is no direct multiplier on rewards. Their willingness to pay for this cost‑neutral transfer indicates they value social or strategic aspects beyond immediate earnings, such as sustaining a cooperative climate or expressing approval. They also adjust reward intensity over time, sometimes using more reward and sometimes none, which suggests learning and experimentation rather than a fixed heuristic. In summary, they are a “conditional rewarder”: they use reward as a tool to support cooperation and reciprocate, but in a measured and context‑dependent way rather than as a blanket rule.

This player never spends coins to reward anyone, despite frequently being in situations where others cooperate and despite repeatedly receiving rewards themselves. Thus, they do not use the reward mechanism as a tool for norm enforcement, gratitude, or alliance building. The fact that rewarding is cost-neutral in this environment (each unit costs as much as it gives to the target) means their refusal to reward is not about monetary sacrifice in net terms; it is more consistent with indifference to the reward tool, inattention to that stage, or a preference for simple, contribution-only strategies. They appear to separate their own cooperative behavior from any responsibility to actively incentivize or acknowledge others' cooperation. There is no evidence of contingent rewarding (e.g., rewarding only high contributors) or of strategic reciprocity through rewards. Their approach to reward is best described as “non-user”: they rely on their own contributions rather than social reinforcement mechanisms to shape group behavior.

They almost never reward. Across the game they make a single, non‑trivial reward allocation to a player who had been repeatedly targeted with heavy punishment for low contributions. This reward occurs in a context where that recipient is an unpopular, norm‑violating target rather than a model cooperator. This isolated act can be interpreted in two low‑confidence ways: (1) a small, possibly emotional or fairness‑based gesture toward an extremely disadvantaged player, or (2) an experimental or noise move without a clear systematic logic. Because they do not reward high cooperators more generally, and do not repeat or build on this pattern, there is no evidence that they use rewards strategically to reinforce cooperation or to build alliances. In practice, they behave as an almost purely non‑rewarding type: they keep their resources for themselves and very rarely invest in others’ payoffs, even when many group members cooperate at high levels.

They never allocate positive rewards to anyone despite having many opportunities and observing multiple stable high contributors. This is notable given that several other players do use rewards. Their refusal to reward is stable across cooperative and non-cooperative phases, indicating either that they discount the value of reward as a tool, prioritize maintaining their own payoff when it comes to positive transfers, or see high contributions as expected behavior that does not merit additional compensation. Given that they are willing to spend considerably on punishment, the asymmetry suggests a “stick but no carrot” enforcement style. They seem to rely exclusively on negative incentives to uphold their cooperation norm and do not attempt to strategically reinforce or signal gratitude to good contributors.

They never reward anyone, including in situations where others cooperate near fully and where they themselves receive occasional rewards from others. This holds across both highly cooperative and more mixed contribution environments. Given the reward mechanism is relatively inefficient (it costs more than it transfers), their refusal to reward can be read as payoff‑maximizing in a narrow sense; but some peers do expend resources on rewards, indicating that prosocial rewarding is a live option in this group. Their complete abstention suggests they do not seek to positively reinforce others’ contributions or to express gratitude for being rewarded themselves. There is no evidence they use rewards strategically—for instance, to build a reputation or encourage tolerance toward their own free‑riding—despite anonymity limiting reputational benefits. They behave as if any positive transfer to others, even small, is not worth the cost. This is consistent with a type that is focused on own monetary payoff and does not value fostering goodwill via rewards.

The player effectively does not use the reward mechanism. Across the interaction they give no rewards, despite often being surrounded by consistently high contributors and despite occasionally being rewarded by others. Given the technology (reward is relatively inefficient compared to punishment), their behavior suggests they see punishment as the primary and more effective enforcement tool. There is no observed attempt to positively reinforce cooperative partners or show gratitude through reward, even toward consistently high contributors or those who reward them. This makes their enforcement style one-sided: they rely on negative sanctions only. They appear motivated more by deterring or disciplining defectors than by actively encouraging or thanking cooperators.

This player has a strong asymmetry between punishment and reward: they almost never use the reward option, and when they do, it is modest and late. The rare use of reward is directed toward a participant who consistently bears large punishment costs while continuing to contribute fully, which fits a narrative of acknowledging costly norm-enforcing cooperation. They never systematically reward high contributors or those who increase contributions, nor do they use reward to offset the harshness of others’ punishment on targets. This asymmetry is consistent with a belief that negative sanctions are the primary way to maintain cooperation, whereas positive reinforcement is secondary, weak, or not worth its poor cost-effectiveness in this mechanism. Their occasional reward appears more like a symbolic gesture toward a particularly “deserving” cooperator than a deliberate strategic tool to shift broader group behavior. In general, they are a punishment-focused, not reward-focused, norm enforcer.

This player never spends any of their own resources on rewarding others, despite the presence of a one‑for‑one reward mechanism and despite regularly being on the receiving end of small and occasionally larger rewards. They therefore act as a pure non‑rewarder in this environment. Their refusal to reward persists under both high and low group contribution conditions, so it does not seem contingent on whether others are doing “well enough.” They do not use rewards to encourage already high contributors, to thank those who sustain cooperation, or to attempt to rescue cooperation when group contributions sag. They also do not reciprocate the rewards they receive: their own reward behavior remains at zero even when others clearly signal approval of their high contributions. From this, the most grounded interpretation is that they adopt a payoff‑preserving, non‑altruistic stance toward second‑order cooperation: they are willing to give to the public good itself but are not willing to spend extra to reinforce or shape others’ behavior through the reward instrument. Whether this is driven by a principled dislike of the reward mechanism, a belief that rewards are ineffective, simple self‑interest at the second‑order level, or inattention is unknown. What is clear is that they separate first‑order cooperation (where they are generous) from second‑order cooperation (where they are strictly non‑contributing).

The player never spends their own coins to reward others, despite having multiple opportunities and frequently benefitting from high contributions by peers. They consistently treat the reward mechanism as dominated: if reward is one-to-one in cost and benefit, they appear to reason that using it is not personally advantageous. There is no evidence they use rewards to signal gratitude, stabilize cooperation, or reciprocate to high contributors; they accept rewards when given but do not reciprocate through the mechanism. This refusal to reward persists regardless of whether others cooperate a lot or a little, suggesting a stable stance rather than situational reluctance. It points to a self-interested approach to costly prosocial tools: they are willing to be moderately cooperative via contributions themselves, but they avoid second-order cooperation (paying to support or encourage others’ cooperation).
