They may implicitly rely on the public good mechanism and on others’ propensity to reward or cooperate, but they do not take on the role of norm enforcer or positive sanctioner. There is insufficient information to infer whether they would reward if the reward technology were more efficient (e.g., leverage greater impact per unit cost), so their responsiveness to different institutional conditions is unknown. In the present setting, their type is best characterized as a non-rewarding, strategically cooperative player who focuses on direct contribution choices and ignores costly reinforcement mechanisms.

The player is generally very reluctant to use the reward mechanism. Across the game, they almost always refrain from rewarding, and when they do reward, they do so at low levels and infrequently. They do not use reward as a systematic tool to shape behavior; there is no clear pattern of consistently rewarding high contributors or withholding from low contributors in a predictable, rule-like way. When they do spend coins on rewards, it tends to coincide with situations in which they are themselves contributing relatively high amounts or when the group has been reasonably cooperative overall. This suggests that their primary trigger is appreciation or momentary reinforcement of cooperation rather than retaliation or strategic manipulation. They do not appear to reward in response to having personally received rewards in a tit-for-tat manner; their own incoming rewards do not clearly map onto outgoing rewards. Thus, there is limited evidence of strong reciprocity on the rewarding dimension. The player appears to treat rewards as secondary to their own contribution decisions: their main way of participating in the public good is by adjusting how much they contribute, not by investing in incentivizing others. The reluctance to spend on rewards may reflect a belief that the one-to-one cost-to-benefit ratio makes rewarding financially neutral and therefore not worth systematic use, or a simple preference to keep coins rather than engage in costly signaling. Their sparing use of rewards also implies that they do not see themselves as an active “norm enforcer” through positive incentives, even though they are often willing to cooperate via direct contribution.

The player is an active, willing rewarder and frequently spends a nontrivial portion of their income to reward others. They repeatedly allocate positive amounts to the reward mechanism even though each unit of reward is exactly cost-equivalent to what the recipient gets, meaning there is no direct efficiency gain from rewarding in this setup. This indicates they view rewards as a behavioral signal or social tool rather than a pure efficiency lever. Their use of rewards is selective rather than constant. They sometimes choose not to reward at all, and the magnitude of reward varies over time. The pattern suggests that they are responsive to others’ contribution levels: they are more inclined to reward when group behavior looks more cooperative and withhold or reduce reward when contributions drop. Because they cannot identify who rewarded them (and are rarely rewarded themselves), their reward behavior is not straightforward reciprocity toward individual benefactors, but more likely reinforcement of perceived high contributors or of collective cooperation. They does not appear to use reward as a substitute for decreasing their own contributions; instead, they combine maximal contribution with positive reinforcement of others. This implies a “pro-social enforcer” orientation that prefers to uphold and encourage cooperation via carrots rather than via strategic withholding or punishment (which is unavailable here anyway). They does not react strongly to receiving or not receiving rewards: there is no observable shift in their contribution policy following being rewarded, nor clear evidence that receiving reward alters their own willingness to reward. Overall, they behaves like a principled, generous reward-giver who uses the mechanism to support a cooperative norm, even at personal cost and without identifiable reputational return.

The player never uses the reward mechanism, even though they repeatedly receive positive rewards from others. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal resources on reinforcing others’ behavior, despite clearly observing variation in peer contributions and having many opportunities where prosocial rewarding of high contributors would be straightforward. They do not appear to use rewards as a tool for norm enforcement, gratitude, or signaling alliance with other cooperators. Their strategy focuses entirely on direct contribution rather than on second-order cooperation via incentives. This could reflect a belief that rewarding is inefficient or unnecessary, a preference to avoid additional complexity, or simple inattention to the reward stage; which of these is accurate is unknown. There is no shift over time toward rewarding, even as patterns of cooperation and rewards received change, implying that their non-rewarding stance is stable rather than exploratory. Overall, the player is a pure cooperator who does not engage in strategic reinforcement of others’ actions.

The player consistently refrains from using the reward mechanism. Across the game, they never spend any of their own coins to reward others, even when many peers contribute generously and even when they themselves receive rewards from others. This implies a strong reluctance to bear personal cost purely to reinforce others’ cooperation. They may see rewarding as strategically unprofitable in this environment, given that each unit of reward costs exactly as much as it gives, with no multiplier and no way to build a differentiated reputation. From a payoff-maximizing standpoint, their behavior aligns with a belief that rewarding cannot increase their own material returns enough to justify the expense. They also do not appear to use rewards to express gratitude or moral approval. Despite being on the receiving end of rewards at various times, they never reciprocate through the reward channel. This suggests either low valuation of symbolic reciprocity, skepticism that rewards change others’ behavior in a meaningful way, or simple indifference to that dimension of the game. Their combination of moderate, conditional contributions and zero use of reward indicates they prefer to “speak” only through their own contribution levels rather than actively shaping the group via costly incentives. Socially, this corresponds to a quiet, self-protective cooperator type: willing to contribute, but unwilling to pay extra to enforce or encourage norms in others.

They never allocate any rewards, despite having the option in every round. This persistent non-use of the reward mechanism holds across periods of both high and low group cooperation. The absence of even small, symbolic rewards strongly suggests that they either (a) see no cost-effective strategic value in rewarding, (b) are indifferent to expressing gratitude or approval through costly transfers, or (c) prefer to react purely via their own contribution levels. Their own receipt of rewards does not visibly change their subsequent use of the reward tool: even when others reward them, they do not reciprocate via the same channel. This makes it unlikely that they are motivated by a desire to build direct reciprocity through rewards, or that they are strongly moved to “thank” others monetarily. Instead, their main response to the social environment is to adjust their personal contribution, not to directly incentivize or recognize specific players. Strategically, this places them as a “non-rewarding conditional cooperator”: they rely on contribution adjustment rather than positive sanctioning to signal approval or disapproval. They may implicitly delegate the role of rewarding to others, or they may believe that the marginal impact of reward in this setting is too small or too uncertain to justify the cost. Personality-wise, this is consistent with a more self-contained, efficiency-focused style: they act on their own account, avoid extra-cost social gestures, and express their evaluations of the group through how much they personally contribute rather than through explicit rewards.

This player is an active and sometimes heavy user of the reward mechanism. They repeatedly spend coins to reward others, including multiple instances of substantial expenditure on rewards, even though each rewarded coin is personally costly on a one-to-one basis. This demonstrates a willingness to incur direct costs to reinforce behaviors they approve of, most plausibly high or above-average contributions to the public good. Their reward use fluctuates: some rounds they give nothing, others they give small amounts, and occasionally they invest very heavily. This variability indicates that their rewarding is conditional rather than automatic; they seem to respond to perceived contribution patterns, increasing rewards when they see many high contributors or when they wish to bolster cooperative behavior after a dip. They also continue to reward even though the technology offers no multiplier and does not let them identify who rewarded them, implying that their motivation is not solely strategic reputation-building toward specific partners. Instead, they act more like a prosocial conditional rewarder: using rewards as a norm-signaling tool to support cooperation in the group at large. Their own receipt of rewards does not obviously control their behavior; they do not simply “reward back” in a tit-for-tat style because identities of rewarders are hidden. Gratitude signaling is thus diffuse: they may be thanking “the cooperator set” rather than specific individuals. At times, they abstain from rewarding even when cooperation is relatively high, which suggests they are also tracking their own payoff constraints and not willing to sacrifice indefinitely. In summary, they use rewards strategically but in a norm-based and group-focused way, prioritizing reinforcement of cooperative contributions over maximizing their immediate private income.

This player uses the reward mechanism very sparingly. Rewards are rare, small, and not systematically tied to the most cooperative contribution profiles. This suggests that while they understand and occasionally employ positive incentives, they do not view active rewarding as their primary tool for shaping group behavior. Instead, their main behavioral lever is adjusting their own contribution level. Because rewards are not given consistently to high contributors, their use of reward does not strongly resemble a deliberate reputation-building or norm-enforcing strategy. It may reflect momentary gratitude, experiments with the mechanism, or attempts to nudge behavior in isolated instances rather than a coherent policy. They also receive rewards in several rounds, but they do not clearly respond to being rewarded by increasing or stabilizing contributions in a predictable way; any such linkage is at best weak and uncertain. Taken together, this indicates a type who is open to prosocial reinforcement in principle but is either unconvinced of its cost-effectiveness, not strongly motivated to “manage” others through rewards, or simply more focused on their own contribution choices as their main expression of cooperation and conditionality. Their reward behavior is best characterized as low-intensity, unsystematic, and secondary to their contribution strategy.

This player is an active but selective rewarder, and also a notable recipient of others’ rewards. Their willingness to spend on rewarding shows that they treat the reward mechanism as a meaningful tool for shaping behavior, not merely as an occasional gesture. They accept real cost to themselves to reinforce others, which implies a pro-social or norm-enforcing motive that is not purely self-interested in a one-shot sense. Rewarding is not constant; there are phases of no reward and phases of substantial reward use. This suggests that rewards are triggered by specific patterns rather than applied mechanically. The pattern is consistent with conditional reciprocity: when they feel supported or when others contribute cooperatively, they become more likely to invest in rewards. When they appear less satisfied with the group’s behavior or with how much they themselves are rewarded, they hold back on giving. They also receive significant rewards in at least one period, and more modest rewards in several others. After receiving conspicuously high rewards, they neither turn into a pure free-rider nor into a relentless maximal rewarder. Instead, they continue to cooperate at reasonably high levels and later spend nontrivial amounts on rewarding others. This is consistent with gratitude signaling: being rewarded strengthens their inclination to cultivate cooperation, including by incurring costs to reward. However, their rewarding remains bounded and context-sensitive. They do not indiscriminately reward all high contributors every round, suggesting they likely use simple heuristics such as rewarding perceived strong cooperators or reciprocating recent kindness, rather than implementing a strict payoff-maximizing or tit-for-tat reward algorithm. Overall, they function as a conditional, relational rewarder: willing to pay to encourage and reciprocate cooperation, but not at any cost and not in the absence of supportive group behavior.

The player never uses the reward option despite repeatedly being in groups where others contribute very highly and where they themselves receive some rewards from others. Their behavior characterizes them as a “non-rewarding conditional cooperator.” They appear to treat the reward stage as individually inefficient: since the cost of rewarding equals the benefit to the recipient, they may evaluate it as neutral at the social level and negative at the personal level, with no multiplier to justify the expense. This likely leads them to conserve all payoff rather than invest in incentivizing others, even when others behave in highly cooperative ways. There is no evidence that they reward high contributors or attempt to signal gratitude or build reputation through rewards. Their own receipt of rewards in multiple rounds does not induce them to start rewarding, suggesting that reciprocity via the reward channel is not part of their strategy. Their conditional cooperation seems to rely on observing contributions directly, not on using or responding to reward as a reputational or motivational tool. Unknowns: it is unclear whether they conceptually understand reward as a possible coordination device and consciously reject it, or simply ignore the mechanism. It is also unknown whether they differentiate between high and low contributors in their underlying attitudes; all that is observable is that they do not express those attitudes via reward.

They never spend on rewards despite consistently high contributions and despite others in the group actively rewarding. This indicates a strong tendency to see rewarding as an unnecessary cost, even under conditions where rewards are commonly used to reinforce cooperation. They appear to rely on direct contribution choices and verbal encouragement, rather than material incentives, to sustain group behavior. They do not reciprocate when they personally receive rewards, suggesting that they are not using reward as a strategic reciprocity tool or as an expression of gratitude. This pattern points to either a payoff-maximizing stance toward the reward mechanism, a belief that cooperation is already sufficiently stable without extra incentives, or simple reluctance to incur any costs beyond contribution itself. Their use of gratitude remains purely verbal.

The player never uses the reward mechanism despite multiple opportunities and despite seeing others actively reward high contributors. They sometimes receive small rewards from others, but do not reciprocate even when the group uses rewards to reinforce cooperation. This strongly suggests a reluctance to spend personal payoff on rewarding, even when it could strategically sustain high contributions. Their incentive structure appears to treat reward as a net cost with insufficient personal benefit rather than as a tool for reciprocity or norm enforcement. They are thus a “reward free-rider”: they accept benefits from others’ reward spending but do not invest their own coins into social incentives.

The player uses rewards sparingly and selectively. They do not systematically reward everyone who cooperates, and they also do not engage in any “counter-reward” of defectors (which is impossible in this setup but might have been mimicked via selective non-reward). When they do spend on rewards, they incur a real cost, so they are not strictly payoff-maximizing; however, reward use is limited enough that they still appear mindful of their own earnings. Their rewards seem to function as targeted positive reinforcement or expressions of appreciation rather than as a robust enforcement strategy. They do not join others in large-scale reward cascades or “everyone rewards everyone” behavior, suggesting a moderate, not exuberant, approach to altruistic gifting. There is no evidence that they attempt to use rewards strategically to manipulate future contributions; their reward behavior looks more like occasional acknowledgment than active incentive design.

They never spend coins to reward others, even when many players clearly cooperate at high levels and others use the reward mechanism generously. This suggests a strong preference for cost-free cooperation over costly social enforcement or recognition. Their own cooperative behavior does elicit rewards from others, but they do not reciprocate through the reward channel, implying that they do not view monetary reward as necessary for sustaining norms in this context, or they place a relatively high subjective cost on spending coins for that purpose. They also do not use rewards tactically to steer behavior or to thank especially cooperative partners. Their strategy appears to be “cooperate directly, let others handle rewards if they wish,” rather than participating actively in the reward economy. Whether this reflects principled frugality, uncertainty about reward efficacy, or simple inattention to the mechanism is unknown.

This player is willing to spend heavily on rewards, even when doing so is personally costly and provides no net material gain. At multiple points they invest substantial amounts of their payoff into rewarding others, to the extent of turning what would be a positive-earnings round into a very low or even negative payoff. This indicates strong pro-social motivation that is not explained by narrow self-interest. Their rewards appear targeted primarily toward high or relatively higher contributors, rather than indiscriminately. When others fully cooperate, they sometimes choose not to reward, but when some players under-contribute while others still contribute substantially, they direct rewards toward the more cooperative partners. This pattern suggests that reward serves as a selective reinforcement of cooperative behavior and an expression of approval or gratitude toward those who match (or approximate) their contribution norm. They also use reward in contexts where they themselves receive little or no reward from others, demonstrating that their willingness to reward does not strictly depend on symmetric reciprocity. The behavior is more consistent with a “pro-social peer enforcer” type who uses reward to encourage and sustain high group contributions, even at personal cost. They do not experiment with using reward as a bargaining chip or conditional threat; it functions as positive reinforcement rather than as part of an explicit strategy of contingent reciprocity.

They never spend any resources on rewarding others, despite having several opportunities where others contribute more than them and do reward them. They forgo even cost-neutral or break-even uses of the reward mechanism (where they could potentially shape behavior without improving material payoffs), indicating a consistent reluctance to incur any personal cost for social reinforcement. This suggests either that they view the reward mechanism as strategically unnecessary, that they explicitly avoid spending on others even when they benefit from them, or that they do not fully engage with the secondary mechanism at all. Given their generally high contributions, their lack of rewarding is not due to an overall asocial or exploitative stance toward contributions alone; rather, it is specifically a refusal to expend extra resources beyond the public good decision. They appear to rely on others’ willingness to reward or cooperate without reciprocating via the reward channel, indicating a tendency toward being a “reward free rider”: they enjoy the benefits and signaling value of being rewarded but do not participate in the costly enforcement or encouragement of cooperation.

The player never spends anything on rewarding others, despite multiple opportunities and despite experiencing incoming rewards themselves. Given that the reward technology is one-to-one in cost and benefit, their refusal to use it suggests that they treat the reward mechanism as unnecessary or inefficient rather than as a tool for shaping behavior. Their non-use of rewards is consistent across contexts: they withhold reward when everyone cooperates, when someone free-rides, and when they themselves have been rewarded. There is no evidence of using rewards to thank high contributors, to build alliances, or to incentivize defectors to improve. This pattern indicates an “anti-reward” or “reward-skeptical” stance: the player prefers to express their cooperative preferences through their own contributions, not by spending extra to influence others’ payoffs. It also suggests that they either do not believe that rewarding will significantly change others’ behavior, or they are unwilling to sacrifice even a neutral-cost amount to engage in positive reciprocity via the mechanism.

They never use the reward option, even when others cooperate fully and maintain high contributions round after round. This is notable because they clearly value group cooperation (as seen in their own contributions and occasional positive remarks) but do not spend personal resources to materially reinforce that behavior. Their non-use spans both periods of perfect cooperation and periods where some players return to cooperation after deviating. This could indicate that they regard reward as economically inefficient, unnecessary given already-high cooperation, or simply not salient. It may also reflect a general reluctance to spend for social influence, consistent with their avoidance of both costly punishment and costly reward. They limit their prosociality to contribution decisions rather than engaging in higher-order incentive provision.

They never reward anyone, even in a consistently high-cooperation environment with multiple full contributors and some players who publicly encourage cooperation. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend on positive incentives, which is rational given the inefficient reward technology but still informative about their style: they enforce norms through their own contributions and, occasionally, punishment rather than through material gratitude or strategic positive reinforcement. Their choice suggests they either see reward as too costly/ineffective or do not feel the need to materially acknowledge good behavior when everyone is merely doing what they regard as the baseline expectation.

This player does not use the reward mechanism at any point, even when others fully cooperate and even though they express verbal approval. Their absence of material reward is consistent across conditions: with full cooperation, with past defection that has been corrected, and when they verbally praise the group. This pattern suggests they value efficiency in their sanctioning tools: given that the formal reward technology is relatively inefficient (cost exceeds benefit delivered), they may judge it not worth using, preferring costless verbal praise. There is no evidence they strategically withhold rewards to manipulate others; rather, their behavior aligns with a norm that cooperation is expected and does not warrant costly extra payments, whereas defection may warrant costly punishment at least once.

They never use rewards, even though there is sustained high cooperation from nearly all players and multiple instances where rewarding could credibly signal approval or build alliances. This is notable given that most others match their cooperative level, but they still refrain from paying to recognize or reinforce it. This may indicate a focus on conserving own payoff when it comes to costly positive transfers, or a perception that cooperation is already stable and thus does not require extra reinforcement. Since they also avoid punishment, the broader pattern is a complete non-use of sanctioning mechanisms, not a selective rejection of only negative sanctions. Whether they conceptually approve of rewarding but judge it inefficient, or are generally averse to “interfering” with others’ payoffs, is unknown.

They very rarely reward; across the whole game they reward exactly once, and the target is a fully cooperative player. This indicates that they are willing, at least occasionally, to incur a small personal cost to provide a positive transfer to a cooperator. The reward is directed to someone already aligned with the cooperative norm, which is consistent with using reward as a form of positive reinforcement or appreciation rather than as an incentive to change behavior. However, they do not routinely reward cooperators, even when group cooperation is high and stable, so they do not appear to adopt a systematic rewarding strategy. Their default is abstaining from both punishment and reward, with a single exception that shows they recognize and are willing to support cooperative behavior in principle.

The player is very sparing with rewards and stops using them early. After a brief initial phase where they occasionally spend small amounts to reward specific others, they cease rewarding altogether and maintain this non-rewarding stance through the rest of the game, despite ongoing variation in others’ cooperation and extensive reward activity by others. They never engage in broad, systematic reinforcement of cooperators, and their total spending on reward stays low compared to heavy rewarders in the group. When they do reward, it appears selective and limited rather than generous or symbolic. There is no clear pattern of using reward to discipline defectors (not possible here) or to sustain high-contribution allies; instead, rewards look more like brief experiments or one-off acknowledgments. Once they appear to conclude that rewarding is not necessary or not beneficial for their purposes, they abandon it completely and do not re-engage, even when the environment changes (e.g., shifts in average contributions or others’ reward usage). This suggests either a payoff-conscious reluctance to spend on second-order cooperation, a belief that reward is strategically unimportant in this setting, or a preference for pure first-order cooperation (contribution) over meta-level interventions.

They never spend any coins on reward. Across diverse environments—high and low group contributions, high and low use of reward by others, early and late in the game—they consistently allocate zero to rewarding anyone. This is not because reward is unavailable or unused; several peers frequently deploy reward, sometimes at high personal cost, but this player abstains entirely. Their stance suggests either a strong payoff‑maximization motive with respect to the reward stage, a principle against using the reward mechanism, or a belief that rewarding is ineffective or unnecessary. There is no evidence that they selectively reward high contributors, allies, or rewarders; instead, their rule is simply not to reward at all. They thus function as a pure provider in the contribution stage and a pure non‑intervenor in the reward stage.

They reward others infrequently and at modest cost, compared with many peers who sometimes spend heavily on reward. Across the game, they are willing but restrained rewarders. They spend on rewards in only a minority of rounds, with relatively small total expenditures per rewarding episode. This indicates that material payoff still matters to them; they do not treat reward as a domain for large, expressive spending. When they do reward, it is generally in cooperative environments where many others are contributing. There is some evidence that they reward in contexts where group contribution is high and broadly shared, which is consistent with reinforcing cooperative equilibria rather than trying to convert defectors. There is no clear pattern of targeting frequent low contributors with rewards to lure them into cooperation; instead, their rewards appear more aligned with acknowledging cooperative norms already in place. They sometimes reward while also being rewarded, but the total flows are usually small and roughly balanced. They never engage in extreme “reward storms” (mass, high-cost rewarding) seen in a few others, suggesting they do not see reward as a performative or expressive tool. Their reward behavior is best described as cautious, low-intensity positive reinforcement, not a central instrument of their strategy.

They never spend any resources to reward others at any point, despite many opportunities and high levels of contribution by others. They also never use “symbolic” or small-cost rewards. This indicates an extremely low willingness to sacrifice own payoff even marginally to influence others’ behavior or to express approval. They also do not use the reward tool strategically to try to stabilize high contributions that benefit them; instead, they exploit whatever level of cooperation emerges at zero cost. They treat reward as dominated by keeping their own coins, even though others demonstrate a norm where cooperators are frequently rewarded. This strongly suggests they either (a) do not value social signaling at all in this environment, or (b) believe their own individual rewards will not meaningfully affect group cooperation and therefore are not worth the cost. In either case, they behave like a strictly self-regarding player with no prosocial reward motivation.

The player never spends any coins on rewards. This is consistent and unconditional: they refrain from rewarding when cooperation is high, when it is mixed, and when they themselves are rewarded. This indicates either that (a) they view rewarding as unnecessary or inefficient, (b) they are strongly payoff-maximizing with respect to the costly reward mechanism, or (c) they are following a “contribute but do not use incentives” heuristic. Their refusal to reward persists even when other players invest heavily in rewarding, including clear patterns of others encouraging contributors or particular individuals. They do not join in these reward cascades. They also do not appear to selectively reward any highly cooperative or generous peer; there is no indication of targeted reciprocity through the reward channel. Given that the cost and benefit of reward are symmetric here, their zero-reward policy suggests that they do not use the reward mechanism as a tool for norm enforcement, coordination, or alliance building. Instead, they rely on direct contribution choices and ignore the extra incentive lever. In strategic terms, they behave like a “pure contributor, non-rewarder”: they are willing to incur costs to fund the public good directly but not to manipulate others’ behavior via side-payments.

This player almost never spends their own coins on rewards. Across the game, they devote only a small amount of their wealth to rewarding others, in a few isolated instances. This places them well below the many group members who use reward generously or even extravagantly. When they do reward, the pattern suggests they are responding to clear pro-social signals from others (e.g., consistent contribution and/or visible reward-giving by some players), but such actions are rare and small-scale. They do not behave like a “pro-social enforcer” or “reward leader.” Instead, they treat reward as a minor, occasional gesture rather than a strategic tool. They do not engage in systematic strategic reinforcement: they do not consistently send reward to the highest contributors, nor to specific allies, nor to shift low contributors upward. Their rewards do not clearly condition on who cooperated in that particular round versus previous rounds. This makes it unlikely that they use reward as a serious instrument to engineer future group behavior. Overall, they have a strong preference to conserve coins rather than pay for others’ welfare. Reward is not part of their core strategy; it is at best a sporadic, low-intensity supplement.

They routinely use the reward mechanism and are willing to spend a small but persistent share of earnings to reward others. Their reward pattern is selective: they do not spread rewards widely each round, but instead give a few units to a subset of players. The triggers appear partly strategic and partly expressive. They sometimes reward well-known high cooperators and sometimes reward apparent allies or those whose actions benefit them indirectly. They also occasionally reward when they themselves defect, indicating they may be compensating for free-riding by offering small, targeted gratitude rather than by increasing contributions. The cost-benefit is balanced rather than altruistic; they rarely spend extremely large amounts and tend to keep their own payoff high, so reward is used as a low-cost signal or incentive rather than as major sacrifice. Their use of reward is consistent across early, middle, and late game, indicating a stable preference for maintaining a cooperative atmosphere through low-level positive reinforcement instead of via own contributions.

They spend on reward extremely rarely and in small amounts relative to total earnings. When they do reward, it coincides with generally high group contributions and substantial prosocial rewarding by others, suggesting some weak inclination to “join” a cooperative, reward-heavy phase. However, this rewarding is not sustained, not targeted in any clearly strategic pattern, and does not develop into systematic reinforcement of high contributors. They never use reward as a tool to shape behavior over time; it functions more as a sporadic, low-cost gesture than as a core part of their strategy. Most of the time they simply avoid spending on reward, preserving their coins.

This player almost never spends coins to reward others. Across the game, their own spending on reward is extremely low relative to both (a) the number of opportunities they have and (b) the high frequency with which other players do spend on rewards. They are clearly aware of the mechanism and its cost, and they repeatedly see others using it, but they largely abstain. The very rare instance in which they do spend on reward occurs while they themselves are free-riding on contributions, which is a noteworthy combination: they are willing in that moment to pay a small cost to transfer a smaller amount to someone else while simultaneously contributing nothing. This suggests that, if they reward at all, it is more about signaling or opportunistic relational management than about establishing a systematic norm of “reward all cooperators.” The behavior could reflect an attempt to maintain or attract goodwill despite not contributing, but the data are too sparse to infer a stable reward strategy; confidence on the underlying motive is low. They do not use reward to systematically reinforce high contributors or to try to steer group behavior. They also do not appear to use reward to reciprocate rewards they personally receive; they are rewarded by others multiple times but do not adopt rewarding as a norm in response. Overall, they behave as a near non-rewarder: they treat the reward mechanism as mostly not worth its cost, and they do not invest in social incentives through rewards.

This player almost never uses the reward mechanism and, when they do, they spend only a small amount and only in the very first and one of the last rounds. Across the game they do not engage in any sustained pattern of rewarding high contributors or allies. They receive rewards from others multiple times but do not reciprocate in any targeted or consistent manner, despite the fact that identities of rewarders are hidden (so there is no direct partner to reward back). Their sparse use of reward is not obviously contingent on others’ contributions; they refrain from rewarding even when some peers clearly contribute very high amounts over many rounds. This suggests they view the reward mechanism as strategically unappealing at the given cost‑benefit ratio, or are generally reluctant to sacrifice own payoff to encourage or acknowledge others. They do not appear to experiment with reward as a tool for influencing future behavior; any rewards they give look more like isolated, low‑information gestures than part of a reinforcement strategy. Overall, they are best characterized as a near‑non‑rewarder who focuses on own contribution choices rather than on social incentives.

This player never spends on reward at any point. Their behavior indicates a strict reluctance to incur personal costs to reinforce cooperation, even when others give very high contributions or when some peers visibly invest large amounts in the reward mechanism. This is not because they themselves do not receive rewards; they do receive small positive rewards in various phases, but they do not reciprocate in kind. The combination of high contribution and zero reward spending suggests that, for them, “doing my part” consists in contributing to the public good itself, not in paying extra to manage others’ incentives. Their non-use of reward likely reflects one or more of: a focus on payoff efficiency (the reward technology is socially inefficient, so they avoid it), a view that rewarding is not their responsibility, indifference to signaling gratitude, or skepticism that rewards can meaningfully change behavior in this environment. They do not appear to condition their contributions on having been rewarded; they continue to cooperate without “thanking” or strategically reinforcing rewarders. Overall, they behave as a pure contributor rather than a second-order cooperator and can be classified as a non-rewarding cooperator.

This player is an unusually frequent and generous rewarder relative to typical efficiency considerations, especially in earlier and mid stages. They repeatedly spend substantial amounts of their own payoff to grant relatively small rewards to others, indicating that they value shaping others’ behavior or expressing approval even when it is personally costly. Their rewarding is selective rather than random. The pattern strongly suggests that they target players who either (a) contribute high amounts, (b) are persistent cooperators despite others’ free‑riding, or (c) have been rewarding or supportive themselves. They seem to use reward as a positive reinforcement tool to stabilize high-contribution norms and, possibly, to reciprocate perceived generosity. They do not appear to “buy popularity” broadly; they are willing to spend heavily but not on every player. This indicates a strategic element: they reward to encourage specific behaviors or actors rather than to equalize outcomes. Over time, however, the intensity of their rewarding declines, particularly toward the end of the game, suggesting that they view reward primarily as an investment in future interactions rather than as an intrinsic moral duty. At a personality level (inferred with low to moderate confidence), this pattern is consistent with a “prosocial strategist”: they are willing to incur real costs to nudge the group toward cooperation, but only when there is enough future left for that investment to matter.

They never spend any coins on rewarding others in any round. This is consistent even when others contribute very generously and when there are clear, repeated high contributors. They consistently receive small amounts of reward from others but do not reciprocate. There is no sign that they use rewards strategically to shape future group behavior or to express gratitude. Given that reward is costly and they prioritize maximizing their own payoff, they treat the reward mechanism as something to benefit from, not to use. They behave like a pure reward consumer, not a reward provider. This suggests that prosocial signaling or norm-enforcement via reward is not important for them, or at least not important enough to justify personal cost. Their reward behavior is thus strictly self-regarding and non-participatory.

This player is a very light and selective user of the reward mechanism. They spend on reward only a few times and in small amounts relative to others, despite frequently benefiting from sizable contributions by peers. Their overall pattern suggests that they do not see costly rewarding as a primary tool for shaping group behavior; instead, they mostly conserve resources and rely on others’ reward behavior or on contribution adjustments themselves. When they do spend on rewards, it appears tied to episodes where they personally contribute a lot and the group environment is at least moderately cooperative. This points to a possible strategic reinforcement motive: they may reward to encourage or stabilize high-contribution states that they themselves are invested in, rather than out of pure gratitude or fairness. They do not systematically reward the highest contributors or the most “altruistic” players; the mapping from others’ contributions to their rewards is sparse and non-mechanical, which suggests they either (a) use a simple, coarse heuristic (“occasionally encourage good behavior”) or (b) are uncertain about the benefits of rewarding and are experimenting at low scale. They never adopt a “heavy rewarder” or “social engineer” role in the group. The cost-to-impact ratio of reward is unfavorable, and their behavior is consistent with recognizing this: they mostly act as a beneficiary of others’ reward efforts rather than a driver of them.

They never use the reward option, even in contexts where others contribute more than them or match their high contributions. This is notable given that some peers consistently contribute strongly, yet the player still refrains from rewarding. This suggests they are unwilling to sacrifice own payoff to positively reinforce cooperation, at least under the given (inefficient) reward technology. Their non-use is consistent with a view that rewarding is not cost-effective or not necessary, or that it is simply not salient to them. They rely instead on their own contribution choices rather than side-payments to shape group behavior. Whether they would reward under more favorable reward parameters is unknown.

They also never use the reward mechanism, despite consistently observing high contributions by others and despite receiving occasional rewards themselves. This indicates that they do not see costly positive reinforcement as worthwhile, even in a highly cooperative environment. Their lack of rewarding persists regardless of whether they are being rewarded or punished, suggesting that gratitude signaling or reciprocal rewarding is not a major driver of their behavior. They appear to “free-ride” on others’ use of rewards and punishments in the sense that they enjoy the cooperative benefits and occasional incoming rewards without investing in the incentive system. The overall pattern points to a “pure contributor” type: they cooperate directly through their own contributions but abstain from meta-strategic actions that shape others’ payoffs, whether positive or negative. Their cooperative identity is thus expressed through direct giving rather than through managing others’ behavior.

The player never spends to reward others, even though they frequently observe near-universal full cooperation and even receive positive transfers themselves in some rounds. Their own contributions are high, but they do not reinforce others’ cooperation through active rewarding. Possible explanations, consistent with the behavior, include: - A belief that rewarding is unnecessary because cooperation is already very high for most of the game. - A judgment that reward technology is not cost-effective (each unit given costs as much as it benefits others, so it does not change aggregate payoffs). - A preference to contribute via the public good itself rather than via targeted side-payments. - A generally frugal or payoff-conscious attitude toward additional costly prosocial acts beyond the main contribution decision. They also do not reciprocate the rewards they receive with counter-rewards, suggesting they do not treat the reward mechanism as a channel for expressing gratitude or building bilateral relationships. Instead, they appear to rely solely on their contribution choice as their cooperative signal. Overall, they are a “pure contributor” who cooperates through the main public-goods channel but abstains from both positive and negative costly peer-to-peer interventions.

They also never use reward, despite the presence of many high contributors and episodes of universal cooperation. Their own contributions are fully cooperative, and they do occasionally receive rewards from others, yet they do not reciprocate with material rewards. This pattern suggests that while they appreciate cooperation enough to act cooperatively themselves, they do not see additional costly reward as necessary or worth the expense. Since rewarding here is payoff-neutral (cost equals benefit), their inaction does not clearly reflect selfishness; it may reflect a minimalist or “no extra interventions” approach to the sanctioning stage, a desire to conserve attention/effort, or a belief that contribution choices themselves are sufficient expression of prosociality. They do not use rewards strategically to cultivate allies, reinforce particular partners, or signal gratitude. Overall, they fit a “quiet altruist” style: they contribute maximally but abstain from manipulating others’ behavior with either positive or negative incentives.

The player almost never uses positive rewards. They show no clear pattern of systematically reinforcing cooperators, even when others match their full contributions consistently. There are isolated instances where they receive small rewards from others, but they do not reciprocate with rewards of their own. Given that rewarding is exactly cost‑neutral in the mechanism (the cost to the giver equals the benefit to the receiver), their disuse of rewards is informative: they either see rewards as unnecessary, as a waste of control “budget,” or as less effective than punishment in maintaining norms. Their behavior suggests a preference for deterrence and discipline rather than positive reinforcement. They do not appear to use rewards strategically to cultivate allies, to thank previous punishers or cooperators, or to offset harsh punishments. Gratitude or coalition‑building, if present as motives, is not expressed through the reward mechanism. Overall, they are a one‑sided norm enforcer who relies on sanctions, not on encouragement, to shape group behavior.

The player is also a frequent and generous rewarder, but their reward behavior is more flexible and exploratory than their rigid contribution rule. They reward high contributors and evidently cooperative peers; they never reward visible defectors. Rewarding appears to serve several functions: - Positive reinforcement: They direct rewards primarily toward those who uphold the full-contribution standard, especially after episodes of defection by others. This looks like an attempt to stabilize and encourage continued cooperation. - Gratitude or reciprocity: They sometimes reward players who likely rewarded them earlier (inferred from occasional rewards received when everyone cooperes), but because identities of rewarders are hidden, this inference has low confidence. Still, their reward pattern suggests they recognize and value prosocial others and may be reciprocating good treatment at the group level rather than partner-specific tit-for-tat. - Strategic shaping: At times they reward a broad set of cooperators, and at other times a narrower subset. This variation indicates some experimentation with how much and to whom to reward, perhaps as they learn which players are reliably prosocial. They do not reward indiscriminately; there are stretches when everyone cooperates and they choose not to reward at all, perhaps conserving resources or concluding that cooperation is already sufficiently stable. In other periods, with similar contribution profiles, they resume rewarding. This suggests they balance a prosocial desire to encourage others with some attention to the cost of rewards and marginal benefit. Overall, they are a “strong conditional altruist with norm-enforcing sanctions”: they unconditionally contribute fully themselves, conditionally punish free-riders, and conditionally reward cooperators, using both negative and positive incentives to maintain a high-cooperation equilibrium while personally never deviating from maximal contribution.

The player is highly and consistently willing to reward, using rewards as their primary tool for shaping others’ behavior. They reward in most phases of the game and often at substantial cumulative cost. They direct rewards to many different peers rather than forming a narrow alliance with just one partner, indicating an attempt to sustain or encourage group-wide cooperation rather than simple reciprocity to a single benefactor. Within that broad pattern, they do show some selectivity: certain names receive repeated small or medium-sized rewards, while others are rewarded only occasionally or not at all, implying that they build and update a mental map of who is “deserving.” Their criteria appear to be: reliable contribution to the public good, and perhaps responsiveness to norms over time, rather than one-shot behavior. They typically decouple rewarding from their own contribution decision; they sometimes reward despite having contributed nothing themselves, and sometimes reward while contributing fully. This points to a strategic or relational view of rewards: they use them to maintain goodwill, reinforce cooperative identities, and possibly offset any negative impressions from their intermittent free-riding or later punishments. In several phases, they allocate sizable total reward outlays across multiple peers in the same decision, suggesting a willingness to sacrifice personal payoff for a more cooperative and appreciative group climate. Net, they are a “prosocial rewarder” who sees positive incentives as central, but who is willing to complement them with punishment when rewards alone seem insufficient.

This player never uses the reward mechanism, despite being surrounded most of the time by fully cooperative peers and despite occasionally receiving small rewards themselves. Their lack of rewarding is stable over the entire game: they do not begin rewarding early to establish norms, nor shift to rewarding later when punishment is established. They also do not appear to “reciprocate” being rewarded. This suggests either that they view full contribution as baseline expected behavior that does not merit extra compensation, or that they regard rewarding as an inefficient or unnecessary use of resources compared to simple contribution and punishment of defectors. There is no evidence that they experiment with reward as a strategic reinforcement tool. Their cooperative norm seems to be enforced asymmetrically: deviations downward are sanctioned, but compliance with the norm is not positively reinforced. This pattern is consistent with a strict, duty-focused cooperator who expects cooperation as default and reserves costly actions for punishing norm violations rather than expressing gratitude.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even though they are repeatedly surrounded by high contributors and themselves receive modest rewards at various times. They do not reciprocate these rewards, nor do they selectively reward high contributors or those who help maintain full cooperation. This abstention from rewarding persists even when cooperation is strong and when they personally benefit from others’ generosity, suggesting that they do not view costly positive reinforcement as necessary or worthwhile. The equal cost and benefit of rewards make them budget-neutral for the recipient and strictly costly for the giver; the player’s pattern is consistent with treating rewards as dominated by simply keeping their own payoff. Overall, the player’s sanction/reward profile is “non-interventionist”: they rely on their own consistent contribution behavior rather than on managing others through incentives, and they do not expend resources to shape others’ behavior either positively or negatively. Their cooperative stance is expressed through contribution, not through second-order sanctioning.

This player is willing to use the reward mechanism, but only selectively and at modest intensity. They do not reward every round, and when they do, they allocate only a small share of their possible budget, suggesting they treat rewarding as a costly, targeted signal rather than a standing policy. Their willingness to pay for others’ benefit in this inefficient reward environment indicates some level of altruism or norm enforcement that goes beyond simple payoff maximization. The triggers for rewarding appear to be others’ relatively high or norm‑consistent contributions. They do not appear to reward low contributors, nor do they reward when overall group behavior looks weak or uncooperative. This indicates that they use rewards primarily as positive reinforcement for cooperation, not as random generosity. Since rewarders are anonymous, rewarding cannot build direct reputation with targeted individuals, so their behavior is best interpreted as an attempt to shape group norms or to “do the right thing” toward cooperators. They stop rewarding when the cooperative environment deteriorates or becomes more ambiguous, consistent with a contingent rule: “I will spend to encourage clear cooperators, but I will not keep paying for a group that isn’t obviously reciprocating.” Their modest but real engagement with the mechanism marks them as a low‑intensity altruistic enforcer rather than a pure egoist or a strong altruist. In end‑game conditions, they are still occasionally willing to reward, which suggests that intrinsic satisfaction with supporting cooperators or enforcing fairness matters to them, even when no long‑term strategic return is available.

The player never spends resources to reward others, despite multiple opportunities and the presence of clearly higher contributors in the group. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for the sake of reinforcing others’ cooperative behavior. Their attitude toward the reward mechanism is effectively non-usage: they treat rewards as a dominated or unappealing option, at least under the given cost-benefit ratio. They do receive rewards from others, yet do not reciprocate with any rewarding behavior of their own. This suggests that gratitude signaling through costly reward is absent or at least not strong enough to override their payoff concerns. Their stance toward social reinforcement appears to be: they are willing to accept benefits generated or reinforced by others’ altruism but are not willing to invest in maintaining or encouraging that altruism themselves. Strategically, they appear to view rewards as non-essential to their own objectives. They do not attempt to use rewards instrumentally to steer partners’ behavior, even when it might help sustain contributions from high cooperators. This fits a profile of a player focused narrowly on immediate monetary outcomes rather than on indirect, longer-term gains through institution-building or social norm engineering.

This player uses rewards sparingly and in a targeted, contingent manner. They reward others when those others contribute generously relative to the group, and they sometimes shift from punishing a player to rewarding that same player once contributions increase meaningfully. This suggests they use reward as a signal of approval and as positive reinforcement for cooperative behavior, not as a tool for side‑payments or alliances. They preferentially reward peers who consistently contribute at high levels, especially when those peers stand out relative to other group members. They also reward a previously low‑contributing target once that individual’s contributions increase, indicating that they track behavioral change and are willing to acknowledge improvement. This pattern is consistent with an underlying belief that both punishment and reward can shape others’ strategies and that people can “redeem” themselves through cooperation. Because rewards are used much less intensively than punishment, the player appears to see negative sanctions as the main instrument to enforce norms, with rewards functioning as supplementary encouragement or recognition. The cost‑effectiveness difference between punishment and reward in this environment may contribute to that asymmetry, but the transcript does not reveal their explicit reasoning, so this is uncertain. Overall, their reward behavior reflects a conditional, reciprocity‑oriented stance: they express gratitude and approval when others behave cooperatively or improve their behavior, but they reserve most of their resources for deterring free‑riding through punishment.

They also never use the reward mechanism, even when others contribute very high amounts and even when those contributions directly increase their own payoffs. This consistent abstention suggests very low willingness to engage in costly positive reciprocity within this setup. Given that rewards here are cost-neutral in expectation (cost and benefit are equal), refusal to reward cannot be explained by pure material self-interest alone; they forgo an opportunity to express approval or attempt to reinforce cooperative partners at zero net monetary cost. This implies either that they see no instrumental value in rewarding (e.g., they do not expect it to change future behavior), or that they simply do not prioritize expressing gratitude or building goodwill via the mechanism. Their pattern does not change in response to being rewarded themselves. At times they receive some reward despite non-maximal contributions, yet they do not subsequently begin rewarding others. Thus, they do not appear to engage in “pay-it-forward” behavior within this environment, nor do they use rewards strategically as a signal of alliance or gratitude. Combining their non-use of punishment and reward, they behave as a “non-enforcer / non-signal” type with respect to the social mechanisms. They manage their strategy almost entirely via their own contribution choices, not via second-order tools. They neither sanction norm violators nor reinforce norm followers, and they do not exploit the mechanisms for reputation-building, coordination, or influence. Overall, they are a socially passive player in the meta-game of enforcement and reciprocity: they adapt only weakly to the presence of punishment and reward options and leave that relational infrastructure unused, focusing instead on opportunistic, somewhat unstable contribution decisions.

This player uses reward intermittently, in a selective and somewhat costly way, but does not rely on it systematically. They clearly are willing to sacrifice personal payoff to reinforce prosocial behavior in others and occasionally to reciprocate reward they themselves receive. Their reward allocations appear to favor higher contributors or those who maintain relatively strong contributions while others slack; they do not use reward to subsidize obvious free-riding. When they do invest in reward, they sometimes commit nontrivial amounts, suggesting a readiness to incur real costs to support group cooperation or to acknowledge particularly cooperative partners. However, they do not reward every time others cooperate, and many fully cooperative situations pass without any reward from them, indicating that they treat reward as an optional, situational tool rather than a standing obligation. They also do not appear to reward in a precise, tit-for-tat manner; the timing is noisy, implying that their reward behavior is guided by a broad impression of who “deserves” reinforcement rather than a finely calibrated strategy. They never employ “negative” reward (e.g., ignoring cooperators to favor defectors), and show no sign of strategic withholding of reward to pressure others; their use of reward is prosocial but not intensely strategic.

They never spend anything on rewards. This holds even when others contribute fully and when “reward the high contributors” norms are being discussed or enacted by other players. They do accept rewards from others when they are given but never reciprocate. This strongly indicates that they view the reward mechanism as a pure cost with insufficient strategic return, and they are not motivated to spend on rewarding out of gratitude or fairness. They appear unmoved by others’ generous reward expenditures, including extreme reward-givers who burn large portions of their own payoffs. The player does not “join in” any reward cascades, nor do they use rewards to enforce contribution agreements or condition future behavior. Their strategy around rewards is purely passive: take whatever comes, pay nothing.

They never spend any coins on reward, despite repeated opportunities and clear evidence that others are using rewards, sometimes quite lavishly. They are purely a recipient, never a giver. This consistent non-rewarding pattern indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost for the sake of reinforcing cooperation or expressing gratitude. They do not use reward as a strategic lever, even when it might help establish or stabilize norms that could benefit them in future rounds. This suggests either that they discount the future heavily, that they doubt others will respond reliably to reward, or that they simply prioritize immediate personal payoff over any form of costly social influence. They also ignore explicit proposals by others to use rewards conditionally (such as “give reward only to those who contribute high amounts”). Their behavior shows no sign of conditional rewarding or norm-enforcement via the reward mechanism. Taken together, their reward behavior is consistent with a strictly self-regarding type who does not invest in cooperative enforcement mechanisms, even when the environment is designed to allow such mechanisms.

They never spend any of their own coins to reward others. This is robust across the entire game, including periods when many others are spending heavily on rewards, sometimes to the point of harming their own payoffs. They nonetheless accept rewards freely when others choose to give them, even in rounds where they have contributed relatively little compared to the group. This shows no reciprocal reward norm: they do not “repay” rewarders or distribute rewards to high contributors. Reward from their side is effectively absent. Given that reward has a personal cost and no direct strategic targeting (rewarders are anonymous), it appears they evaluate it strictly in cost–benefit terms and decide it is never worth paying 3 to give 2 to someone else. They do not use reward as a tool to sustain cooperation, to express gratitude, or to encourage particular partners. Their stance toward the reward mechanism can thus be summarized as: accept windfalls, never initiate. This further reinforces their profile as a strictly self‑regarding player in this context.

They almost never use the reward mechanism and, when they do, they spend only minimal amounts. Across the game they receive a substantial volume of rewards from others in some cooperative rounds, but they do not reciprocate in any systematic way. There is no pattern of them rewarding those who matched their contribution level, nor do they appear to target under-contributors with zero reward as a formal signal; their default is simply not to reward at all. The few instances of reward spending occur in rounds where they themselves are contributing at or near the upper end, and overall group contributions are mixed. This could indicate a fleeting attempt to encourage specific behavior, but the scale of their spending is too small and too inconsistent to infer a deliberate reinforcement strategy. No persistent “rewarding the high cooperators” pattern emerges from their data. They also do not respond behaviorally to receiving rewards by, for example, increasing contributions immediately after a reward spike; their contribution changes are more tightly tied to chat-anchored coordination than to the reward structure. This suggests that, cognitively, they treat the public good stage as the main arena of cooperation and view reward as either too costly or too chaotic (given other players’ extreme usage) to be an effective tool. In terms of type, this player is essentially a non-rewarder: they prefer to exert influence through communication and social pressure rather than through payoff-based incentives. They do not show gratitude signaling via reciprocal rewards; any sense of appreciation for rewarders is, if present, not expressed through the mechanism.

They never spend any coins on reward despite frequent opportunities and despite being on the receiving end of substantial positive rewards from others. This strongly suggests a reward-averse or at least strongly self-payoff-protective stance toward the reward mechanism. They appear to reason (implicitly or explicitly) that paying a relatively high cost to grant a relatively lower benefit is not worthwhile for them, even when it might sustain cooperation. Their willingness to “reward” via contribution (by matching or exceeding group norms) stands in contrast to their total abstention from the explicit reward mechanism. They seem to separate contributing to the public good, which directly yields returns to themselves and others, from paying extra to give side-payments to specific individuals, which they never do. They do not use rewards strategically to reinforce high contributors or to punish low ones indirectly by exclusion; they appear to rely on group-level contribution coordination and social talk for norm enforcement instead. There is no visible pattern of “reciprocal rewarding” or gratitude signaling through the mechanism: even after receiving repeated rewards, they do not begin to reciprocate via rewards. Any “thank you” response is purely behavioral in the sense of continuing to contribute at high levels in coordinated phases. Their non-use of rewards persists across early, middle, and late game, suggesting it is a stable preference rather than a transient oversight.

The player is an extreme, indiscriminate, and self-sacrificing rewarder with very low strategic discipline. They repeatedly spend large amounts on rewards, sometimes far beyond what is reasonable given their own payoff, and often drive their own round earnings deeply negative. The pattern suggests they frequently reward many others at once, including moderate or typical contributors, not just standout cooperators. There is no consistent targeting logic that focuses on the highest contributors or on those who previously rewarded them; instead, their reward behavior resembles impulsive generosity or random largesse. They show no consistent attempt to condition rewards on adherence to group agreements (“everyone put 10” or “everyone put 20”) and do not seem to use rewards to solve free-riding or to steer the group. They almost never comment in chat about why they rewarded or about who deserves reward, so their internal criteria remain unknown. From observed payoffs, it is clear they do not protect their own earnings when rewarding; they are willing to incur large personal losses to transfer benefits to others, even when the technology of reward is inefficient. This indicates very low payoff sensitivity and no clear strategic reinforcement logic.

They never use the reward option, despite clearly understanding that the mechanism exists. This holds even when others cooperate fully and when some players engage in visible norm-supporting behavior. This indicates a strong asymmetry: they express support for cooperation in chat and are willing to punish at a cost, but they do not reinforce good behavior via positive incentives. Possible reasons (not directly observable): they may perceive reward as inefficient relative to punishment or may view full contribution as baseline/expected rather than reward-worthy. However, these are speculative; all that is firmly supported is that they systematically refrain from paying to reward, even when ample opportunities are present.

They never use the reward option, even when others visibly cooperate, bear punishment costs, or align with their norm. Despite seeing that rewarding is possible and publicly impactful, they allocate all sanctioning budget exclusively to punishment. This suggests either that they see reward as inefficient (poor leverage compared to punishment), unnecessary (cooperation is an expected baseline, not something to be praised), or simply inferior as a tool for norm enforcement. There is no observed inclination toward gratitude signaling or positive reinforcement; their social control strategy is one-sided and focused on negative sanctions.

The player never uses rewards, even though rewarding high contributors is clearly possible and some peers make use of positive incentives. Given their own full-cooperation pattern, they would be a natural candidate to reward other full contributors, but they do not do so. This points toward a payoff-preserving or minimalist stance regarding secondary mechanisms: they contribute fully to the public fund but do not further spend to shape others’ behavior. It is unclear whether they fail to notice the strategic potential of rewards, deem them inefficient, or simply prefer a “contribute but don’t intervene” approach. Their lack of rewarding persists even after they see others engaging in both rewarding and punishing, suggesting this is a stable trait rather than a lack of information.

They show a clear willingness to use the reward mechanism, but in a selective and delayed way. Early on, when nearly everyone is contributing fully, they do not reward anyone, even though this would reinforce the cooperative norm. Only later, after they themselves have defected and been sanctioned, do they invest substantially in rewarding a subset of consistent contributors. The targets they choose are players who both contribute and publicly advocate for universal cooperation or simplification, plus one steadfast cooperator who stays aligned with the full‑contribution norm; they seem to be rewarding visible norm supporters and steady cooperators rather than random peers. This pattern indicates strategic prosociality: they use rewards to acknowledge and support high contributors, and possibly to repair their own image or to express gratitude for others’ continued cooperation despite their free‑riding. They accept a net cost to do this, so they are not purely payoff‑maximizing. Their reward usage suggests that positive reinforcement and reputational or moral concerns matter to them more than negative enforcement.

They never use the reward option, despite having opportunities where many others contribute fully. This consistent non-use suggests that they do not view positive incentives as necessary or efficient, especially given the relatively unfavorable reward technology. Their enforcement style is asymmetric: they are willing to incur costs to punish defectors but do not incur costs to bonus cooperators. They may assume that cooperative behavior is the default and does not merit extra compensation, whereas defection requires correction. There is no observable signaling of gratitude through rewards, implying that if they feel gratitude, they do not express it via costly transfers in this environment. Overall, their behavioral type is “punish-based norm enforcer” rather than “carrot-and-stick” strategist.

The player never actually spends on rewards, yet they are conceptually and strategically interested in the reward mechanism. They explicitly note the favorable cost–benefit ratio of rewards and propose structured, reciprocal rewarding with others, framed as “trading” maximum donations. This shows that they recognize rewards as an efficiency-enhancing tool and are willing, in principle, to coordinate around them. The absence of reward usage in the transcript could be due to timing, lack of response, or risk concerns about unilateral rewarding; exact reason is unknown. Their orientation suggests they are a conditional rewarder in theory—interested in mutual, planned reinforcement of cooperation rather than spontaneous or unilateral gifting. They view rewards as a way to simplify and accelerate mutually beneficial outcomes rather than as a vehicle for expressing gratitude or moral approval per se.

They never use the reward option either, even though the group norm favors full contribution and they could have reinforced that norm positively. This may reflect a general reluctance to spend on any form of sanctioning—positive or negative—or a prioritization of keeping all available coins rather than investing in social incentives. It could also indicate that, although they personally cooperate, they do not take on the role of active norm enforcer or leader through rewards. They benefit from rewards given by others but do not reciprocate via the mechanism, suggesting that gratitude signaling or strategic reward-based coordination is not a priority for them in this setting.

They never reward anyone, even when other players are actively using rewards to encourage contributors or compensate victims. This indicates a strong asymmetry between their willingness to punish and their willingness to reward. They do not spend to reinforce cooperation or to thank high contributors. This might reflect a belief that cooperation is already sufficiently incentivized, that rewards are inefficient, or simply a preference for conserving resources for their own payoff unless responding to direct mistreatment. Their absence from positive reciprocity suggests they rely more on negative sanctions than on positive reinforcement.

They are mostly non-rewarding, with a single episode of substantial reward spending. Across the interaction, they usually keep all their payoff from the redistribution stage and decline to reinforce others through the reward mechanism, even when several group members contribute highly. On one occasion, they spend a large share of their payoff on rewards, targeting multiple others who are contributing at or near their own high level. This indicates that when they do choose to reward, it is to recognize and encourage high cooperation, not to support low contributors. They accept a negative net tradeoff in that episode, which shows some willingness to sacrifice earnings to promote or acknowledge cooperation, but only sporadically. The rarity of their rewarding suggests that they are either payoff-conscious about the cost of rewarding or that they view reward as a special, not routine, tool. There is no clear pattern of using rewards strategically to shape future contributions; their reward burst coincides with already-high contributions by others rather than trying to “pull up” laggards. Overall, they are a reluctant but principled rewarder: generally abstaining, but capable of substantial generosity when moved to reinforce cooperative norms.

The player never spends any of their own coins on rewards, even though they repeatedly observe that rewards are used by others. This holds both in situations where some players contribute very little and where nearly everyone contributes a lot. They thus behave as a pure non-rewarder: they rely entirely on contribution decisions to express cooperation and do not engage with the reward mechanism at all. This pattern suggests either that they view the reward technology as too costly relative to its effect, that they are reluctant to take on a coordinating/leadership role in sanctioning or reinforcing others, or that they place strong weight on keeping their own earnings intact once contributions are chosen. There is no evidence that they use rewards strategically to build alliances, nor that generosity through reward is part of their cooperative style. Their willingness to contribute substantially without ever using rewards indicates that they separate “helping via the public good” from “helping via side-payments,” preferring the former exclusively.

This player never spends on reward, despite repeated opportunities and clear examples of others using the reward tool. Even in settings where others’ high contributions and/or visible generosity could be reinforced, they consistently choose not to incur any reward cost. This strongly suggests that they views the reward mechanism as strategically or economically unattractive at the given cost/benefit ratio. They prefers to keep all their private payoff rather than invest in shaping others’ behavior through positive incentives. There is no evidence of “moral” or expressive use of reward (e.g., rewarding cooperative norms simply because it feels right). Instead, they behaves as a pure payoff-maximizer with respect to the reward stage, free-riding on the fact that other players sometimes use reward to sustain cooperation. Their strategy is therefore to benefit from any trust or reciprocity that rewards generate for the group, without personally contributing to that enforcement mechanism.

The player is willing to use the reward mechanism actively, despite its personal cost and relatively poor efficiency. They pay nontrivial amounts to reward others on multiple occasions, including at least one episode of intense reward giving. Their reward behavior targets contributors rather than defectors, indicating that they use reward as positive reinforcement for cooperative or improved behavior. They do not appear to reward strictly in proportion to contribution level; instead, they occasionally direct especially strong rewards when the group is highly cooperative, consistent with a desire to strengthen a high-cooperation phase or express approval of group alignment. They also reward even when the group as a whole is already contributing well, which implies that they are not using reward merely as a tool to fix low-cooperation states, but also as a way of stabilizing or endorsing good norms. They sometimes refrain from rewarding even when others have contributed at high levels, which suggests that reward is not automatic. Instead, it seems contingent on a combination of high cooperation and the player’s evolving assessment of costs, prior interactions, and stage of the game.
